The VAP bundle consists of daily chest X-rays and sputum cultures
rationale: The VAP prevention bundle includes evidence-based interventions: 1) Head of bed elevation ≥30° (reduces aspiration of gastric contents — strong evidence). 2) Daily sedation vacation and spontaneous breathing trials (reduces ventilator days). 3) Oral care with chlorhexidine (reduces oropharyngeal bacterial colonization). 4) DVT prophylaxis. 5) Stress ulcer prophylaxis. Additional measures: subglottic suctioning ETTs (level 1 evidence), minimizing ventilator circuit changes, maintaining ETT cuff pressure 20-30 cmH2O, and hand hygiene. Bundle compliance reduces VAP rates by 40-70%.

stem: A patient on mechanical ventilation develops a sudden increase in both PIP and plateau pressure. What conditions cause simultaneous elevation of both PIP and Pplat?
options: Decreased lung compliance — pneumothorax, atelectasis, pulmonary edema, abdominal distension, mainstem intubation, or ARDS progression
Increased airway resistance only
Circuit leak or disconnection
Improved lung compliance from bronchodilator therapy
rationale: Simultaneous elevation of PIP and Pplat indicates decreased respiratory system compliance (the lung and/or chest wall is stiffer). The differential includes: tension pneumothorax (emergent!), atelectasis (lobar collapse), pulmonary edema, abdominal compartment syndrome or distension (pushing diaphragm up), mainstem bronchial intubation (ventilating only one lung), ARDS progression, and pleural effusion. Contrast this with isolated PIP elevation (normal Pplat), which indicates increased airway resistance. Immediate assessment should rule out pneumothorax and mainstem intubation first.

stem: A morbidly obese patient (BMI 55) requires mechanical ventilation after surgery. What ventilator adjustments are necessary to account for the physiological effects of obesity?
options: Use IBW for VT calculation (not actual weight), apply higher PEEP (10-15 cmH2O) to counteract decreased FRC, and use a reverse Trendelenburg position to offload abdominal weight from the diaphragm
Calculate tidal volume based on actual body weight
Use minimal PEEP since obese patients have high FRC
Standard ventilator settings work for all body habitus
rationale: Obesity significantly affects respiratory mechanics: decreased FRC (from abdominal mass compressing the diaphragm), decreased compliance (chest wall mass), increased oxygen consumption, and increased closing capacity. Ventilator strategies: 1) VT based on IBW — lung size correlates with height, not weight. 2) Higher PEEP (10-15 cmH2O) to maintain FRC above closing capacity and prevent atelectasis. 3) Reverse Trendelenburg or head-up positioning to offload abdominal pressure from the diaphragm. 4) Recruitment maneuvers may be needed. 5) Higher FiO2 may be initially required.

stem: What is the difference between flow triggering and pressure triggering in mechanical ventilation, and which is generally preferred?
options: Pressure triggering requires the patient to generate a negative pressure to initiate a breath; flow triggering detects a change in bias flow; flow triggering is preferred as it requires less patient effort
Both methods are identical in function
Pressure triggering requires less work than flow triggering
Only flow triggering can be used in modern ventilators
rationale: Pressure triggering: a continuous low flow maintains circuit pressure; the patient must generate a negative pressure deflection (typically -1 to -2 cmH2O below PEEP) to trigger inspiration. This requires the patient to overcome circuit impedance. Flow triggering: a continuous bias flow (typically 5-20 L/min) circulates through the circuit; the ventilator detects when the patient inhales from this flow (typically a 2-3 L/min change triggers a breath). Flow triggering reduces trigger work by 30-60% and improves patient-ventilator synchrony, making it the preferred method in most situations.

stem: Mechanical power is an emerging concept in ventilator-induced lung injury. What does mechanical power represent, and what threshold is associated with increased risk of VILI?
options: Mechanical power is the total energy delivered to the respiratory system per minute (incorporating VT, driving pressure, PEEP, flow, and rate); values >17 J/min are associated with increased VILI risk
Mechanical power is the same as peak inspiratory pressure
Mechanical power cannot be calculated from ventilator parameters
Mechanical power has no relationship to lung injury
rationale: Mechanical power (MP) quantifies the total energy per minute transferred from the ventilator to the respiratory system. It integrates multiple variables into a single parameter: MP = 0.098 × RR × VT × (Ppeak - ½ × driving pressure). Components include: elastic power (from driving pressure), resistive power (from airway resistance), and PEEP-related power. Gattinoni proposed that MP >17 J/min exceeds the lung's ability to dissipate energy safely, leading to VILI. Normalizing MP to functional lung size or compliance may improve its predictive value.

stem: A patient with traumatic brain injury requires mechanical ventilation. The neurosurgeon requests PaCO2 be maintained at 35-40 mmHg. Why is CO2 management critical in TBI, and what are the dangers of excessive hyperventilation?
options: CO2 affects cerebral blood flow — hypocapnia causes cerebral vasoconstriction reducing ICP but excessive hyperventilation can cause cerebral ischemia
CO2 has no effect on cerebral blood flow
Hyperventilation should always target PaCO2 <20 mmHg in TBI
Hypercapnia is beneficial in all TBI patients
rationale: In TBI, PaCO2 directly affects cerebral blood flow (CBF): each 1 mmHg change in PaCO2 changes CBF by approximately 3%. Mild hyperventilation (PaCO2 35 mmHg) can transiently reduce ICP by causing cerebral vasoconstriction. However, aggressive hyperventilation (PaCO2 <25 mmHg) can cause dangerous cerebral ischemia by excessively reducing CBF to already injured brain tissue. Current guidelines recommend maintaining PaCO2 35-40 mmHg, using brief hyperventilation (30-35 mmHg) only as a temporizing measure for acute ICP crises. Routine prophylactic hyperventilation is no longer recommended.

stem: Noninvasive ventilation (NIV) via BiPAP is initiated for a patient with acute hypercapnic respiratory failure from COPD exacerbation. Initial settings are IPAP 12 cmH2O and EPAP 5 cmH2O. After 30 minutes, ABG shows no improvement in PaCO2. What is the appropriate adjustment?
options: Increase IPAP to 16-18 cmH2O to increase tidal volume and minute ventilation for better CO2 removal
Increase EPAP to 15 cmH2O to improve CO2 removal
Decrease IPAP to reduce patient discomfort
Switch immediately to invasive mechanical ventilation
rationale: In BiPAP, the pressure support (PS = IPAP - EPAP) determines the tidal volume delivered. If CO2 removal is inadequate, increasing IPAP increases the pressure support level, generating larger tidal volumes and increasing minute ventilation. IPAP should be titrated upward in increments of 2 cmH2O (up to 20-25 cmH2O) to achieve clinical improvement and PaCO2 reduction. Increasing EPAP alone does not improve ventilation — it increases mean airway pressure and helps oxygenation. If NIV fails after adequate adjustment (IPAP 20-25, proper mask fit), then consider intubation.

stem: An ARDS patient is being ventilated with VT 350 mL, RR 28, FiO2 0.60, PEEP 14 cmH2O. The Pplat is 30 cmH2O. The ABG shows pH 7.25 with PaCO2 58 mmHg. The team wants to increase minute ventilation. What is the safest approach?
options: Increase the respiratory rate (not exceeding 35/min and monitoring for auto-PEEP) since increasing tidal volume would exceed safe plateau pressure limits
Increase tidal volume to 600 mL to normalize CO2
Add dead space to the circuit to increase CO2 elimination
Decrease PEEP to 0 to allow more room for tidal volume
rationale: With Pplat already at 30 cmH2O (the upper safe limit), increasing tidal volume would cause dangerous overdistension. The safest way to increase minute ventilation is to increase the respiratory rate, up to 35 breaths/min, while monitoring for auto-PEEP (incomplete exhalation). At RR 28, there is still room to increase. If rate increases cause auto-PEEP, consider accepting permissive hypercapnia (pH >7.20 is generally tolerated). Decreasing PEEP would worsen oxygenation and potentially cause derecruitment. Adding dead space would worsen CO2 retention, not improve it.

stem: A patient on mechanical ventilation has a pressure-time scalar showing a convex (bowing upward) inspiratory pressure curve during volume-controlled ventilation. What does this waveform pattern indicate?
options: Overdistension of the lung — compliance is decreasing as the breath is delivered, suggesting the tidal volume or PEEP is too high
Underdistension requiring higher tidal volumes
Normal pressure-time relationship during volume delivery
The ventilator flow sensor is malfunctioning
rationale: A convex (bowing upward, also called 'beaking') pressure-time waveform during volume-controlled inspiration indicates that compliance is decreasing progressively during the breath — the lung is becoming stiffer as more volume is delivered. This is a sign of tidal overdistension, where alveoli are being stretched beyond their optimal compliance range. The opposite pattern (concave or scooped) indicates flow starvation. A linear pressure rise indicates relatively constant compliance. Recognizing the beaking pattern is important for preventing VILI — consider reducing tidal volume, reducing PEEP, or both.

stem: Volume-guaranteed pressure modes (such as PRVC — Pressure Regulated Volume Control) combine features of volume and pressure ventilation. How does PRVC work?
options: PRVC delivers breaths in a pressure-controlled fashion but automatically adjusts the inspiratory pressure level breath-by-breath to achieve a target tidal volume
PRVC delivers a fixed volume at a fixed pressure regardless of lung mechanics
PRVC is identical to standard volume control
PRVC cannot adjust to changing lung compliance
rationale: PRVC (Pressure Regulated Volume Control) is a dual-control mode that uses a pressure-targeted breath delivery (decelerating flow, pressure-limited) while targeting a set tidal volume. The ventilator measures the delivered VT and adjusts the pressure level for the next breath: if VT is too low, pressure increases; if VT is too high, pressure decreases. This provides the benefits of pressure ventilation (decelerating flow, lower peak pressures) with volume guarantee. The pressure adjusts gradually (typically ≤3 cmH2O per breath) to avoid large swings. Clinicians must still monitor that the auto-adjusted pressures remain within safe limits.

stem: The cuff pressure of an endotracheal tube should be maintained within a specific range. What is the recommended range and what are the consequences of pressures outside this range?
options: 20-30 cmH2O — pressure below 20 increases aspiration risk; pressure above 30 can cause tracheal ischemia, necrosis, and stenosis
5-10 cmH2O for comfort
40-50 cmH2O to ensure a complete seal
Cuff pressure monitoring is unnecessary with modern ETT cuffs
rationale: ETT cuff pressure should be maintained at 20-30 cmH2O (approximately 15-22 mmHg). Pressures below 20 cmH2O allow secretions to leak around the cuff into the lower airways, increasing aspiration and VAP risk. Pressures above 30 cmH2O exceed tracheal mucosal capillary perfusion pressure (approximately 25-35 mmHg), causing ischemia, ulceration, and potentially tracheal necrosis, tracheomalacia, tracheo-esophageal fistula, or tracheal stenosis. Continuous cuff pressure monitoring or minimum of every-8-hour checks is recommended. Minimal occlusive volume or minimal leak techniques can be used.

stem: A ventilated patient on A/C volume control with VT 500 mL and RR 16 has a sudden decrease in exhaled CO2 on capnography (ETCO2 drops from 38 to 12 mmHg) with stable ventilator pressures. What is the most likely cause?
options: Sudden decrease in pulmonary blood flow — cardiac arrest, massive PE, or severe hypotension
ETT obstruction by mucus plug
Ventilator malfunction delivering insufficient tidal volume
Bronchospasm causing air trapping
rationale: A sudden drop in ETCO2 with maintained ventilation (stable pressures and volumes) indicates a perfusion problem — less CO2 is being delivered to the lungs for elimination. The most critical cause is cardiac arrest (immediate assessment needed). Other causes: massive pulmonary embolism (obstruction of pulmonary blood flow), severe hypotension from any cause, or air embolism. If ETCO2 drops to near zero during CPR, it indicates poor quality compressions or return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) if it suddenly increases. Capnography is an essential monitoring tool for detecting perfusion changes.

stem: A patient with Guillain-Barré syndrome is on mechanical ventilation. The team is performing daily assessments of respiratory muscle recovery. Which measurements are most useful for predicting readiness for extubation in neuromuscular disease?
options: Maximum inspiratory pressure (MIP ≤ -30 cmH2O), vital capacity (>10-15 mL/kg), and maximal expiratory pressure (MEP >40 cmH2O for adequate cough)
Only ABG values predict extubation readiness
Chest X-ray improvement is the sole predictor
Heart rate variability predicts extubation success
rationale: In neuromuscular disease, respiratory muscle strength determines extubation readiness. Key metrics: MIP (NIF) more negative than -30 cmH2O (adequate inspiratory muscle strength); vital capacity >10-15 mL/kg (adequate tidal breathing reserve — serial VC measurements are particularly valuable as they trend recovery); MEP >40 cmH2O (adequate expiratory muscle strength for effective cough and secretion clearance). The 20/30/40 rule is a useful guide: VC >20 mL/kg, MIP ≤ -30, MEP >40. These should be combined with SBT assessment and evaluation of bulbar function (swallowing, airway protection).

stem: What is patient self-inflicted lung injury (P-SILI), and how does it relate to the timing of intubation in acute respiratory failure?
options: P-SILI occurs when strong spontaneous inspiratory efforts generate excessive transpulmonary pressure swings, causing lung injury similar to VILI; this supports earlier intubation in deteriorating patients
P-SILI only occurs during mechanical ventilation
Spontaneous breathing cannot cause lung injury
P-SILI is prevented by increasing FiO2 alone
rationale: P-SILI describes lung injury caused by vigorous spontaneous inspiratory efforts in patients with acute respiratory failure. Strong negative pleural pressure swings (-15 to -25 cmH2O or more) generate high transpulmonary pressures that can overdistend dependent lung regions, cause pendelluft (air redistribution between lung regions), increase pulmonary edema from negative pressure pulmonary edema, and worsen inflammation. This concept supports the argument for earlier intubation in patients with high respiratory drive and deteriorating gas exchange, rather than prolonged attempts at noninvasive support with visible respiratory distress.

stem: Esophageal pressure monitoring can be used to estimate pleural pressure and guide PEEP titration. How does the transpulmonary pressure calculated from esophageal manometry help optimize ventilator settings?
options: Transpulmonary pressure (Paw - Pes) represents the actual distending pressure of the lung; PEEP can be titrated to maintain positive end-expiratory transpulmonary pressure to prevent collapse
Esophageal pressure has no relationship to pleural pressure
Transpulmonary pressure monitoring is only used during surgery
Esophageal manometry measures cardiac function, not respiratory mechanics
rationale: Esophageal pressure (Pes) approximates pleural pressure. Transpulmonary pressure (PL = Paw - Pes) represents the true distending pressure of the lung. In obese patients or those with high abdominal pressure, standard PEEP based on Paw alone may be misleading — much of the PEEP may be overcoming chest wall elastance rather than distending the lung. The EPVent-2 trial explored Pes-guided PEEP titration to maintain positive end-expiratory PL (preventing atelectasis while avoiding overdistension). This approach allows individualized PEEP optimization based on actual lung mechanics.

stem: A patient being ventilated with high-flow nasal cannula (HFNC) at 60 L/min and FiO2 0.60 has a ROX index of 3.5 at 12 hours. What does the ROX index indicate and what action should be taken?
options: ROX index = (SpO2/FiO2)/RR; a value <3.85 at 12 hours predicts HFNC failure and the need for intubation
ROX index >3.5 always indicates HFNC success
The ROX index is used only for CPAP, not HFNC
ROX index <3.5 indicates the patient should have HFNC flow decreased
rationale: The ROX index = (SpO2/FiO2) / Respiratory Rate, developed by Roca et al. to predict HFNC failure. A ROX index <2.85 at 2 hours, <3.47 at 6 hours, or <3.85 at 12 hours predicts HFNC failure requiring intubation with good sensitivity. This patient's ROX of 3.5 at 12 hours is below the 3.85 threshold, suggesting HFNC failure and the need for escalation to NIV or intubation. The ROX index helps avoid delayed intubation, which is associated with worse outcomes in acute respiratory failure.

stem: A patient on mechanical ventilation develops ventilator-associated event (VAE) based on CDC surveillance criteria. What defines a VAE versus the older VAP definition?
options: VAE is defined by sustained increase in FiO2 (≥0.20) or PEEP (≥3 cmH2O) for ≥2 days after ≥2 days of stable or decreasing settings; it is broader than the clinical VAP definition
VAE and VAP are identical definitions
VAE only includes pneumonia caused by specific organisms
VAE is defined solely by positive sputum cultures
rationale: The CDC's ventilator-associated event (VAE) surveillance framework replaced the subjective VAP definition in 2013. VAE is defined by: ≥2 calendar days of stable or decreasing ventilator settings (FiO2 or PEEP) followed by ≥2 days of increased FiO2 (≥0.20 increase) or PEEP (≥3 cmH2O increase). VAE is a broader definition capturing any condition causing respiratory deterioration during ventilation, not just pneumonia. Subtypes include: infection-related VAC (IVAC) if temperature, WBC, or antibiotics are also abnormal, and possible/probable VAP if respiratory cultures are positive.

stem: A patient on volume-controlled ventilation has reverse triggering — the ventilator breath causes a reflex diaphragmatic contraction during or after the mechanical breath. What is the clinical significance?
options: Reverse triggering can cause breath-stacking, excessive tidal volumes, and eccentric diaphragmatic contractions leading to injury; it may require adjusting sedation or ventilator settings
Reverse triggering is normal and expected during all mechanical ventilation
Reverse triggering only occurs in pediatric patients
Reverse triggering improves oxygenation and should be encouraged
rationale: Reverse triggering is an underrecognized form of patient-ventilator dyssynchrony where the mechanical breath reflexively activates the patient's diaphragm through respiratory center entrainment. The diaphragmatic contraction occurs during or after the mechanical inflation, potentially causing: breath-stacking (double-triggering), excessive tidal volumes (combining mechanical and spontaneous efforts), and eccentric diaphragmatic contractions (the diaphragm contracts while being stretched by the mechanical breath, which can cause injury). Reverse triggering is common in deeply sedated patients and may worsen with neuromuscular blockade cessation. Management includes adjusting sedation depth or ventilator rate.

stem: Closed-loop ventilation modes like ASV (Adaptive Support Ventilation) and INTELLiVENT use algorithms to automatically adjust ventilator settings. What parameters does ASV optimize automatically?
options: ASV automatically adjusts tidal volume, respiratory rate, and inspiratory pressure to minimize work of breathing based on the Otis equation, targeting optimal minute ventilation for the patient's lung mechanics
ASV only adjusts FiO2 based on pulse oximetry
ASV requires manual adjustment of all settings every hour
ASV is identical to standard volume control with no automated features
rationale: ASV is an automated closed-loop mode that: 1) Takes input of patient IBW and %MinVol. 2) Measures respiratory mechanics (compliance, resistance, auto-PEEP). 3) Uses the Otis equation to calculate the optimal VT-RR combination that minimizes work of breathing for the measured mechanics. 4) Delivers pressure-controlled breaths with automatically adjusted pressure, rate, and I-time. 5) Adapts breath-by-breath to changing conditions. ASV can function from full support through weaning. INTELLiVENT adds automated FiO2/PEEP adjustments based on SpO2 and ETCO2. These modes aim to reduce clinician workload while maintaining optimal ventilation.

stem: A patient with severe bronchospasm on mechanical ventilation has an auto-PEEP of 15 cmH2O. The respiratory therapist disconnects the patient from the ventilator briefly. What therapeutic rationale supports this action?
options: Disconnecting allows trapped gas to escape, immediately reducing auto-PEEP and intrathoracic pressure, potentially improving hemodynamics
Disconnection has no benefit and should never be done
Disconnection increases auto-PEEP by allowing atmospheric pressure into the lungs
The purpose is to check the ETT position
rationale: In severe air trapping with hemodynamic compromise (auto-PEEP causing hypotension from decreased venous return), briefly disconnecting the patient from the ventilator allows trapped gas to passively escape through the ETT. This immediately reduces intrathoracic pressure and can dramatically improve blood pressure. This maneuver is both diagnostic (if BP improves with disconnection, auto-PEEP was the cause of hypotension) and therapeutic. After reconnection, adjust ventilator settings to minimize air trapping: lower rate, longer expiratory time, smaller VT, and aggressive bronchodilator therapy.

stem: What is the difference between controlled mechanical ventilation (CMV), assist-control (A/C), and pressure support (PSV) in terms of patient effort and ventilator control?
options: CMV delivers all breaths by the ventilator with no patient triggering; A/C allows patient-triggered breaths but gives full support for each breath; PSV requires patient triggering for every breath with variable support
All three modes function identically
PSV provides the most support; CMV provides the least
Patient effort is not relevant in any ventilator mode
rationale: These modes represent a spectrum of ventilator support vs. patient effort: CMV (true controlled ventilation) — the ventilator delivers all breaths at set rate, VT, and timing; patient effort is not sensed (used only with paralysis or deep sedation). A/C — mandatory breaths delivered at a minimum set rate, but the patient can trigger additional breaths, each receiving the full set VT/pressure; moderate patient interaction. PSV — purely spontaneous; the patient initiates every breath and controls rate, timing, and VT (modulated by set PS level); highest patient interaction. The trend in modern care is toward modes that allow spontaneous breathing when possible.

stem: Stress index monitoring during volume-controlled ventilation provides information about lung recruitability. What does a stress index >1, =1, and <1 indicate?
options: Stress index >1 indicates tidal overdistension (convex pressure curve); =1 indicates optimal ventilation (linear pressure rise); <1 indicates tidal recruitment (concave pressure curve)
Stress index values have no clinical meaning
Stress index >1 means inadequate ventilation
The stress index can only be measured in pressure-controlled modes
rationale: The stress index analyzes the shape of the pressure-time curve during constant-flow volume-controlled ventilation. Stress index = 1: linear pressure rise indicates constant compliance throughout inspiration (optimal). Stress index >1: convex (upward curving) pressure waveform indicates decreasing compliance during breath delivery — overdistension is occurring; consider reducing VT or PEEP. Stress index <1: concave (downward curving) pressure waveform indicates increasing compliance during inspiration — tidal recruitment is occurring; consider increasing PEEP to maintain recruitment. This tool helps personalize PEEP and VT settings.

stem: A 65-year-old patient with COPD has been ventilated for 14 days and has failed three SBTs. The team is considering tracheostomy. What are the evidence-based indications for tracheostomy timing in prolonged mechanical ventilation?
options: Consider tracheostomy if the patient is expected to require >14-21 days of mechanical ventilation; early tracheostomy (within 7-10 days) may reduce sedation needs, improve comfort, and facilitate weaning
Tracheostomy should never be performed before day 30
Tracheostomy is contraindicated in COPD patients
All patients should receive tracheostomy on day 1 of ventilation
rationale: Tracheostomy is considered when prolonged mechanical ventilation is anticipated (>14-21 days). Benefits over translaryngeal ETT include: reduced sedation requirements, improved patient comfort and communication, easier oral care, improved secretion management, reduced airway resistance (shorter tube), and facilitated weaning trials. The TracMan trial showed no mortality difference between early (day 4) and late (day 10+) tracheostomy, but early tracheostomy reduced sedation days. Percutaneous dilatational tracheostomy (PDT) at the bedside is the most common technique in ICU settings.

stem: A patient with acute exacerbation of COPD is placed on BiPAP. After 30 minutes, significant air leak is noted around the mask. The patient's mouth is open, and the exhaled tidal volume is only 200 mL despite IPAP of 16 cmH2O. What interventions should be tried?
options: Secure a chin strap to reduce mouth leak, adjust mask fit, consider switching to a full-face mask, and re-evaluate exhaled volumes
Increase IPAP to 30 cmH2O to compensate for the leak
Switch to nasal cannula oxygen
Immediately intubate the patient
rationale: Air leak is the most common cause of NIV failure. With a nasal mask, mouth opening allows air to escape, reducing effective ventilation. Interventions in order: 1) Apply a chin strap to keep the mouth closed. 2) Switch to a full-face (oronasal) mask that covers mouth and nose. 3) Readjust mask fit — minimize leak while avoiding excessive strap tension (which causes skin breakdown and patient discomfort). 4) Ensure proper mask size. 5) Some ventilators have leak compensation algorithms that can be adjusted. Effective NIV requires minimal leak, adequate exhaled volumes, and patient cooperation. Intubation is reserved for NIV failure after optimization.

stem: Electrical impedance tomography (EIT) is an emerging tool for monitoring ventilation distribution at the bedside. What information does EIT provide that conventional monitoring cannot?
options: EIT provides real-time, radiation-free visualization of regional ventilation distribution, allowing detection of overdistension and collapse in specific lung regions to guide individualized PEEP and VT settings
EIT measures only heart function, not lung ventilation
EIT is identical to pulse oximetry in the information it provides
EIT requires surgical implantation of electrodes
rationale: EIT uses a belt of 16-32 electrodes placed around the chest to measure changes in thoracic impedance during breathing. As air fills the lungs, impedance increases (air is a poor conductor). EIT provides: real-time regional ventilation maps, detection of ventral overdistension or dorsal collapse, guidance for PEEP titration (identifying optimal PEEP where the least amount of lung is either collapsed or overdistended), assessment of pendelluft, and monitoring of recruitment maneuver effectiveness. Unlike CT, EIT is radiation-free, portable, and continuous. It represents a paradigm shift toward individualized, physiology-guided ventilation.

stem: A patient on volume-controlled ventilation shows a flow-time waveform with an abrupt, sawtooth pattern in the expiratory phase. What does this pattern indicate?
options: Secretions or fluid in the airways causing turbulent flow — the patient needs suctioning
Normal laminar flow pattern during exhalation
Auto-PEEP with air trapping
Ventilator circuit disconnection
rationale: A sawtooth pattern on the expiratory flow-time waveform is characteristic of secretions in the airway or water in the ventilator circuit. The turbulent flow caused by secretions creates irregular flow oscillations visible as a jagged, sawtooth pattern. This finding should prompt: 1) Assessment of the need for suctioning. 2) Check for condensation in the circuit tubing. 3) Evaluation of humidification settings. The sawtooth pattern is a useful visual indicator that precedes audible secretion sounds and can guide proactive airway management. It resolves after effective suctioning or circuit drainage.

stem: What is the role of inhaled nitric oxide (iNO) in mechanical ventilation, and why does it selectively dilate pulmonary vessels in ventilated lung regions?
options: iNO reaches only ventilated alveoli, dilating adjacent pulmonary vessels and improving V/Q matching; it is rapidly inactivated by hemoglobin in the bloodstream, preventing systemic vasodilation
iNO dilates all blood vessels equally, including systemic vessels
iNO constricts pulmonary vessels to improve gas exchange
iNO has no effect on pulmonary vasculature
rationale: Inhaled nitric oxide (iNO) at 5-40 ppm selectively vasodilates pulmonary vessels adjacent to ventilated alveoli — it can only reach vessels near alveoli that receive ventilation. This improves V/Q matching by redirecting blood flow to ventilated regions. Once iNO enters the bloodstream, it is immediately bound and inactivated by hemoglobin, preventing systemic hypotension. This selectivity is unique to inhaled delivery. iNO is used in: persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn (FDA-approved), ARDS (rescue therapy, no proven mortality benefit), and post-cardiac surgery RV failure. Methemoglobin levels must be monitored.

stem: A patient with ARDS has the following ventilator data: VT 380 mL, Pplat 28 cmH2O, PEEP 12 cmH2O, RR 24. IBW is 65 kg. Calculate the tidal volume per kg IBW, driving pressure, and static compliance. Are these values within the lung-protective range?
options: VT/kg = 5.8 mL/kg IBW, driving pressure = 16 cmH2O, static compliance = 23.75 mL/cmH2O; VT and Pplat are within range but driving pressure slightly exceeds the 15 cmH2O target
All values are within the lung-protective range with no concerns
VT is too high at 10 mL/kg IBW
These calculations cannot be performed without cardiac output data
rationale: VT/kg IBW = 380/65 = 5.8 mL/kg (within 4-8 range). Driving pressure (ΔP) = Pplat - PEEP = 28 - 12 = 16 cmH2O (slightly above the ≤15 cmH2O target associated with improved outcomes). Static compliance = VT/(Pplat - PEEP) = 380/16 = 23.75 mL/cmH2O (severely reduced, consistent with ARDS). While VT and Pplat are within traditional limits, the driving pressure of 16 suggests potential overdistension relative to the available functional lung. Consider reducing VT slightly to 350 mL (5.4 mL/kg) to achieve ΔP ≤15, even at the cost of slightly higher PaCO2.

stem: Diaphragm-protective ventilation is an emerging concept alongside lung-protective ventilation. What level of diaphragmatic effort is considered optimal to prevent both disuse atrophy and overexertion injury?
options: Moderate diaphragmatic effort (pressure-time product of 50-150 cmH2O·s/min or thickening fraction of 15-30%) prevents both atrophy and injury
Complete diaphragm rest with full ventilator support is always optimal
Maximum diaphragmatic effort should be encouraged at all times
Diaphragm function is irrelevant during mechanical ventilation
rationale: Diaphragm-protective ventilation aims to maintain intermediate levels of diaphragmatic activity: too little effort (excessive ventilator support) causes rapid disuse atrophy (up to 50% decrease in diaphragm thickness within 48 hours of controlled ventilation), while excessive effort can cause load-induced diaphragm injury, P-SILI, and fatigue. Optimal targets: diaphragm thickening fraction (by ultrasound) 15-30%, or pressure-time product 50-150 cmH2O·s/min. Strategies include: partial ventilatory support (PSV, proportional assist ventilation), avoiding deep sedation, and daily SBTs. Diaphragm ultrasound is increasingly used to monitor diaphragm activity.

stem: A patient has been extubated after 10 days of mechanical ventilation. Within 30 minutes, the patient develops stridor, increased work of breathing, and desaturation. What is the most likely cause and immediate management?
options: Post-extubation laryngeal edema/stridor — administer nebulized racemic epinephrine, IV dexamethasone, and prepare for possible reintubation
Pulmonary embolism — start anticoagulation
Pneumothorax — perform needle decompression
Bronchospasm — administer albuterol MDI
rationale: Post-extubation stridor (PES) occurs in 5-15% of extubated patients due to laryngeal edema from prolonged ETT contact causing mucosal inflammation. Risk factors include: prolonged intubation, large ETT relative to airway, female sex, traumatic intubation, and self-extubation. Immediate management: nebulized racemic epinephrine (0.5 mL of 2.25% in 3 mL NS) — reduces mucosal edema via alpha-adrenergic vasoconstriction; IV dexamethasone (reduces inflammation); HeliOx (80:20 helium-oxygen) to reduce work of breathing through the narrowed airway. If progressive, reintubation with a smaller ETT is needed. Prevention: cuff leak test before extubation, prophylactic steroids in high-risk patients.

stem: A trauma patient requires emergent intubation. The patient has a suspected cervical spine injury, blood in the airway, and a GCS of 6. What is the preferred intubation approach?
options: Rapid sequence intubation (RSI) with manual inline stabilization of the cervical spine, video laryngoscopy preferred, and preparation for a surgical airway as backup
Nasotracheal intubation without sedation
Blind orotracheal intubation
Delay intubation until CT of the cervical spine is completed
rationale: RSI with manual inline stabilization (MILS) is the standard approach for trauma patients with suspected C-spine injury. MILS maintains neutral cervical alignment while the anterior portion of the cervical collar is removed for jaw access. Video laryngoscopy is preferred as it provides better glottic visualization with less cervical spine movement. Suction should be immediately available for blood/secretions. A surgical airway plan (cricothyrotomy) must be immediately available. Nasotracheal intubation is contraindicated with suspected basilar skull fracture. Delaying definitive airway management in a GCS 6 patient with aspiration risk is dangerous.

stem: A patient with severe septic shock requires high-dose vasopressors and is on mechanical ventilation with FiO2 0.50 and PEEP 12 cmH2O. The PEEP is increased to 16 cmH2O for oxygenation. The blood pressure drops from 90/60 to 65/40 mmHg. What happened and what should be done?
options: The PEEP increase reduced venous return in an already preload-dependent patient — reduce PEEP back to 12, give a fluid bolus, and consider increasing vasopressors before re-attempting higher PEEP
The drop in BP is unrelated to the PEEP change
Continue with PEEP 16 as oxygenation is the priority
The hypotension is caused by sepsis progression and PEEP should be increased further
rationale: Increasing PEEP raises intrathoracic pressure, which decreases venous return (preload) to the right heart. In a preload-dependent patient (septic shock with vasodilators causing venous pooling), even small increases in PEEP can cause significant hemodynamic compromise. Management: reduce PEEP to the previous tolerated level, administer IV fluid bolus to increase preload, and consider increasing vasopressor support before re-attempting PEEP titration. Hemodynamic monitoring (arterial line, CVP, echocardiography) is essential when titrating PEEP in hemodynamically unstable patients.

stem: A 52-year-old patient with severe pneumonia develops acute type I (hypoxemic) respiratory failure. ABG shows pH 7.44, PaCO2 32 mmHg, PaO2 52 mmHg on FiO2 0.60 via NRB. The P/F ratio is 87. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Intubation and mechanical ventilation with lung-protective settings — the severely low P/F ratio indicates severe ARDS requiring definitive airway management
Continue NRB mask with close monitoring
Switch to simple face mask at 8 L/min
Start oral antibiotics and discharge home
rationale: A P/F ratio of 87 (<100) with bilateral infiltrates meets Berlin criteria for severe ARDS. Despite adequate ventilatory drive (normal pH with compensatory hyperventilation), the severe hypoxemia unresponsive to high-flow oxygen indicates significant shunt physiology requiring positive pressure ventilation. Initial settings: VT 6 mL/kg IBW, RR to target pH >7.25, PEEP per ARDSNet table (start 12-16 cmH2O for FiO2 0.60), Pplat target ≤30 cmH2O. Consider early prone positioning given P/F <150. NIV may be attempted briefly but has high failure rates in severe ARDS and delays definitive management.

stem: Proportional assist ventilation (PAV+) and neurally adjusted ventilatory assist (NAVA) are proportional modes of ventilation. How do they differ from conventional pressure support?
options: PAV+ and NAVA deliver support proportional to the patient's instantaneous effort — more effort receives more support — unlike PSV which delivers a fixed pressure regardless of effort
PAV+ and NAVA are identical to PSV
PAV+ and NAVA deliver fixed tidal volumes like volume control
These modes cannot be used in ICU ventilators
rationale: In conventional PSV, the set pressure level is constant regardless of how hard the patient breathes. In proportional modes: PAV+ measures instantaneous flow and volume and applies amplification (gain) proportional to the patient's effort — the harder the patient breathes, the more support they receive. NAVA uses an esophageal catheter to detect the electrical activity of the diaphragm (Edi) and delivers pressure proportional to Edi. Both modes improve patient-ventilator synchrony, reduce dyssynchrony, prevent both over- and under-assistance, and may facilitate weaning. NAVA has the advantage of triggering from neural signals, eliminating trigger delays and auto-triggering.

stem: A patient with acute asthma exacerbation is being managed with heliox (80% helium, 20% oxygen). What is the physiologic basis for using heliox in severe airway obstruction?
options: Helium's lower density reduces turbulent airflow resistance through narrowed airways, decreasing work of breathing and improving gas delivery to the distal airways
Helium has direct bronchodilator properties
Helium increases the FiO2 delivered to the patient
Heliox works by increasing mucus clearance
rationale: Helium is 1/7 the density of nitrogen. In turbulent flow (Reynolds number >4000), resistance is proportional to gas density. In narrowed asthmatic airways where turbulent flow predominates, replacing nitrogen with helium reduces gas density, converting turbulent to more laminar flow, reducing airway resistance by 28-50%, and decreasing work of breathing. Heliox does NOT directly bronchodilate — it reduces the resistance to flow through already narrowed airways. Limitations: requires at least 60% helium (max FiO2 0.40 with 60:40 mix), standard flow meters underestimate heliox flow, and nebulizer delivery is altered. It is a bridge therapy while definitive treatments take effect.

stem: A ventilated patient has an acute desaturation event. Using the DOPE mnemonic, what should be systematically assessed?
options: Displacement (ETT displaced/dislodged), Obstruction (ETT blocked by secretions/kink), Pneumothorax, and Equipment failure (ventilator malfunction, circuit disconnect, gas supply)
Diet, Opioids, Position, Electrolytes
Diuretics, Oxygen, Pressors, Electrolytes
Drainage, Obstruction, Paralysis, Edema
rationale: The DOPE mnemonic provides a systematic approach to acute desaturation in ventilated patients: D = Displacement (ETT has migrated out of the trachea or into the right mainstem bronchus — auscultate bilateral breath sounds, check ETT depth marking); O = Obstruction (mucus plug, blood, kinking of ETT — pass suction catheter, check for biting); P = Pneumothorax (tension pneumothorax — absent breath sounds, tracheal deviation — needle decompress); E = Equipment failure (ventilator malfunction, circuit disconnect, loss of gas supply, depleted O2 tank — manually ventilate with bag-valve while troubleshooting). Always disconnect and hand-ventilate while systematically working through DOPE.

stem: High-frequency oscillatory ventilation (HFOV) uses very small tidal volumes at very high frequencies. What are the typical settings and the proposed mechanism of gas exchange?
options: HFOV delivers tidal volumes of 1-3 mL/kg at 3-15 Hz (180-900 breaths/min); gas exchange occurs through several mechanisms including enhanced molecular diffusion, Taylor dispersion, pendelluft, and asymmetric velocity profiles
HFOV uses standard tidal volumes of 6-8 mL/kg at normal rates
HFOV only ventilates the upper airways
Gas exchange in HFOV relies solely on bulk convective flow
rationale: HFOV delivers extremely small tidal volumes (often less than anatomical dead space) at very high frequencies (typically 3-15 Hz). Gas exchange cannot be explained by conventional bulk flow and instead relies on: 1) Enhanced molecular diffusion from high oscillation frequencies. 2) Taylor dispersion (interaction between convective and diffusive transport). 3) Pendelluft (gas mixing between lung units with different time constants). 4) Asymmetric velocity profiles between inspiration and expiration. 5) Cardiogenic mixing. While theoretically ideal for lung protection (minimal VT), the OSCAR and OSCILLATE trials showed no benefit (and potential harm) in adult ARDS. HFOV remains used in neonatal care.

stem: A 67-year-old patient with COPD presents with acute worsening of dyspnea and increased sputum purulence. The Anthonisen criteria classify COPD exacerbations based on cardinal symptoms. Which combination defines a Type I (most severe) exacerbation?
options: All three: increased dyspnea, increased sputum volume, and increased sputum purulence
Only increased dyspnea
Increased sputum purulence alone
Fever with chest pain
rationale: Anthonisen criteria classify COPD exacerbations by cardinal symptoms: Type I — all three cardinal symptoms present (increased dyspnea, increased sputum volume, increased sputum purulence); Type II — two of three symptoms; Type III — one symptom plus at least one additional finding (URI, fever, increased wheezing, increased cough, or 20% increase in HR or RR). Type I exacerbations are most likely bacterial and most likely to benefit from antibiotic therapy. This classification helps guide treatment decisions, particularly antibiotic prescribing.

stem: A patient with known PE is started on heparin anticoagulation. What is the mechanism by which heparin prevents clot propagation, and what monitoring parameter is used?
options: Heparin potentiates antithrombin III activity, inhibiting thrombin and factor Xa; monitored by aPTT (target 1.5-2.5× control) or anti-Xa levels
Heparin directly dissolves existing clots
Heparin is monitored by PT/INR
Heparin blocks platelet aggregation only
rationale: Unfractionated heparin (UFH) binds antithrombin III (AT-III), accelerating its inhibition of thrombin (factor IIa) and factor Xa by 1000-fold. This prevents clot propagation but does not dissolve existing clot — the body's own fibrinolytic system handles clot resolution. Monitoring: aPTT target 1.5-2.5× control value (approximately 60-80 seconds), or anti-Xa level 0.3-0.7 IU/mL. Low molecular weight heparin (enoxaparin) primarily inhibits factor Xa and has more predictable pharmacokinetics, reducing the need for routine monitoring. Complications include HIT (heparin-induced thrombocytopenia) and bleeding.

stem: A patient with cystic fibrosis is prescribed dornase alfa (Pulmozyme). What is the mechanism of action and expected clinical benefit?
options: Dornase alfa is recombinant DNase that cleaves extracellular DNA in CF sputum, reducing mucus viscosity and improving airway clearance
Dornase alfa is a bronchodilator that relaxes airway smooth muscle
Dornase alfa is an antibiotic that kills Pseudomonas
Dornase alfa stimulates surfactant production
rationale: In cystic fibrosis, neutrophil-rich airway inflammation results in high concentrations of extracellular DNA from degranulated neutrophils, contributing to the extreme viscosity of CF sputum. Dornase alfa (recombinant human DNase I) cleaves this extracellular DNA, reducing mucus viscosity and improving mucociliary and cough clearance. Clinical benefits include improved FEV1 (5-8% improvement), reduced exacerbation frequency, and improved quality of life. It is administered via nebulizer once daily. It is specific to CF and not beneficial in non-CF bronchiectasis.

stem: A patient receiving invasive mechanical ventilation develops subcutaneous emphysema of the chest wall, neck, and face. Peak pressures are elevated. What underlying complication should be suspected?
options: Pneumomediastinum or pneumothorax with air tracking along fascial planes
Allergic reaction to medications
Fluid overload
Ventilator circuit over-pressurization
rationale: Subcutaneous emphysema (crepitus on palpation — air under the skin) in a ventilated patient indicates air leak from the respiratory tract, most commonly from pneumothorax or pneumomediastinum. Air tracks along fascial planes from the mediastinum to the neck, face, and chest wall. Immediate evaluation: chest X-ray for pneumothorax/pneumomediastinum, CT if X-ray is equivocal. If tension pneumothorax is present, immediate decompression is required. Elevated peak pressures suggest the underlying air leak source. Reduce ventilator pressures and volumes if possible to minimize ongoing air leak.

stem: During bronchoscopy in a ventilated patient, the respiratory therapist must adjust the ventilator to compensate for the reduced effective cross-sectional area of the ETT. What adjustments are typically needed?
options: Increase FiO2 to 1.0, increase PEEP slightly to compensate for air leak, and expect higher peak pressures due to increased airway resistance from the bronchoscope occupying the ETT lumen
No ventilator adjustments are needed during bronchoscopy
Decrease FiO2 to prevent oxygen toxicity during the procedure
Switch to spontaneous breathing mode during bronchoscopy
rationale: A bronchoscope in the ETT significantly reduces the effective airway diameter (occupying 40-66% of ETT cross-sectional area depending on sizes). Per Poiseuille's law, this dramatically increases resistance (resistance ∝ 1/r⁴). Adjustments needed: 1) FiO2 to 1.0 — desaturation is common during bronchoscopy. 2) Expect elevated PIP from increased resistance. 3) Auto-PEEP may develop from impaired exhalation. 4) Consider increasing PEEP slightly to offset air leak around the bronchoscope. 5) Use volume control to guarantee VT delivery. 6) Have manual ventilation available as backup.

stem: A patient with acute respiratory failure and bilateral infiltrates has a P/F ratio of 180 on PEEP 10 cmH2O. Echocardiography shows normal LV function with EF 60% and no elevated left atrial pressure. The onset was acute following aspiration. Does this patient meet Berlin criteria for ARDS?
options: Yes — acute onset, bilateral opacities, P/F 100-200 (moderate ARDS) on PEEP ≥5 cmH2O, and respiratory failure not fully explained by cardiac failure
No — the P/F ratio is too high for ARDS
No — aspiration cannot cause ARDS
Cannot be determined without a pulmonary artery catheter
rationale: The Berlin definition (2012) requires: 1) Acute onset within 1 week of known clinical insult or new/worsening respiratory symptoms — yes, acute post-aspiration. 2) Bilateral opacities on imaging not fully explained by effusions, lobar/lung collapse, or nodules — yes, bilateral infiltrates. 3) Respiratory failure not fully explained by cardiac failure or fluid overload (echo confirms normal LV function). 4) PaO2/FiO2 ≤300 on PEEP ≥5 cmH2O — P/F of 180 on PEEP 10, meeting moderate ARDS criteria (100 < P/F ≤ 200). Aspiration is a recognized direct (pulmonary) risk factor for ARDS.

stem: A patient with severe asthma has been started on IV ketamine for sedation. What property of ketamine makes it particularly useful in status asthmaticus?
options: Ketamine is a bronchodilator that relaxes airway smooth muscle via catecholamine release and direct smooth muscle relaxation
Ketamine is a bronchoconstrictor that increases airway resistance
Ketamine has no effect on the respiratory system
Ketamine is only useful as a paralytic agent
rationale: Ketamine is a dissociative anesthetic with significant bronchodilatory properties through multiple mechanisms: 1) Stimulates catecholamine release (sympathomimetic effect causing beta-2 mediated bronchodilation). 2) Direct relaxation of airway smooth muscle. 3) Inhibits vagally-mediated bronchoconstriction. 4) Preserves respiratory drive and protective airway reflexes (unlike other sedatives). These properties make ketamine the preferred induction and sedation agent for intubation and ventilation of severe asthmatic patients. Dose for sedation: 0.5-1.0 mg/kg IV bolus, then 0.5-1.0 mg/kg/hr infusion. Side effects include emergence reactions, hypersalivation, and increased ICP.

stem: A ventilated patient on neuromuscular blockade is being monitored with train-of-four (TOF) stimulation. The TOF shows 2 out of 4 twitches. What does this indicate about the level of paralysis?
options: Approximately 75-80% of receptors are blocked — moderate neuromuscular blockade appropriate for ARDS management (target 1-2 twitches)
No significant paralysis — all receptors are available
Complete paralysis — no twitches should be visible
The TOF monitor is malfunctioning
rationale: Train-of-four (TOF) monitoring delivers four sequential stimulations to a peripheral nerve (usually ulnar nerve) and counts visible twitches. The number of twitches correlates with receptor occupancy: 4/4 twitches = <75% receptor blockade; 3/4 = ~75%; 2/4 = ~80%; 1/4 = ~85-90%; 0/4 = >90%. For ARDS management with neuromuscular blockade, the target is typically 1-2 out of 4 twitches (75-85% blockade), providing adequate paralysis for ventilator synchrony while minimizing drug accumulation. Deeper paralysis (0/4) increases the risk of prolonged weakness and should be avoided.

stem: A patient with ARDS is being considered for conservative versus liberal fluid management. The FACTT trial compared these strategies. What did the trial find?
options: Conservative fluid strategy improved oxygenation and reduced ventilator days without increasing organ failure, compared to liberal fluid management
Liberal fluid management improved all outcomes
Fluid management has no effect on ARDS outcomes
Conservative fluids caused increased renal failure
rationale: The FACTT trial (Fluid and Catheter Treatment Trial, 2006) randomized ARDS patients to conservative vs. liberal fluid management after initial resuscitation. Conservative strategy targeted CVP <4 (or PAOP <8) using diuretics and fluid restriction. Results: conservative strategy had significantly improved oxygenation index, more ventilator-free days (14.6 vs 11.2), and more ICU-free days, without increased non-pulmonary organ failure or dialysis need. No mortality difference was demonstrated. Conservative fluid management (after initial hemodynamic stabilization) is now standard practice in ARDS management.

stem: A patient is being ventilated with VT 420 mL at a respiratory rate of 22. The measured ETCO2 is 32 mmHg but the PaCO2 on ABG is 48 mmHg. What does the large gradient between ETCO2 and PaCO2 indicate?
options: Increased dead space ventilation — a large PaCO2-ETCO2 gradient indicates significant alveolar dead space, commonly seen in PE, ARDS, or low cardiac output
Normal relationship between ETCO2 and PaCO2
The capnograph is reading too high
The ABG sample was venous, not arterial
rationale: The normal PaCO2-ETCO2 gradient is 2-5 mmHg (ETCO2 is normally slightly lower than PaCO2). A gradient of 16 mmHg (48 - 32) indicates significant alveolar dead space — ventilated areas that are not receiving perfusion. Dead space gas (containing no CO2) dilutes the exhaled CO2, lowering ETCO2 while PaCO2 remains elevated. Common causes: pulmonary embolism (classic finding), ARDS, low cardiac output states, excessive PEEP (compressing alveolar capillaries), and pulmonary hypertension. This gradient can be used to estimate VD/VT and guide management.

stem: A 48-year-old patient develops acute hypoxemic respiratory failure after major abdominal surgery. The patient has no prior lung disease. CT shows bilateral ground-glass opacities with dependent consolidation. Echocardiogram shows normal cardiac function. What is the initial ventilator strategy?
options: Lung-protective ventilation with VT 6-8 mL/kg IBW, PEEP titrated to oxygenation and mechanics, FiO2 targeted to SpO2 88-95%, and early consideration of prone positioning if P/F < 150
High tidal volume ventilation with VT 12 mL/kg to rapidly recruit collapsed lung
Minimal PEEP (3 cmH2O) with high FiO2 (1.0) to maximize oxygen delivery
Immediate ECMO without attempting conventional ventilation
rationale: Post-operative ARDS requires the same evidence-based lung-protective ventilation strategy: VT 6-8 mL/kg IBW (not actual weight), Pplat ≤30 cmH2O, driving pressure ≤15 cmH2O, PEEP titrated by ARDSNet table or best compliance, and FiO2 to achieve SpO2 88-95%. Early prone positioning is indicated if P/F <150 despite initial optimization. Post-surgical ARDS risk factors include aspiration, transfusion-related lung injury (TRALI), sepsis, and direct surgical lung manipulation. Adequate analgesia is important to allow lung-protective ventilation without excessive sedation.

stem: A patient with myasthenia gravis is admitted with worsening weakness. The vital capacity (VC) has dropped from 2.5 L to 1.2 L over 24 hours. The 20/30/40 rule suggests intubation criteria. What are these thresholds?
options: VC < 20 mL/kg, MIP > -30 cmH2O (less negative), and MEP < 40 cmH2O indicate the need for intubation
VC < 20 L, MIP < -30, MEP < 40
Heart rate > 20, respiratory rate > 30, SpO2 < 40%
The 20/30/40 rule applies to asthma, not neuromuscular disease
rationale: The 20/30/40 rule provides objective criteria for intubation in neuromuscular respiratory failure: VC < 20 mL/kg (or absolute VC < 1 L) — inadequate ventilatory reserve; MIP less negative than -30 cmH2O (e.g., -20, -15) — weak inspiratory muscles unable to generate adequate tidal volumes; MEP < 40 cmH2O — weak expiratory muscles unable to generate effective cough for secretion clearance. This patient's declining VC (from 2.5 L to 1.2 L in 24 hours) demonstrates rapid deterioration. If the patient weighs 70 kg, VC 1.2 L = 17 mL/kg, meeting intubation criteria. Serial measurements every 4-6 hours are more valuable than single assessments.

stem: A patient with severe ARDS has been on mechanical ventilation for 5 days. The team obtains a respiratory system compliance curve (pressure-volume curve). The compliance is 18 mL/cmH2O with PEEP 12 cmH2O. After increasing PEEP to 16 cmH2O, compliance improves to 28 mL/cmH2O. What does this improvement indicate?
options: The higher PEEP recruited previously collapsed lung units, increasing the functional lung size and improving compliance — this suggests PEEP-responsive (recruitable) lung
The improvement is coincidental and unrelated to PEEP
Compliance should decrease with higher PEEP if lungs are recruitable
PEEP changes have no effect on compliance
rationale: Improved compliance with increased PEEP indicates that the PEEP increment opened previously collapsed (atelectatic) lung units, increasing the functional lung volume available for ventilation. This is PEEP-responsive or recruitable lung — a sign that the patient will benefit from an open-lung strategy. In contrast, if compliance worsened with higher PEEP, it would suggest overdistension of already open alveoli without further recruitment. Tracking compliance during incremental PEEP titration (decremental PEEP trial) helps identify the optimal PEEP for each patient — the PEEP at which compliance is maximized.

stem: A premature neonate at 26 weeks gestation is receiving surfactant replacement therapy via the INSURE method. What does INSURE stand for, and what is its advantage over conventional surfactant administration?
options: INtubate, SURfactant, Extubate — it allows surfactant delivery while minimizing the duration of mechanical ventilation by rapidly returning to non-invasive support
INSert, Utilize, REpeat — referring to repeated surfactant doses
INSert, Utilize, REplace — referring to ETT replacement after surfactant
INSURE is a brand name for a specific surfactant preparation
rationale: INSURE (INtubate, SURfactant, Extubate to CPAP) is a strategy for surfactant delivery that minimizes mechanical ventilation exposure. The neonate is briefly intubated, surfactant is instilled via the ETT, and the patient is immediately extubated to nasal CPAP. This avoids the prolonged mechanical ventilation associated with conventional surfactant delivery (intubate, ventilate, slowly wean). Benefits include reduced ventilator-induced lung injury, reduced BPD risk, and shorter hospital stay. Newer techniques like LISA (Less Invasive Surfactant Administration) use a thin catheter placed under direct laryngoscopy during spontaneous breathing on CPAP, avoiding intubation entirely.

stem: A patient has been on mechanical ventilation for 21 days and is diagnosed with ventilator-associated diaphragm dysfunction (VIDD). Diaphragm ultrasound shows a thickening fraction of 5% (normal >20%). What is the pathophysiology of VIDD?
options: Controlled mechanical ventilation causes rapid diaphragm atrophy through proteolytic pathways, oxidative stress, and disuse; the diaphragm can lose 50% of its force-generating capacity within 48-72 hours
VIDD is caused by infection of the diaphragm muscle
Diaphragm dysfunction only occurs after >30 days of ventilation
VIDD is caused by phrenic nerve damage during central line placement
rationale: Ventilator-induced diaphragmatic dysfunction (VIDD) is a form of disuse atrophy caused by diaphragmatic unloading during controlled mechanical ventilation. Within 18-48 hours of controlled ventilation, diaphragmatic myofiber atrophy begins through activation of proteolytic pathways (ubiquitin-proteasome system, calpain, caspase-3), mitochondrial dysfunction, and oxidative stress. Studies show up to 50% loss of diaphragmatic force within 5-6 days. Diaphragm ultrasound monitors thickness (atrophy if decreased >10%) and thickening fraction (effort). Prevention: allow spontaneous breathing (PSV, PAV, NAVA), minimize deep sedation, early mobilization, and inspiratory muscle training.

stem: A patient with restrictive lung disease from morbid obesity is being ventilated post-operatively. The patient has extremely poor compliance (12 mL/cmH2O) and plateau pressures are reaching 35 cmH2O with VT of only 300 mL. Transpulmonary pressure measured with an esophageal balloon shows the end-inspiratory transpulmonary pressure is only 18 cmH2O. Is the patient at risk for lung overdistension?
options: No — the high airway pressure is mostly overcoming chest wall elastance; the transpulmonary pressure of 18 cmH2O indicates the lung itself is not overdistended
Yes — any plateau pressure above 30 cmH2O causes lung injury regardless of transpulmonary pressure
Transpulmonary pressure is irrelevant in obese patients
The patient should be extubated immediately since airway pressures are dangerous
rationale: In morbidly obese patients, a significant portion of the airway pressure is required to overcome the heavy chest wall (elevated chest wall elastance from adipose tissue and abdominal pressure). The transpulmonary pressure (PL = Paw - Pes) isolates the pressure actually distending the lung. An end-inspiratory PL of 18 cmH2O is within safe limits (<25 cmH2O), indicating the lung itself is not overdistended despite the high Pplat. This scenario demonstrates why esophageal manometry is valuable in obese patients — standard Pplat targets may be misleadingly high, causing inappropriate tidal volume reduction and worsening atelectasis.

stem: A patient with severe community-acquired pneumonia has a respiratory rate of 38, SpO2 85% on 15 L NRB, and is using accessory muscles. The team is debating between HFNC and immediate intubation. What clinical factors favor immediate intubation over HFNC in this scenario?
options: Hemodynamic instability, altered mental status, inability to protect the airway, severe respiratory acidosis, or multi-organ failure favor immediate intubation over HFNC trial
All patients should try HFNC before intubation regardless of severity
Intubation should always be avoided if the patient is conscious
HFNC and intubation have identical outcomes in all patients
rationale: While HFNC may be appropriate as initial therapy for many patients with hypoxemic respiratory failure, immediate intubation is favored when: 1) Hemodynamic instability (need for vasopressors — risk of arrest during HFNC). 2) Altered mental status/inability to protect airway (aspiration risk). 3) Severe respiratory acidosis (pH <7.25) indicating ventilatory failure. 4) Multi-organ failure (need for controlled ventilation). 5) Inability to manage secretions. 6) Anticipated difficult airway (intubate while still controlled rather than emergently during HFNC failure). Delayed intubation after HFNC failure is associated with worse outcomes. The ROX index and clinical trajectory should guide escalation decisions.

stem: A patient with COPD on long-term oxygen therapy has a resting PaO2 of 55 mmHg on room air. The respiratory therapist performs an oxygen titration study. What is the target PaO2 and SpO2 during titration?
options: Target PaO2 ≥60 mmHg and SpO2 88-92% — enough to reach the steep portion of the oxyhemoglobin curve while avoiding oxygen-induced hypercapnia
Target PaO2 100 mmHg and SpO2 100%
Target PaO2 40 mmHg and SpO2 75%
No specific target — use the highest FiO2 available
rationale: For COPD patients on LTOT, the oxygen titration target is PaO2 ≥60 mmHg or SpO2 88-92%. This range corresponds to the steep portion of the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve where SaO2 is 90-95% — adequate for tissue oxygenation. Targeting higher levels in chronic CO2 retainers risks suppressing hypoxic drive, causing hypoventilation and worsening hypercapnia. The titration should be performed at rest, during exercise, and during sleep, as oxygen requirements differ between activities. Starting at 1-2 L/min via nasal cannula with ABG confirmation is standard practice.

stem: A patient presents with severe hyperkalemia (K+ 7.2 mEq/L) and ABG: pH 7.18, PaCO2 28 mmHg, HCO3 11 mEq/L. What is the relationship between acidosis and hyperkalemia, and how does treating the acidosis affect potassium levels?
options: In acidosis, H+ ions move intracellularly while K+ shifts extracellularly, raising serum K+; correcting the acidosis reverses this shift, lowering serum K+
Acidosis and hyperkalemia are unrelated conditions
Treating acidosis always worsens hyperkalemia
Potassium only shifts during respiratory acidosis, not metabolic
rationale: In metabolic acidosis, the excess H+ ions are buffered intracellularly. To maintain electrical neutrality, K+ exits cells in exchange for H+ entering, raising serum potassium by approximately 0.6 mEq/L for each 0.1 unit decrease in pH. This transcellular shift means serum K+ overestimates total body potassium. Correcting acidosis (with sodium bicarbonate or treating the underlying cause) reverses the shift, driving K+ back into cells and lowering serum K+. Important consideration: once acidosis is corrected, the patient may become hypokalemic if total body K+ was already depleted (as in DKA). Monitor potassium closely during treatment.

stem: What are the five types of hypoxia, and which type is NOT reflected by a low PaO2?
options: Hypoxemic (low PaO2), anemic (low Hb), circulatory/stagnant (poor perfusion), histotoxic (mitochondrial poisoning), and demand (excessive consumption); anemic, circulatory, histotoxic, and demand hypoxia have normal PaO2
There is only one type of hypoxia — low PaO2
All types of hypoxia are reflected by low PaO2
Hypoxia and hypoxemia are identical terms
rationale: Five types of tissue hypoxia: 1) Hypoxemic — decreased PaO2 (lung disease, altitude). 2) Anemic — decreased Hb content (hemorrhage, CO poisoning, methemoglobinemia) — normal PaO2 but decreased CaO2. 3) Circulatory/stagnant — decreased cardiac output or blood flow (heart failure, shock) — normal PaO2 and CaO2 but impaired delivery. 4) Histotoxic — mitochondrial inability to use delivered O2 (cyanide poisoning) — normal PaO2, CaO2, and delivery, but tissues cannot utilize oxygen. 5) Demand — oxygen consumption exceeds delivery (thyrotoxicosis, seizures, severe fever). Understanding these types is crucial because treatment differs for each.

stem: A patient with severe ARDS is receiving inhaled epoprostenol (prostacyclin) as rescue therapy. What is the mechanism of action and expected physiologic effect?
options: Inhaled epoprostenol selectively dilates pulmonary vessels in ventilated lung regions (like iNO), improving V/Q matching and oxygenation without systemic hypotension
Epoprostenol constricts pulmonary vessels to reduce shunt
Epoprostenol is a bronchodilator with no vascular effects
Inhaled epoprostenol works identically to IV epoprostenol
rationale: Inhaled epoprostenol (prostacyclin) is a potent pulmonary vasodilator that, when delivered via the inhaled route, selectively reaches ventilated alveoli. It dilates the pulmonary vessels adjacent to these ventilated regions, redirecting blood flow from shunt units to gas-exchange units, improving V/Q matching and oxygenation. Unlike IV epoprostenol (which causes systemic vasodilation and hypotension), the inhaled route limits effects to the pulmonary circulation. Inhaled epoprostenol is less expensive than iNO and may have comparable efficacy. It is used as rescue therapy in ARDS and for acute pulmonary hypertension.

stem: A patient with type I respiratory failure (hypoxemic) has been placed on CPAP at 10 cmH2O via face mask. What is the mechanism by which CPAP improves oxygenation?
options: CPAP maintains continuous positive airway pressure throughout the respiratory cycle, preventing alveolar collapse, recruiting atelectatic lung units, and increasing FRC
CPAP delivers variable positive and negative pressures
CPAP provides tidal volume support like a ventilator
CPAP only works by increasing the FiO2 delivered
rationale: CPAP applies a constant positive pressure during both inspiration and expiration. Mechanisms of improved oxygenation: 1) Splints open airways and prevents alveolar collapse at end-expiration. 2) Recruits previously atelectatic lung units, converting shunt to functional gas exchange. 3) Increases FRC, keeping the lung volume above closing capacity. 4) Reduces the work of breathing by offsetting intrinsic PEEP. 5) In cardiogenic pulmonary edema, reduces preload and afterload by increasing intrathoracic pressure. Unlike BiPAP, CPAP does not provide inspiratory pressure support above the set CPAP level — it does not augment tidal volume or assist ventilation.

stem: A patient on mechanical ventilation has developed critical illness polyneuropathy/myopathy (CIP/CIM). What are the risk factors and how does this affect ventilator weaning?
options: Sepsis, multi-organ failure, prolonged immobility, corticosteroids, and neuromuscular blockade are risk factors; CIP/CIM causes generalized weakness including respiratory muscles, significantly prolonging weaning
CIP/CIM only affects the extremities and does not impact weaning
CIP/CIM resolves immediately when the patient is extubated
Only corticosteroid use causes CIP/CIM
rationale: Critical illness polyneuropathy (CIP) and critical illness myopathy (CIM) are common ICU complications affecting 25-80% of ICU patients with sepsis or MODS. Risk factors: sepsis, multi-organ failure, prolonged immobilization, corticosteroids (especially with NMBAs), hyperglycemia, and ICU stay >7 days. CIP/CIM causes diffuse weakness including respiratory muscles, with diaphragmatic weakness being a major barrier to ventilator weaning. Diagnosis: EMG/nerve conduction studies or diaphragm ultrasound. Treatment: no specific therapy — supportive care with early mobilization, glycemic control, minimizing NMBAs, and prolonged weaning protocols. Recovery may take weeks to months.

stem: A patient who was difficult to ventilate in the supine position is moved to the lateral decubitus position with the unaffected lung down. The patient's oxygenation worsens. What physiologic explanation accounts for this?
options: In lateral positioning, perfusion follows gravity to the dependent (down) lung; if the diseased lung is up, ventilation goes to the better but non-dependent (up) lung while blood flows to the less-ventilated dependent (down) lung, worsening V/Q mismatch
Lateral positioning always improves oxygenation
The dependent lung always receives more ventilation than perfusion
Body position has no effect on gas exchange
rationale: In the lateral decubitus position, gravity directs blood flow preferentially to the dependent (lower) lung, which receives approximately 60% of perfusion. Ventilation also preferentially goes to the dependent lung (the diaphragm is in a more mechanically advantageous position). With unilateral lung disease, the ideal position is diseased lung UP (non-dependent): perfusion is directed to the healthier dependent lung where gas exchange is preserved. If the healthy lung is placed UP and the diseased lung is dependent, perfusion goes to the poorly ventilated diseased lung, worsening V/Q mismatch and shunt. Exception: in pediatric patients, the relationship may differ due to different chest wall compliance.

stem: A patient with acute respiratory failure is receiving bag-mask ventilation. The respiratory therapist notes poor chest rise despite using a two-person technique with proper head positioning. What is the most likely cause, and what rescue airway should be considered?
options: Upper airway obstruction from tongue or soft tissue — insert an oropharyngeal airway (OPA) to displace the tongue and improve ventilation
The bag is not generating enough pressure
Chest wall rigidity from medications
The patient has a tension pneumothorax
rationale: The most common cause of difficult bag-mask ventilation is upper airway obstruction from the tongue falling back against the posterior pharynx. Despite proper positioning (jaw thrust, head tilt-chin lift, two-person technique), obesity, facial hair, edentulous patients, and neck masses can make BVM difficult. An oropharyngeal airway (OPA) — sized from the corner of the mouth to the tragus of the ear — lifts the tongue off the posterior pharynx. A nasopharyngeal airway (NPA) is an alternative when trismus prevents OPA insertion. If BVM remains inadequate, supraglottic airways (LMA, King airway) or intubation are next steps. The MOANS mnemonic (Mask seal, Obstruction, Age >55, No teeth, Stiffness) predicts difficult BVM.

stem: During prone positioning for ARDS, a ventilated patient develops facial edema and serous drainage from the eyes. Are these findings expected, and what precautions should be taken?
options: Facial and periorbital edema are expected complications of prone positioning due to dependent fluid redistribution; precautions include careful eye care, head repositioning every 2 hours, and padding pressure points
These findings indicate an allergic reaction requiring immediate supination
Facial edema is a contraindication to continued prone positioning
No special precautions are needed during prone positioning
rationale: During prone positioning, gravity causes fluid redistribution to the dependent face, resulting in facial and periorbital edema. This is expected and not a reason to discontinue proning. Precautions: 1) Eye care — use lubricating eye drops/ointment, tape eyelids closed, check eyes every 2 hours for pressure injury. 2) Reposition the head every 2 hours alternating left and right to prevent pressure injuries. 3) Pad pressure points (forehead, cheeks, chest, iliac crests, knees). 4) Ensure ETT is secured with tape (not ties that may shift). 5) Maintain continuous monitoring of ETT position. Facial edema typically resolves within hours of returning to supine position.

stem: A patient with interstitial lung disease has severely reduced DLCO (25% predicted) and develops exercise-induced hypoxemia with SpO2 dropping from 95% to 80% during a 6-minute walk test. Why does exercise worsen hypoxemia in diffusion-limited disease?
options: During exercise, increased cardiac output reduces pulmonary capillary transit time below the time needed for complete diffusion equilibrium across the thickened alveolar membrane
Exercise increases dead space ventilation
Exercise causes bronchospasm in ILD
Exercise reduces cardiac output, causing V/Q mismatch
rationale: At rest, blood spends approximately 0.75 seconds in the pulmonary capillary. Oxygen equilibration normally completes in 0.25 seconds, providing a 3× safety margin. In diffusion-limited disease (fibrosis, thickened membrane), equilibration takes longer but may still complete at rest. During exercise, increased cardiac output shortens capillary transit time to 0.25-0.35 seconds. If the thickened membrane requires longer than this reduced transit time for equilibration, end-capillary PO2 will be low — diffusion limitation becomes clinically apparent as exercise-induced hypoxemia. This is why exercise SpO2 monitoring and the 6MWT are important in ILD assessment.

stem: A patient with ARDS on FiO2 0.90 has been stable for 24 hours. The respiratory therapist wants to wean FiO2. What is the general approach to FiO2 weaning in ARDS?
options: Decrease FiO2 in increments of 0.05-0.10, monitoring SpO2 and targeting SpO2 88-95%; reduce FiO2 to ≤0.60 as quickly as tolerated to minimize oxygen toxicity before weaning PEEP
Rapidly decrease FiO2 from 0.90 to 0.21 in one step
Wean PEEP first, then FiO2
Keep FiO2 at 0.90 until the patient is ready for extubation
rationale: FiO2 weaning in ARDS follows a systematic approach: 1) Priority is reducing FiO2 to ≤0.60 (below which oxygen toxicity risk decreases significantly). 2) Decrease in increments of 0.05-0.10 every 15-30 minutes, monitoring SpO2 (target 88-95%). 3) Generally, FiO2 is weaned before PEEP because high FiO2 carries oxygen toxicity risk (free radical damage, absorption atelectasis) while PEEP supports recruitment. 4) Once FiO2 ≤0.40, begin gradual PEEP reduction (1-2 cmH2O decrements). 5) Monitor for derecruitment with PEEP changes (SpO2 drop, worsening compliance). Rapid FiO2 reduction can cause rebound hypoxemia from absorption atelectasis in high-FiO2 units.

stem: A patient with severe bronchiectasis produces over 300 mL of sputum daily. What airway clearance techniques are most effective for mobilizing secretions in bronchiectasis?
options: High-frequency chest wall oscillation (vest), oscillating PEP devices (Acapella/Flutter), postural drainage with percussion, and active cycle of breathing technique (ACBT)
Cough suppression with codeine
Bed rest with minimal movement
Deep suctioning every hour through nasopharyngeal catheter
rationale: Bronchiectasis requires multimodal airway clearance: 1) HFCWO (vest therapy) — delivers oscillatory positive pressures to the chest wall, shearing mucus from airway walls. 2) Oscillating PEP devices (Acapella, Flutter, Aerobika) — generate oscillatory positive expiratory pressure that splints airways open and vibrates secretions loose. 3) Postural drainage with percussion — uses gravity to drain specific lung segments (modify positions based on involvement). 4) ACBT — cycles of breathing control, thoracic expansion, and forced expiratory technique (huffing). 5) Nebulized hypertonic saline (7%) — osmotically draws fluid into airways, hydrating secretions. 6) Regular exercise — improves mucociliary clearance and cardiovascular fitness.

stem: An ABG drawn from a patient's radial artery shows pH 7.48, PaCO2 25 mmHg, HCO3 18 mEq/L. The patient is hyperventilating due to pain after surgery. After treating the pain with morphine, a repeat ABG 30 minutes later shows pH 7.40, PaCO2 38 mmHg, HCO3 23 mEq/L. What does this rapid normalization indicate about the nature of the original disorder?
options: The original acute respiratory alkalosis resolved once the stimulus (pain-driven hyperventilation) was removed, confirming it was a simple, acute disorder without significant metabolic component
The patient developed a new metabolic disorder
The repeat ABG is abnormal and requires treatment
Morphine caused metabolic compensation
rationale: The rapid normalization of ABG values after pain control confirms that the original acid-base disturbance was simple acute respiratory alkalosis driven by pain-induced hyperventilation. Key points: 1) Acute respiratory alkalosis has minimal metabolic compensation because renal mechanisms take 24-72 hours to respond. 2) The slight decrease in HCO3 (18 to 23) was from acute buffering, not renal compensation. 3) Once the stimulus was removed, ventilation normalized and the acid-base status corrected within minutes. This case illustrates the importance of identifying and treating the underlying cause of acid-base disorders rather than treating the numbers alone.

stem: A patient with a large right pleural effusion has a mediastinal shift to the left on chest X-ray. After thoracentesis removing 1.5 L of fluid, the patient develops severe cough, chest tightness, and frothy sputum from the right lung. What complication has occurred?
options: Re-expansion pulmonary edema — rapid re-inflation of the collapsed lung caused increased capillary permeability and edema
Tension pneumothorax from the thoracentesis
Pulmonary embolism during the procedure
Allergic reaction to the local anesthetic
rationale: Re-expansion pulmonary edema (RPE) occurs when a chronically collapsed lung is rapidly re-expanded, either by thoracentesis for large effusions or after evacuation of pneumothorax. Mechanisms include: reperfusion injury with increased capillary permeability, surfactant dysfunction in the collapsed lung, and generation of free radicals. Risk increases when >1-1.5 L is removed at once or when the effusion has been present >3 days. Prevention: limit removal to 1-1.5 L per session, stop if the patient develops cough or chest tightness, and use manometry to monitor pleural pressure (stop if pressure drops below -20 cmH2O). Treatment is supportive with oxygen and positive pressure if severe.

stem: A patient on long-term mechanical ventilation is being assessed with diaphragm ultrasound. The diaphragm thickness at end-expiration is measured at 1.5 mm (normal >2 mm), and the thickening fraction during a spontaneous breath is 8% (normal >20%). What do these measurements indicate for weaning prognosis?
