options: Use a video laryngoscope (provides an indirect view around the tongue), attempt bougie-assisted intubation, or use a supraglottic airway as a bridge while preparing for alternative intubation techniques
Perform a surgical cricothyrotomy immediately
Attempt blind intubation with maximum force
Wait for the patient to wake up and attempt again
rationale: A Cormack-Lehane Grade III view (only epiglottis visible) with direct laryngoscopy indicates a difficult airway. Stepwise approach: 1) Optimize positioning (ear-to-sternal notch alignment, ramping in obese patients). 2) Switch to video laryngoscopy (VL provides a magnified indirect view — improves Grade III to Grade I in 75-90% of cases). 3) Use a bougie (gum elastic bougie) — can be passed blindly under the epiglottis into the trachea, then rail-road the ETT over it. 4) If unable to intubate, place a supraglottic airway (LMA, King) as a bridge while preparing alternatives (fiberoptic intubation, cricothyrotomy). 5) Surgical cricothyrotomy is reserved for 'can't intubate, can't oxygenate' (CICO) scenarios.

stem: A post-cardiac surgery patient develops phrenic nerve injury causing unilateral diaphragm paralysis. Fluoroscopy shows paradoxical upward movement of the affected hemidiaphragm during sniff test. What is the expected impact on lung function?
options: Reduction in FVC by 15-25% (more in supine position), decreased exercise tolerance, and risk of basilar atelectasis on the affected side
Complete respiratory failure requiring permanent ventilation
No impact on lung function — the other hemidiaphragm compensates completely
Only affects pulmonary function during sleep
rationale: Unilateral diaphragm paralysis causes: 1) FVC reduction of 15-25% (up to 50% reduction in supine position when gravity pushes abdominal contents against the paralyzed hemidiaphragm). 2) Reduced exercise capacity (the contralateral hemidiaphragm and accessory muscles compensate but cannot fully match bilateral function). 3) Basilar atelectasis on the affected side. 4) The sniff test on fluoroscopy shows paradoxical upward movement — during rapid inspiration, the functional hemidiaphragm descends, creating negative intrathoracic pressure that pulls the paralyzed hemidiaphragm upward. Most patients compensate adequately. Recovery may occur (6-24 months) if the nerve injury is neuropraxia rather than transection. Diaphragm plication may benefit symptomatic patients without expected recovery.

stem: A patient with moderate ARDS is receiving lung-protective ventilation. The respiratory therapist is asked about the role of corticosteroids in ARDS. Based on the DEXA-ARDS trial, what is the current evidence?
options: Early dexamethasone (within 24 hours of ARDS onset) for 10 days reduces ventilator days and mortality in moderate-severe ARDS
Corticosteroids are contraindicated in all phases of ARDS
Only high-dose methylprednisolone is effective
Corticosteroids should only be started after day 14 of ARDS
rationale: The DEXA-ARDS trial (Villar et al., Lancet RM 2020) randomized moderate-severe ARDS patients (P/F ≤200 at 24 hours despite lung-protective ventilation) to IV dexamethasone 20 mg daily for 5 days, then 10 mg daily for 5 days, vs. standard care. Results: significantly more ventilator-free days (12.3 vs 7.5) and lower 60-day mortality (21% vs 36%). The COVID-STEROID-2 trial further supported dexamethasone in ARDS. Current evidence favors early (within 24-72 hours) corticosteroid use in moderate-severe ARDS. Starting after day 14 is not recommended (increased mortality in late-phase corticosteroid initiation). Benefit is greatest when started early in the fibroproliferative phase.

stem: A ventilated patient with cirrhosis and ascites develops acute respiratory distress. The respiratory therapist notes that abdominal distension is increasing, plateau pressures have risen from 24 to 34 cmH2O, and tidal volumes are decreasing (pressure-controlled mode). What is the most likely cause of the respiratory deterioration?
options: Increased intra-abdominal pressure from tense ascites causing elevated diaphragm position, decreased lung compliance, and functional restriction
Acute ARDS from liver failure
Ventilator malfunction
Bilateral pneumothoraces
rationale: Tense ascites increases intra-abdominal pressure (IAP), pushing the diaphragm cephalad. This causes: 1) Decreased FRC and total lung volume (functional restriction). 2) Basilar atelectasis. 3) Decreased compliance (the elevated plateau pressure reflects increased chest wall elastance from abdominal pressure). 4) Decreased tidal volumes in pressure-controlled modes. 5) Elevated airway pressures in volume-controlled modes. Management: large-volume paracentesis (removing 5-10 L with albumin replacement) typically produces immediate improvement in respiratory mechanics. For refractory ascites, TIPS may be considered. Monitoring: IAP measurement (via bladder pressure transducer) — IAP >20 mmHg with new organ dysfunction defines abdominal compartment syndrome.

stem: A patient with recurrent pulmonary emboli despite therapeutic anticoagulation undergoes IVC filter placement. What is the current recommendation regarding retrievable vs permanent IVC filters?
options: Retrievable filters are preferred and should be removed once the indication for filtering has resolved and anticoagulation can be resumed; permanent filters are reserved for patients with absolute long-term contraindication to anticoagulation
All IVC filters should remain permanently
IVC filters are the first-line treatment for all PE
IVC filters prevent all future PE with no complications
rationale: IVC filter indications: 1) Absolute contraindication to anticoagulation in a patient with acute VTE. 2) Recurrent VTE despite adequate anticoagulation. 3) Complications of anticoagulation requiring discontinuation. Retrievable filters are strongly preferred because permanent filters carry long-term risks: filter fracture, IVC thrombosis/occlusion (up to 30%), filter migration, and IVC penetration. The FDA recommends retrieval within 25-54 days when the indication resolves. Despite this, retrieval rates remain low (~30%). IVC filters DO NOT treat PE and do not reduce PE mortality — anticoagulation remains the primary treatment. Filters are a bridge, not a substitute for anticoagulation.

stem: A patient with known COPD is admitted with influenza B pneumonia. The respiratory therapist is managing the patient on BiPAP. The patient develops worsening respiratory failure and requires intubation. What ventilator settings should be initiated considering both the underlying COPD and the acute pneumonia?
options: VT 6-8 mL/kg IBW, RR 14-18 (lower to allow expiratory time for COPD), PEEP 5-8 cmH2O (balance recruitment needs vs. air trapping risk), FiO2 to target SpO2 88-92%, and I:E ratio 1:3 to allow adequate exhalation
VT 12 mL/kg, RR 24, PEEP 0, FiO2 1.0
VT 4 mL/kg, RR 30, PEEP 20, FiO2 0.21
No specific ventilator strategy is needed — standard settings work for all patients
rationale: Managing COPD with acute pneumonia requires balancing competing priorities: lung-protective ventilation for pneumonia/potential ARDS (low VT, moderate PEEP) while preventing air trapping from COPD (adequate expiratory time, cautious PEEP). Settings: VT 6-8 mL/kg IBW (lung-protective), moderate respiratory rate 14-18 (fast enough for adequate minute ventilation, slow enough for complete exhalation), PEEP 5-8 cmH2O (balance alveolar recruitment from pneumonia with risk of worsening auto-PEEP from COPD), I:E ratio 1:3 or longer, and FiO2 titrated to SpO2 88-92% (avoid hyperoxia in chronic CO2 retainers). Monitor closely for auto-PEEP using expiratory hold maneuvers.

stem: A patient with pulmonary alveolar proteinosis (PAP) presents with progressive dyspnea and bilateral 'crazy-paving' pattern on HRCT. The PaO2 is 55 mmHg on room air. What is the definitive treatment for PAP?
options: Whole lung lavage (WLL) — sequential bilateral bronchoalveolar lavage with large volumes of warm saline to physically remove the accumulated surfactant material
High-dose systemic corticosteroids
Inhaled bronchodilators
Lung transplantation as first-line therapy
rationale: Pulmonary alveolar proteinosis is characterized by accumulation of surfactant-derived phospholipoproteins in alveoli due to impaired alveolar macrophage clearance (most commonly from anti-GM-CSF autoantibodies). Whole lung lavage (WLL) is the gold standard treatment: performed under general anesthesia with a DLT, one lung is ventilated while the other is sequentially filled with warm saline (1 L aliquots, total 15-40 L per lung) and drained, physically washing out the proteinaceous material. WLL improves gas exchange, symptoms, and CT findings. Newer treatments include inhaled GM-CSF (rhGM-CSF) for autoimmune PAP, which addresses the underlying pathophysiology.

stem: A patient with acute exacerbation of COPD has been on BiPAP for 4 hours. Despite IPAP 20 cmH2O and EPAP 6 cmH2O, the pH remains 7.25 with PaCO2 72 mmHg. The patient is drowsy but arousable, with poor mask seal due to agitation. What is the most appropriate decision?
options: Proceed to intubation and mechanical ventilation — NIV has failed after adequate trial with optimal settings and the patient shows deteriorating mental status
Continue NIV for another 12 hours
Switch to HFNC as a step-up from BiPAP
Remove NIV and place on nasal cannula
rationale: NIV failure criteria in COPD exacerbation include: 1) No improvement or worsening of pH/PaCO2 after 1-2 hours of optimized NIV. 2) Deteriorating mental status (drowsiness indicates worsening CO2 narcosis). 3) Inability to maintain adequate mask seal. 4) Inability to manage secretions. 5) Hemodynamic instability. This patient meets multiple failure criteria: persistent severe acidosis (pH 7.25) despite maximal IPAP, drowsiness (impending CO2 narcosis), and poor mask seal. Delayed intubation after NIV failure is associated with increased mortality. HFNC does not provide ventilatory support adequate for hypercapnic failure. The decision to intubate should not be delayed when clear failure criteria are met.

stem: A ventilated patient's flow-time waveform shows a brief reversal of flow direction at the end of inspiration before exhalation begins. This is noted consistently on the flow waveform. What phenomenon does this represent?
options: Pendelluft — redistribution of gas between lung units with different time constants at end-inspiration, where fast-filling units pressurize slower-filling units
Normal end-inspiratory pause flow
Auto-triggering by the ventilator
Circuit leak detected by the flow sensor
rationale: Pendelluft is the movement of gas between lung units with different time constants (compliance × resistance) during both inspiration and expiration. At end-inspiration, 'fast' lung units (short time constant, already filled) have higher pressure than 'slow' units (long time constant, still filling), causing gas to flow from fast to slow units. This appears as a brief flow reversal on the flow waveform. Pendelluft is clinically significant because it causes: 1) Overdistension of dependent lung regions during spontaneous breathing in ARDS. 2) Regional redistribution of tidal volume not reflected in circuit measurements. 3) May contribute to P-SILI. It can be detected by electrical impedance tomography (EIT) showing delayed regional filling.

stem: A patient with severe acute asthma has a pH of 7.15 and PaCO2 of 80 mmHg despite aggressive bronchodilator therapy. The decision is made to intubate. Which induction agent should be AVOIDED in this patient?
options: Propofol should be used with caution due to potential bronchospasm in some patients; however, the more important concern is avoiding histamine-releasing agents like morphine and using ketamine as the preferred induction agent for its bronchodilatory properties
Ketamine should be avoided due to bronchospasm risk
All induction agents are equivalent in asthma
Rocuronium should be avoided due to bronchospasm
rationale: When intubating severe asthma patients, drug selection is critical: PREFERRED agents: ketamine (bronchodilator via catecholamine release and direct smooth muscle relaxation — the ideal induction agent for asthma), rocuronium (non-depolarizing NMB, no histamine release), and sevoflurane (bronchodilator properties if available). AVOID: histamine-releasing agents — morphine, atracurium, mivacurium, and thiopental can trigger bronchospasm and worsen the crisis. Propofol has mild bronchodilatory properties but can cause significant hypotension (problematic in already hemodynamically compromised patients with air trapping). Succinylcholine is acceptable for RSI in asthma (no hyperkalemia risk) but rocuronium with sugammadex reversal capability is increasingly preferred.

stem: A patient on prolonged mechanical ventilation develops tracheoinnominate artery fistula (TIF) — massive hemorrhage from the tracheostomy site. What is the immediate life-saving maneuver?
options: Hyperinflate the tracheostomy cuff and apply digital pressure anteriorly through the stoma against the innominate artery (Utley maneuver) to tamponade the bleeding while preparing for emergent surgical repair
Apply external pressure to the neck
Remove the tracheostomy tube immediately
Increase PEEP to tamponade the bleeding
rationale: Tracheoinnominate artery fistula (TIF) is a rare (<1%) but often fatal complication of tracheostomy, usually occurring 3 days to 6 weeks post-placement. The innominate (brachiocephalic) artery crosses the trachea at the level of the 9th tracheal ring — low tracheostomy placement, high cuff pressure erosion, or tube tip erosion can create a fistula. Immediate management: 1) Hyperinflate the tracheostomy cuff (tamponade). 2) If bleeding continues, insert a finger through the stoma and apply anterior pressure against the posterior surface of the sternum (Utley maneuver), compressing the innominate artery between the finger and sternum. 3) Emergent surgical repair (median sternotomy, innominate artery ligation or repair). Mortality exceeds 75% even with surgery.

stem: A respiratory therapist is calculating the static compliance, dynamic compliance, and airway resistance for a ventilated patient with the following data: VT 500 mL, PIP 40 cmH2O, Pplat 25 cmH2O, PEEP 5 cmH2O, inspiratory flow 60 L/min (1 L/s). What are the calculated values?
options: Static compliance = 25 mL/cmH2O, dynamic compliance = 14.3 mL/cmH2O, airway resistance = 15 cmH2O/L/s
Static compliance = 50 mL/cmH2O, dynamic compliance = 25 mL/cmH2O, airway resistance = 5 cmH2O/L/s
Static compliance = 14.3 mL/cmH2O, dynamic compliance = 25 mL/cmH2O, airway resistance = 25 cmH2O/L/s
Cannot be calculated without knowing FiO2
rationale: Static compliance (Cstat) = VT / (Pplat - PEEP) = 500 / (25 - 5) = 500/20 = 25 mL/cmH2O (normal 60-100; reduced — suggests decreased lung/chest wall compliance). Dynamic compliance (Cdyn) = VT / (PIP - PEEP) = 500 / (40 - 5) = 500/35 = 14.3 mL/cmH2O (lower than static because it includes resistive component). Airway resistance (Raw) = (PIP - Pplat) / Flow = (40 - 25) / 1 = 15 cmH2O/L/s (normal 0.6-2.4; markedly elevated — suggests significant airway obstruction, secretions, or kinked tube). The large PIP-Pplat gradient (15 cmH2O) indicates that airway resistance is a major contributor to the elevated PIP. Both compliance and resistance issues need to be addressed.

stem: A patient with acute hypercapnic respiratory failure from an opioid overdose has ABG: pH 7.15, PaCO2 85 mmHg, HCO3 28 mEq/L, PaO2 45 mmHg on room air. After naloxone administration, the patient's respiratory rate increases from 6 to 18 breaths/min and ABG improves to pH 7.35, PaCO2 45 mmHg. What type of respiratory failure was present and how was it corrected?
options: Type II (hypercapnic) respiratory failure from hypoventilation — naloxone reversed the opioid-mediated respiratory depression, restoring ventilatory drive and normalizing PaCO2
Type I (hypoxemic) respiratory failure from pneumonia
Combined type I and type II from ARDS
The improvement was coincidental and unrelated to naloxone
rationale: This patient had type II (hypercapnic/ventilatory) respiratory failure caused by opioid-induced central respiratory depression. Key features: severely elevated PaCO2, low respiratory rate (6/min), and a normal A-a gradient (the lung itself was normal — hypoventilation alone caused the hypoxemia and hypercapnia). Naloxone (opioid antagonist, 0.4-2 mg IV) competitively displaces opioids from µ-receptors in the brainstem respiratory center, restoring ventilatory drive. The rapid normalization of PaCO2 and pH confirms that the acid-base disturbance was purely respiratory with no time for renal compensation (HCO3 28 may reflect early buffering). Patients must be monitored for re-narcotization since naloxone's half-life (60-90 min) may be shorter than the offending opioid.

stem: A patient is transferred from the OR after coronary artery bypass surgery. The patient is intubated and on mechanical ventilation. Initial settings are VT 500 mL, RR 12, FiO2 0.50, PEEP 5 cmH2O. The patient is hypothermic (34°C), hemodynamically labile, and has chest tubes with bloody output. What monitoring priorities should the respiratory therapist focus on?
options: Chest tube output for hemorrhage, rewarming effects on ventilation and hemodynamics, ABG monitoring as temperature normalizes, assessment for pneumothorax/hemothorax, and airway security during shivering
Only SpO2 monitoring is needed
FiO2 adjustment is the only concern
No specific monitoring is needed — standard ICU care is sufficient
rationale: Post-cardiac surgery respiratory management priorities: 1) Chest tube monitoring — bloody output >200 mL/hr suggests surgical bleeding requiring re-exploration; air leak suggests pneumothorax. 2) Rewarming — as the patient warms from 34°C to 37°C, CO2 production and oxygen consumption increase; ventilation may need to be increased; vasodilation can cause hypotension. 3) ABG monitoring — frequent ABGs during rewarming (temperature affects gas values); alpha-stat approach preferred. 4) CXR to assess ETT position, pneumothorax, hemothorax, and effusion. 5) Sternal precautions — avoid excessive suctioning force that could disrupt sternal wires. 6) Early extubation protocols — many cardiac surgery patients can be extubated within 4-8 hours ('fast-track' extubation) if hemodynamically stable, normothermic, and adequately responsive.

stem: A patient with suspected sleep-disordered breathing undergoes polysomnography. The study reveals an AHI of 28 events/hour with predominantly obstructive events, REM-related desaturations to 72%, and a mean sleep SpO2 of 89%. What additional findings on polysomnography distinguish obstructive from central sleep apnea?
options: Obstructive events show continued respiratory effort (chest/abdominal movement) against a closed upper airway, while central events show absence of both airflow AND respiratory effort
Obstructive and central events appear identical on PSG
Only EEG findings differentiate obstructive from central events
Heart rate patterns alone distinguish the two types
rationale: Polysomnography distinguishes apnea types by the presence or absence of respiratory effort (measured by chest/abdominal movement sensors — inductance plethysmography or RIP belts): Obstructive apnea: cessation of airflow ≥10 seconds WITH continued (often increasing) respiratory effort — the patient is trying to breathe but the upper airway is collapsed. Paradoxical chest-abdominal movement is characteristic. Central apnea: cessation of both airflow AND respiratory effort ≥10 seconds — the brain is not sending signals to breathe. Mixed apnea: begins as central (no effort) then becomes obstructive (effort against closed airway). Additional findings: obstructive events often have preceding crescendo snoring, are worse in supine/REM, and show oxyhemoglobin desaturation-reoxygenation cycles.

stem: A ventilated patient on day 5 of ARDS treatment shows improving oxygenation. The team decides to perform a decremental PEEP trial. Starting from PEEP 20 cmH2O (after recruitment maneuver), compliance is measured at each level. At PEEP 14, compliance peaks at 32 mL/cmH2O. At PEEP 12, compliance drops to 26 mL/cmH2O. What is the optimal PEEP based on this finding?
options: Set PEEP at 16 cmH2O (2 cmH2O above the best compliance point of 14), then perform another brief recruitment maneuver to reopen any derecruitment that occurred during the trial
Set PEEP at 12 cmH2O since compliance was still acceptable
Set PEEP at 20 cmH2O — the starting level
PEEP of 0 is optimal since no PEEP allows maximum lung compliance
rationale: The decremental PEEP trial identifies optimal PEEP by finding the level of maximum compliance (best balance between recruitment and overdistension). When compliance drops at PEEP 12 (from 32 to 26 mL/cmH2O), it indicates that derecruitment has begun — alveoli are collapsing. The optimal PEEP is set 2 cmH2O above the best compliance point (14 + 2 = 16 cmH2O) to provide a safety margin against derecruitment. After the trial, a recruitment maneuver must be performed because the decremental process itself causes some derecruitment — the optimal PEEP is then set to maintain the recruited state. This individualized approach may provide better PEEP than protocol-based FiO2/PEEP tables.

stem: A patient with late-stage COPD has been referred for lung transplant evaluation. What are the specific COPD-related criteria for listing a patient for lung transplantation?
options: BODE index 7-10, FEV1 <25% predicted, hospitalization for exacerbation with acute hypercapnia (PaCO2 >50 mmHg), or pulmonary hypertension/cor pulmonale despite oxygen therapy
Any COPD patient with FEV1 <80% predicted
Age >70 with mild COPD
COPD patients cannot receive lung transplants
rationale: ISHLT guidelines for lung transplant referral in COPD: BODE index 5-6 (referral) or 7-10 (listing); FEV1 <25% predicted; history of hospitalization for exacerbation with acute hypercapnia (PaCO2 >50); pulmonary hypertension or cor pulmonale despite optimal therapy; or FEV1 <20% with DLCO <20% or homogeneous emphysema (same criteria that predicted high mortality in NETT trial). The BODE index (BMI, Obstruction [FEV1], Dyspnea [mMRC], Exercise capacity [6MWD]) provides better prognostic information than FEV1 alone. Contraindications include active malignancy, significant extrapulmonary organ dysfunction, non-adherence, and active substance abuse. Bilateral sequential lung transplant is preferred for COPD.

stem: A patient with acute respiratory failure from myasthenic crisis is being managed with BiPAP. The patient's vital capacity is declining from 1.5 L to 1.0 L over 2 hours. MIP has dropped to -15 cmH2O. Despite BiPAP at IPAP 18/EPAP 6, the patient is increasingly drowsy. What is the most appropriate action?
options: Proceed to elective intubation — rapidly declining respiratory muscle function with impending ventilatory failure warrants definitive airway management before emergency decompensation
Continue BiPAP and increase IPAP to 25 cmH2O
Switch to HFNC and monitor
Administer sedation to reduce anxiety
rationale: This patient demonstrates clear signs of impending ventilatory failure: rapidly declining VC (1.5 → 1.0 L in 2 hours, now below the 20 mL/kg threshold if the patient weighs >50 kg), critically low MIP (-15, far beyond the -30 threshold), and progressive drowsiness despite NIV (indicating rising PaCO2/CO2 narcosis). Elective intubation in a controlled setting is far safer than emergency intubation during acute decompensation. Key principle: in progressive neuromuscular respiratory failure, intubate EARLY while the patient is still cooperative and before crisis. Use of succinylcholine is CONTRAINDICATED (risk of hyperkalemia). Use rocuronium for RSI. Contact neurology for plasma exchange or IVIG initiation.

stem: A patient presents with an acute exacerbation of bronchiectasis. Sputum culture grows Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC). CT shows tree-in-bud nodularity in the right middle lobe and lingula. What is the recommended treatment for pulmonary MAC disease?
options: Triple therapy with a macrolide (azithromycin or clarithromycin), ethambutol, and a rifamycin (rifampin) for a minimum of 12 months after sputum culture conversion
Isoniazid monotherapy for 6 months
No treatment is necessary for MAC isolation
Standard RIPE therapy identical to M. tuberculosis
rationale: Pulmonary MAC disease requires multidrug therapy (ATS/IDSA guidelines): macrolide (azithromycin 250 mg daily or clarithromycin 500 mg BID) + ethambutol 15 mg/kg + rifampin 600 mg daily (or rifabutin if drug interactions). Duration: minimum 12 months after sputum culture conversion. The nodular/bronchiectatic form (right middle lobe/lingula — 'Lady Windermere syndrome') may be treated with intermittent (3×/week) dosing. The fibrocavitary form requires daily dosing and may need an injectable aminoglycoside. MAC treatment differs significantly from TB treatment — isoniazid and pyrazinamide are NOT effective against MAC. Treatment is prolonged, with culture conversion rates of 60-75%.

stem: A patient with severe COPD (FEV1 28% predicted) is being treated with triple inhaler therapy: LABA/LAMA/ICS combination. Despite adherence, the patient continues to have frequent exacerbations. Blood eosinophils are 350 cells/µL. What add-on therapy should be considered?
options: Add a biologic targeting type 2 inflammation (e.g., dupilumab or mepolizumab) given the elevated eosinophil count and frequent exacerbations despite maximal inhaled therapy
Add a short-acting beta-agonist as maintenance therapy
Switch to LABA monotherapy
Discontinue all inhaled therapy
rationale: For COPD patients with frequent exacerbations despite triple therapy (LABA/LAMA/ICS) and evidence of eosinophilic inflammation (blood eos ≥300), biologic therapy targeting type 2 inflammation is an emerging option. The METREX and METREO trials showed mepolizumab (anti-IL-5) reduced exacerbations in eosinophilic COPD. Dupilumab (anti-IL-4Rα) showed significant exacerbation reduction and FEV1 improvement in the BOREAS trial. Other add-on options include: roflumilast (PDE-4 inhibitor for chronic bronchitis phenotype), azithromycin prophylaxis (250 mg daily), and pulmonary rehabilitation. Blood eosinophil count ≥300 cells/µL predicts response to eosinophil-targeting therapies.

stem: A ventilated patient with severe ARDS is receiving prone positioning. After 18 hours in the prone position, the patient is returned to supine for nursing care. Within 2 hours of supination, PaO2 drops from 85 to 55 mmHg on FiO2 0.60 and PEEP 14 cmH2O. What explains this rapid deoxygenation and what should be done?
options: Loss of prone-position recruitment causes rapid derecruitment of dorsal lung units in supine position — return to prone positioning and consider extending prone sessions or performing a recruitment maneuver before supination
The patient has developed a new pneumothorax
Deoxygenation is unrelated to position change
The patient no longer benefits from prone positioning
rationale: Rapid desaturation after returning to supine from prone position indicates position-dependent recruitment/derecruitment. In prone position, gravitational redistribution of perfusion to anterior regions (where ventilation improves) and recruitment of dorsal consolidated lung improves V/Q matching. Returning to supine allows dorsal units to collapse again under gravitational forces, causing rapid derecruitment and increased shunt. Management: 1) Return to prone position promptly. 2) Extend prone sessions (≥16 hours, some centers use >20 hours). 3) Perform a recruitment maneuver after supination before prone-back transition. 4) The need for prolonged prone positioning with position-dependent oxygenation indicates ongoing significant ARDS. 5) Prone positioning should continue until P/F >150 on FiO2 ≤0.60 and PEEP ≤10 in supine position for sustained (>4 hours) periods.

stem: A patient with a right-sided chest tube connected to a water-seal drainage system shows continuous bubbling in the water-seal chamber. What does this indicate?
options: An active air leak — either from a bronchopleural fistula (if the lung does not re-expand) or from the chest tube system (connection leaks should be checked first)
Normal functioning of the chest drainage system
The chest tube is blocked
The suction is set too low
rationale: Continuous bubbling in the water seal chamber indicates air is passing through the system. Sources include: 1) Bronchopleural fistula — air from the lung parenchyma (most concerning). 2) Pneumothorax that has not yet resolved. 3) System air leak — check all connections, insertion site, and tubing for loose fittings (systematically clamp from the patient outward to localize). Normal post-insertion bubbling should decrease over 24-48 hours as the pneumothorax resolves. Persistent bubbling >5-7 days suggests ongoing air leak requiring evaluation. The tidaling (respiratory oscillation) in the water-seal chamber should continue — absence of tidaling suggests tube obstruction or complete lung re-expansion. Never clamp a chest tube with an active air leak for transport.

stem: A patient with chronic hypercapnic respiratory failure from kyphoscoliosis is being set up for nocturnal BiPAP. Initial daytime ABG on room air: pH 7.34, PaCO2 58 mmHg, HCO3 30 mEq/L, PaO2 62 mmHg. What BiPAP settings and targets are appropriate?
options: IPAP 16-20 cmH2O, EPAP 4-6 cmH2O, backup rate 14-16; target a reduction in PaCO2 of at least 5-8 mmHg on morning ABG while maintaining comfort and adherence
CPAP 5 cmH2O is sufficient for chest wall disease
IPAP 8, EPAP 4 with no backup rate
Only supplemental oxygen without positive pressure is needed
rationale: Kyphoscoliosis causes restrictive ventilatory failure from reduced chest wall compliance. NIV (BiPAP) for chronic hypercapnic respiratory failure: IPAP 16-20 cmH2O (or higher — targeting 8-14 cmH2O pressure support to generate adequate VT despite the stiff chest wall), EPAP 4-6 cmH2O, backup rate 14-16 (patients with restrictive disease often need higher backup rates due to their physiologically rapid shallow breathing pattern). Targets: morning PaCO2 reduction of ≥5-8 mmHg, improved nocturnal SpO2, resolution of symptoms (morning headache, daytime somnolence). Average volume-assured pressure support (AVAPS/iVAPS) modes may be particularly useful as they auto-adjust IPAP to maintain a target VT. Supplemental O2 via the NIV circuit if SpO2 remains <88% on air.

stem: A patient on long-term invasive mechanical ventilation via tracheostomy is being transitioned to a portable ventilator for discharge home. What are the essential components of a home mechanical ventilation plan?
options: Trained caregivers (24/7), backup ventilator and power source, emergency bag-valve, suction equipment, pulse oximetry, tracheostomy supplies, and an emergency action plan with local EMS notification
The patient needs only a portable ventilator — no other equipment is required
Home ventilation is not possible — all ventilated patients must remain in facilities
Only overnight nursing care is needed for home ventilation
rationale: Home mechanical ventilation requires comprehensive planning: 1) Equipment: primary and backup ventilator, backup power (battery, generator), self-inflating resuscitation bag, portable suction machine with supplies, pulse oximeter, tracheostomy supplies (spare trach tubes, obturator, ties). 2) Caregivers: minimum two trained caregivers for 24/7 coverage; trained in ventilator operation, tracheostomy care, suctioning, emergency procedures, and BLS/CPR. 3) Safety: notify local power company (priority reconnection), notify EMS of ventilator-dependent patient address, develop and rehearse emergency plan. 4) Follow-up: regular respiratory therapy visits, sleep studies if needed, ventilator setting adjustments, equipment maintenance. 5) Home assessment: adequate electrical capacity, clean environment, accessibility. Insurance authorization and durable medical equipment (DME) company coordination are essential.

stem: A patient with acute respiratory failure from COVID-19 pneumonia has been placed on awake prone positioning while receiving HFNC at 60 L/min and FiO2 0.60. After 2 hours of prone positioning, SpO2 improves from 88% to 95%. What is the evidence supporting awake prone positioning?
options: Multiple trials showed awake proning in non-intubated patients reduces the need for intubation and may improve outcomes, with the greatest benefit in patients requiring high FiO2 who can maintain proning for extended periods
Awake proning has no evidence base and is only anecdotal
Awake proning is harmful and increases intubation rates
Awake proning only works in intubated patients
rationale: Awake prone positioning gained prominence during the COVID-19 pandemic. The meta-trial (BMJ 2021 meta-analysis of 6 RCTs) showed: 1) Reduced intubation rate in hypoxemic patients (RR 0.85). 2) Greatest benefit in patients with higher oxygen requirements. 3) Duration matters — more hours/day of proning correlated with better outcomes. 4) Safe and well-tolerated in most cooperative patients. Mechanisms are similar to prone positioning in intubated patients: improved V/Q matching, lung recruitment, and reduced P-SILI by homogenizing pleural pressure. Criteria: cooperative patient, able to self-position, SpO2 <94% on ≥4 L O2 or equivalent. Monitoring: SpO2, respiratory rate, comfort. Failure criteria: inability to maintain proning, worsening respiratory status requiring intubation.

stem: A ventilated patient develops refeeding syndrome after initiation of enteral nutrition following prolonged starvation. ABG shows pH 7.48, PaCO2 32 mmHg, HCO3 23 mEq/L. Phosphate is critically low at 0.8 mg/dL. How does severe hypophosphatemia affect respiratory function?
options: Hypophosphatemia causes respiratory muscle weakness by depleting ATP and 2,3-DPG, potentially causing ventilator dependence and failure to wean
Phosphate levels have no effect on respiratory function
Hypophosphatemia causes bronchospasm
Only hyperphosphatemia affects respiratory muscles
rationale: Severe hypophosphatemia (<1.0 mg/dL) profoundly affects respiratory function through: 1) Diaphragmatic weakness — phosphate is essential for ATP synthesis, and ATP depletion impairs muscle contraction. 2) Decreased 2,3-DPG — shifts the O-Hb dissociation curve leftward, reducing oxygen unloading to tissues. 3) Impaired oxygen delivery at the cellular level. 4) Can cause acute respiratory failure in spontaneously breathing patients and prevent weaning from mechanical ventilation. Refeeding syndrome occurs when malnourished patients receive nutrition (carbohydrate loading stimulates insulin, which drives phosphate, potassium, and magnesium intracellularly). Treatment: IV potassium phosphate replacement with cardiac monitoring. Prevention: start nutrition at 25% of goal, supplement phosphate/K+/Mg2+ prophylactically, and monitor electrolytes every 6-12 hours during refeeding.

stem: A patient with bronchial asthma has a peak expiratory flow (PEF) of 180 L/min. The patient's predicted PEF is 450 L/min. What is the percentage of predicted PEF and what asthma zone does this represent?
options: PEF is 40% of predicted — this is in the red zone (<50% predicted), indicating a severe exacerbation requiring immediate medical attention
PEF is 80% of predicted — green zone (well controlled)
PEF is 60% of predicted — yellow zone (caution)
PEF cannot be compared to predicted values
rationale: PEF % predicted = (180/450) × 100 = 40%. The asthma action plan traffic light system: Green zone (>80% predicted): well controlled, continue maintenance medications. Yellow zone (50-80%): caution, asthma worsening, initiate rescue medications and consider increasing controller therapy. Red zone (<50%): medical alert — severe exacerbation requiring immediate bronchodilator use, systemic corticosteroids, and emergency department evaluation. This patient's 40% PEF indicates severe airflow obstruction requiring aggressive treatment: continuous nebulized albuterol, systemic corticosteroids, ipratropium, consider IV magnesium sulfate, and close monitoring for further deterioration.

stem: A ventilated patient with traumatic brain injury has ICP monitoring showing intermittent elevations to 28 mmHg (normal <20). The neurosurgeon requests brief hyperventilation. The respiratory therapist adjusts the ventilator to decrease PaCO2 from 40 to 30 mmHg. What cerebral physiologic changes will result?
options: Cerebral vasoconstriction from hypocapnia reduces cerebral blood volume, lowering ICP by approximately 25-30%; this effect peaks within minutes but wanes over 6-12 hours due to CSF pH normalization
No change in ICP occurs with hyperventilation
Cerebral vasodilation occurs, increasing ICP
The effect of hyperventilation on ICP lasts permanently
rationale: CO2 is a potent cerebral vasodilator. Reducing PaCO2 by 10 mmHg (40→30) causes cerebral vasoconstriction, reducing cerebral blood flow by approximately 30% and cerebral blood volume (CBV), thereby lowering ICP. Each 1 mmHg decrease in PaCO2 reduces CBF by ~3%. This effect is rapid (minutes) but temporary — over 6-12 hours, the CSF pH normalizes through bicarbonate shifts, and the vasoconstrictive effect wanes. IMPORTANT: aggressive hyperventilation (PaCO2 <25) can cause dangerous cerebral ischemia in already injured brain tissue and is no longer recommended as routine prophylaxis. Brief, targeted hyperventilation is reserved as a temporary measure for acute ICP crises while definitive ICP management (osmotherapy, CSF drainage, surgery) is initiated.

stem: A patient with severe burn injuries (60% TBSA, including inhalation injury) is intubated and on mechanical ventilation. Initial settings are FiO2 1.0, VT 6 mL/kg IBW, RR 20, PEEP 8 cmH2O. What specific respiratory considerations apply to this burn patient?
options: Progressive airway edema may require upsizing ETT, massive fluid resuscitation increases pulmonary edema risk, carbon monoxide and cyanide assessment are critical, and chest wall compliance may decrease with circumferential burns requiring escharotomy
Standard ventilator settings without modification are appropriate
Burn patients do not develop respiratory complications
FiO2 should be kept at 0.21 to prevent oxygen toxicity to burned tissue
rationale: Severe burn patients have unique respiratory challenges: 1) Airway edema — mucosal swelling progresses over 24-48 hours (may need larger ETT or early tracheostomy; do NOT extubate prematurely). 2) Inhalation injury — upper airway thermal injury + lower airway chemical injury + systemic toxicity (CO/HCN). 3) CO poisoning — maintain FiO2 1.0 until COHb <10% (CO half-life: room air ~320 min, 100% O2 ~80 min, HBO ~23 min). 4) Cyanide — consider hydroxocobalamin (Cyanokit) empirically in house fire victims with altered mental status and lactic acidosis. 5) Massive fluid resuscitation (Parkland formula) causes significant pulmonary edema — higher PEEP may be needed. 6) Circumferential chest burns restrict chest wall expansion — surgical escharotomy restores compliance. 7) Hypermetabolic state increases CO2 production, requiring increased minute ventilation.

stem: A patient with COPD is being assessed with the CAT (COPD Assessment Test) score and mMRC dyspnea scale. The CAT score is 22 and mMRC is 3. According to the GOLD 2024 report, what group classification does this patient belong to, and what initial pharmacotherapy is recommended?
options: Group E (exacerbator) if ≥2 moderate or ≥1 severe exacerbation in the past year, or Group B (more symptoms) — LABA/LAMA combination is the preferred initial therapy for both
Group A — SABA as needed only
No pharmacotherapy is needed based on these scores
Group C — ICS monotherapy
rationale: The updated GOLD 2024 classification uses the ABE grouping: Group A (CAT <10, mMRC 0-1, ≤1 moderate exacerbation); Group B (CAT ≥10 or mMRC ≥2, ≤1 moderate exacerbation); Group E (≥2 moderate or ≥1 severe exacerbation requiring hospitalization). This patient's CAT 22 and mMRC 3 indicate significant symptoms. If ≤1 moderate exacerbation → Group B (LABA/LAMA preferred). If ≥2 moderate or ≥1 severe → Group E (LABA/LAMA preferred; consider LABA/LAMA/ICS if blood eosinophils ≥300). Initial therapy for both B and E: LABA/LAMA combination (e.g., umeclidinium/vilanterol, tiotropium/olodaterol). ICS-containing regimens are added based on eosinophil count and exacerbation frequency.

stem: A patient with suspected PE undergoes CT pulmonary angiography which is negative. However, clinical suspicion remains high with a Wells score of 8 (PE likely). The D-dimer is 4500 ng/mL. What is the next diagnostic step?
options: V/Q scan or pulmonary angiography — CTPA has a sensitivity of 83-100% and can miss subsegmental PE; V/Q scan or conventional pulmonary angiography may detect emboli missed by CTPA
No further workup is needed if CTPA is negative
Repeat D-dimer in 48 hours
Start anticoagulation based on D-dimer alone
rationale: When CTPA is negative but clinical suspicion is high (Wells score >6, elevated D-dimer), additional testing is warranted because CTPA sensitivity is not 100%, particularly for subsegmental PE. Options: 1) V/Q scan — may reveal perfusion defects in areas not well-visualized on CTPA. 2) Lower extremity venous duplex ultrasound — if DVT is found, anticoagulation is indicated regardless. 3) Conventional pulmonary angiography — remains the gold standard but is rarely used due to invasiveness. 4) Consider CT venography (performed with CTPA) or MR pulmonary angiography. Clinical assessment tools should also be revisited — a Wells score of 8 gives >50% pre-test probability, making a negative CTPA less reliable for ruling out PE. Anticoagulation may be empirically continued while additional testing is performed.

stem: What is the normal range for arterial blood pH?
rationale: Normal arterial blood pH ranges from 7.35 to 7.45. A pH below 7.35 indicates acidemia, while a pH above 7.45 indicates alkalemia. This is one of the most fundamental values in ABG interpretation.

stem: What is the normal range for PaCO2 on an arterial blood gas?
options: 35–45 mmHg
25–35 mmHg
45–55 mmHg
20–30 mmHg
rationale: The normal PaCO2 range is 35–45 mmHg. PaCO2 reflects the respiratory component of acid-base balance. Values above 45 mmHg indicate respiratory acidosis (hypoventilation), while values below 35 mmHg indicate respiratory alkalosis (hyperventilation).

stem: What is the normal range for arterial bicarbonate (HCO3)?
options: 22–26 mEq/L
18–22 mEq/L
26–30 mEq/L
15–20 mEq/L
rationale: Normal arterial HCO3 is 22–26 mEq/L. Bicarbonate represents the metabolic component of acid-base balance. Values below 22 suggest metabolic acidosis, while values above 26 suggest metabolic alkalosis.

stem: An ABG shows pH 7.48, PaCO2 30 mmHg, HCO3 24 mEq/L. What acid-base disturbance is present?
options: Respiratory alkalosis — the elevated pH and low PaCO2 with normal HCO3 indicate uncompensated respiratory alkalosis
Metabolic alkalosis
Respiratory acidosis
Compensated metabolic acidosis
rationale: pH >7.45 = alkalemia. PaCO2 30 mmHg is below normal (35–45), indicating hyperventilation. HCO3 24 is normal (22–26), meaning there is no metabolic compensation yet. This is acute (uncompensated) respiratory alkalosis. Common causes include anxiety, pain, hypoxemia, and central nervous system disorders.

stem: An ABG shows pH 7.30, PaCO2 55 mmHg, HCO3 26 mEq/L. What is the primary acid-base disorder?
options: Acute respiratory acidosis — elevated PaCO2 with minimally elevated HCO3 and acidemic pH
Chronic respiratory acidosis
Metabolic acidosis
Mixed respiratory and metabolic acidosis
rationale: pH 7.30 = acidemia. PaCO2 55 mmHg is elevated, indicating CO2 retention (respiratory acidosis). HCO3 26 is at the upper limit of normal — in acute respiratory acidosis, HCO3 rises only 1 mEq/L for every 10 mmHg rise in PaCO2 (expected HCO3 ~25). In chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 would rise 3.5 mEq/L per 10 mmHg (expected ~27.5). The significantly acidemic pH and minimal HCO3 rise suggest acute respiratory acidosis.

stem: Which ABG finding is most consistent with metabolic acidosis?
options: Low pH, low HCO3, low PaCO2
Low pH, high PaCO2, normal HCO3
High pH, low PaCO2, high HCO3
High pH, high HCO3, normal PaCO2
rationale: Metabolic acidosis is characterized by a primary decrease in HCO3, causing a low pH. The body compensates through hyperventilation (Kussmaul breathing), lowering PaCO2. The expected respiratory compensation can be calculated using Winter's formula: expected PaCO2 = (1.5 × HCO3) + 8 ± 2.

stem: An ABG shows pH 7.50, PaCO2 40 mmHg, HCO3 32 mEq/L. What is the acid-base disturbance?
options: Uncompensated metabolic alkalosis
Respiratory alkalosis
Compensated respiratory acidosis
Mixed alkalosis
rationale: pH 7.50 = alkalemia. PaCO2 is normal at 40 mmHg, ruling out a respiratory cause. HCO3 is elevated at 32 mEq/L, which is the primary metabolic disturbance. Since PaCO2 has not risen to compensate (expected compensation: PaCO2 rises 0.7 mmHg per 1 mEq/L rise in HCO3), this is uncompensated metabolic alkalosis. Common causes include vomiting, NG suction, and diuretic use.

stem: A patient with COPD has the following ABG: pH 7.37, PaCO2 58 mmHg, HCO3 33 mEq/L, PaO2 62 mmHg. How do you interpret this?
options: Compensated respiratory acidosis with mild hypoxemia — consistent with chronic CO2 retention in COPD
Normal ABG
Acute respiratory acidosis
Metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation
rationale: The pH is near normal (7.37), but PaCO2 is significantly elevated at 58 mmHg (chronic CO2 retention). The elevated HCO3 at 33 mEq/L represents renal compensation (kidneys retain HCO3 to buffer the chronic acidosis). In chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 increases ~3.5 mEq/L per 10 mmHg rise in PaCO2. Expected HCO3 = 24 + 3.5 × (58-40)/10 = 24 + 6.3 = ~30.3 mEq/L. The actual HCO3 of 33 is close, confirming adequate compensation. PaO2 of 62 shows mild hypoxemia typical of COPD.

stem: What formula is used to estimate expected PaCO2 compensation in metabolic acidosis?
options: Winter's formula: Expected PaCO2 = (1.5 × HCO3) + 8 ± 2
Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = 6.1 + log(HCO3/0.03 × PaCO2)
Alveolar gas equation: PAO2 = FiO2(PB - PH2O) - PaCO2/RQ
Bohr equation: VD/VT = (PaCO2 - PeCO2)/PaCO2
rationale: Winter's formula predicts the expected respiratory compensation for metabolic acidosis: PaCO2 = (1.5 × HCO3) + 8 ± 2. If measured PaCO2 is higher than predicted, there is a concurrent respiratory acidosis. If lower than predicted, there is a concurrent respiratory alkalosis. This formula only applies to metabolic acidosis, not metabolic alkalosis.

stem: An ABG shows pH 7.20, PaCO2 60 mmHg, HCO3 14 mEq/L. What acid-base disturbance is present?
options: Mixed respiratory and metabolic acidosis — both PaCO2 is elevated and HCO3 is decreased
Pure respiratory acidosis
Pure metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation
Compensated metabolic acidosis
rationale: pH 7.20 = severe acidemia. PaCO2 60 mmHg = respiratory acidosis. HCO3 14 mEq/L = metabolic acidosis. Both components are moving in the same direction (causing acidosis), which indicates a mixed disorder. In pure metabolic acidosis with HCO3 of 14, Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 = (1.5 × 14) + 8 ± 2 = 27-31 mmHg. The actual PaCO2 of 60 is far higher, confirming a concurrent respiratory acidosis. This combination is dangerous and seen in cardiopulmonary arrest, severe sepsis, or combined respiratory failure with renal failure.

stem: How is the anion gap calculated, and what is the normal range?
options: AG = Na+ − (Cl− + HCO3−); normal range is 8–12 mEq/L (or 10–14 with some labs)
AG = Na+ + K+ − (Cl− + HCO3−); normal 15–20 mEq/L
AG = Cl− − HCO3−; normal 5–10 mEq/L
AG = HCO3− + PaCO2; normal 40–50
rationale: The anion gap (AG) = Na+ − (Cl− + HCO3−). Normal AG is 8–12 mEq/L (some labs use 10–14). An elevated AG (>12) suggests the presence of unmeasured anions such as lactate, ketoacids, uremic toxins, or toxic alcohols. The mnemonic MUDPILES helps remember causes: Methanol, Uremia, DKA, Propylene glycol, Isoniazid/Iron, Lactic acidosis, Ethylene glycol, Salicylates.

stem: A patient's ABG shows pH 7.52, PaCO2 25 mmHg, HCO3 20 mEq/L. The patient is anxious and tachypneic. What is causing this acid-base disturbance?
options: Acute respiratory alkalosis from hyperventilation — anxiety is driving excessive CO2 elimination
Metabolic alkalosis from vomiting
Chronic respiratory alkalosis
Compensated metabolic acidosis
rationale: pH 7.52 = alkalemia. PaCO2 25 mmHg is very low (hyperventilation). HCO3 20 mEq/L is slightly below normal — in acute respiratory alkalosis, HCO3 drops ~2 mEq/L per 10 mmHg decrease in PaCO2. Expected: 24 − 2 × (40-25)/10 = 24 − 3 = 21, which is close to 20. The clinical picture of anxiety with tachypnea confirms hyperventilation syndrome as the cause. Treatment: reassurance, coaching to slow breathing, and possibly rebreathing into a paper bag (with caution).

stem: An ABG shows pH 7.38, PaCO2 22 mmHg, HCO3 13 mEq/L. What is the interpretation?
options: Compensated metabolic acidosis — the low HCO3 is the primary disorder with appropriate respiratory compensation
Normal ABG
Respiratory alkalosis with metabolic compensation
Mixed disorder
rationale: Although pH is near normal (7.38), both PaCO2 and HCO3 are significantly abnormal. HCO3 13 mEq/L is low (metabolic acidosis). PaCO2 22 mmHg is low (respiratory compensation through hyperventilation). Winter's formula: expected PaCO2 = (1.5 × 13) + 8 ± 2 = 25.5–29.5. The actual PaCO2 of 22 is slightly lower, suggesting slight concurrent respiratory alkalosis or slightly over-compensated. The near-normal pH with acidic tendency (7.38 < 7.40) suggests the primary disorder is metabolic acidosis with compensation. Full compensation rarely returns pH to exactly 7.40.

stem: What does a base excess (BE) of -8 mEq/L indicate on an ABG?
options: A metabolic acidosis — negative base excess (base deficit) indicates a deficit of buffer base in the blood
Respiratory acidosis
Metabolic alkalosis
Normal acid-base status
rationale: Base excess (BE) represents the amount of acid or base needed to restore blood pH to 7.40 at PaCO2 40 mmHg and 37°C. Normal BE is -2 to +2 mEq/L. A negative BE (base deficit) of -8 indicates metabolic acidosis — the blood has an excess of acid or deficit of base. A positive BE indicates metabolic alkalosis. BE is useful because it isolates the metabolic component independent of respiratory changes.

stem: A diabetic patient presents with ABG: pH 7.18, PaCO2 20 mmHg, HCO3 7 mEq/L, Na+ 140, Cl- 100, glucose 450 mg/dL. Calculate the anion gap and identify the disorder.
options: AG = 33 mEq/L — high anion gap metabolic acidosis due to diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA)
AG = 12 — normal anion gap metabolic acidosis
AG = 20 — respiratory acidosis
AG = 8 — no metabolic disorder
rationale: AG = Na+ − (Cl- + HCO3-) = 140 − (100 + 7) = 33 mEq/L. This is a severely elevated anion gap (normal 8–12), indicating high anion gap metabolic acidosis (HAGMA). Combined with severely elevated glucose (450 mg/dL), low pH, and low HCO3, this is classic DKA. The unmeasured anions are ketoacids (beta-hydroxybutyrate, acetoacetate). Winter's formula: expected PaCO2 = (1.5 × 7) + 8 ± 2 = 16.5–20.5. Actual PaCO2 of 20 falls within expected range, confirming appropriate respiratory compensation without a concurrent respiratory disorder.

stem: What is the delta-delta (Δ/Δ) ratio used for in ABG interpretation?
options: To identify concurrent non-anion gap metabolic acidosis or metabolic alkalosis hidden within a high anion gap metabolic acidosis
To calculate the respiratory compensation in metabolic alkalosis
To determine the A-a gradient
To assess oxygenation efficiency
rationale: The delta-delta ratio compares the change in anion gap (ΔAG = measured AG − 12) to the change in bicarbonate (ΔHCO3 = 24 − measured HCO3). Ratio interpretation: <1 = concurrent non-anion gap metabolic acidosis (HCO3 dropped more than expected from the AG elevation alone); 1-2 = pure high AG metabolic acidosis; >2 = concurrent metabolic alkalosis (HCO3 did not drop as much as expected). This is essential for identifying mixed metabolic disorders.

stem: A patient on mechanical ventilation has ABG: pH 7.28, PaCO2 65 mmHg, HCO3 30 mEq/L, PaO2 55 mmHg on FiO2 0.60. The A-a gradient is elevated. What is the clinical significance of the elevated A-a gradient?
options: It indicates the hypoxemia is due to a pulmonary cause (V/Q mismatch, shunt, or diffusion impairment) rather than pure hypoventilation
It confirms the hypoxemia is solely from hypoventilation
It indicates metabolic acidosis is the primary problem
A-a gradient is irrelevant in mechanically ventilated patients
rationale: The A-a gradient (PAO2 − PaO2) helps differentiate the cause of hypoxemia. Normal A-a gradient = 5–15 mmHg (increases with age: expected = age/4 + 4). Causes of hypoxemia with normal A-a gradient: hypoventilation, low FiO2 (altitude). Causes with elevated A-a gradient: V/Q mismatch (most common), intrapulmonary shunt, diffusion impairment. PAO2 = FiO2(PB − PH2O) − PaCO2/0.8 = 0.60(760 − 47) − 65/0.8 = 427.8 − 81.25 = 346.6 mmHg. A-a gradient = 346.6 − 55 = 291.6 mmHg, which is markedly elevated, indicating significant intrapulmonary pathology.

stem: An ABG obtained from a patient breathing room air shows PaO2 48 mmHg, PaCO2 70 mmHg. Using the alveolar gas equation, is this hypoxemia explained by hypoventilation alone?
options: Yes — calculated PAO2 is approximately 59.5 mmHg, giving an A-a gradient of about 11.5 mmHg, which is normal, so hypoventilation alone explains the hypoxemia
No — the A-a gradient must be elevated
Cannot determine without knowing the FiO2
The alveolar gas equation does not apply in this scenario
rationale: Alveolar gas equation: PAO2 = FiO2(PB − PH2O) − PaCO2/RQ = 0.21(760 − 47) − 70/0.8 = 149.7 − 87.5 = 62.2 mmHg (some use simplified calculation getting ~59.5). A-a gradient = PAO2 − PaO2 = 62.2 − 48 = ~14 mmHg. A normal A-a gradient is approximately 5–15 mmHg. Since the A-a gradient is within normal limits, the hypoxemia is fully explained by hypoventilation (elevated PaCO2) without an underlying pulmonary parenchymal disease. Supplemental O2 or improving ventilation would correct the hypoxemia.

stem: What does an SpO2 reading of 85% correspond to approximately on the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve in terms of PaO2?
options: Approximately 50–55 mmHg
Approximately 80 mmHg
Approximately 40 mmHg
Approximately 70 mmHg
rationale: The oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve relates SpO2 to PaO2. Key landmarks: SpO2 50% ≈ PaO2 27 mmHg (P50), SpO2 75% ≈ PaO2 40 mmHg (mixed venous), SpO2 85% ≈ PaO2 50–55 mmHg, SpO2 90% ≈ PaO2 60 mmHg, SpO2 95% ≈ PaO2 80 mmHg. The steep portion of the curve (PaO2 20–60 mmHg) means small changes in PaO2 cause large changes in SpO2, while the flat upper portion means large PaO2 changes produce minimal SpO2 changes.

stem: Which of the following conditions causes a leftward shift of the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve?
options: Alkalosis, hypothermia, decreased 2,3-DPG, and carbon monoxide poisoning — all increase hemoglobin's affinity for oxygen
Acidosis and hyperthermia
Increased 2,3-DPG levels
Anemia
rationale: A leftward shift means increased O2 affinity — hemoglobin binds oxygen more tightly and releases it less readily to tissues. Causes: alkalosis (Bohr effect), hypothermia, decreased 2,3-DPG (stored blood), carbon monoxide (COHb), fetal hemoglobin, and methemoglobin. The mnemonic is the opposite of a right shift. Right shift (decreased affinity): acidosis, hyperthermia, increased 2,3-DPG, hypercarbia. Clinical significance: left shift may impair tissue oxygen delivery despite normal PaO2 and SpO2.

stem: A patient with carbon monoxide poisoning has an SpO2 reading of 98% but is symptomatic with headache and confusion. Why is the SpO2 reading unreliable?
options: Standard pulse oximetry cannot distinguish carboxyhemoglobin (COHb) from oxyhemoglobin and reports a falsely normal reading
The SpO2 sensor is malfunctioning
Carbon monoxide does not affect oxygen saturation
The patient's hemoglobin is too low for accurate readings
rationale: Standard pulse oximetry uses two wavelengths of light (660 nm red and 940 nm infrared) to measure the ratio of oxyhemoglobin to deoxyhemoglobin. Carboxyhemoglobin absorbs light at 660 nm similarly to oxyhemoglobin, causing the pulse oximeter to overestimate true O2 saturation. In CO poisoning, SpO2 may read 95–100% while actual SaO2 is much lower. CO-oximetry (arterial blood gas with co-oximetry) measures COHb directly and provides accurate fractional oxyhemoglobin saturation. Some newer pulse oximeters (e.g., Masimo Rad-57) can detect COHb.

stem: A post-surgical patient has ABG: pH 7.50, PaCO2 48 mmHg, HCO3 36 mEq/L, K+ 3.0 mEq/L, Cl- 88 mEq/L. The patient has been on continuous NG suction. What acid-base disturbance is present and what is the underlying mechanism?
options: Hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis from loss of gastric HCl via NG suction — the body retains HCO3 to maintain electroneutrality
Respiratory acidosis from post-anesthesia hypoventilation
Metabolic acidosis from surgical blood loss
Respiratory alkalosis from pain-induced hyperventilation
rationale: NG suction removes gastric acid (HCl), leading to: 1) Loss of H+ ions → metabolic alkalosis (elevated HCO3, elevated pH). 2) Loss of Cl- → hypochloremia (Cl- 88, normal 96-106). The kidney compensates by retaining Cl- but also retains Na+ with HCO3 instead, perpetuating alkalosis. 3) Hypokalemia (K+ 3.0) occurs because the kidney exchanges K+ for H+ to try to correct alkalosis. The elevated PaCO2 (48) represents appropriate respiratory compensation (hypoventilation to retain CO2). Treatment: IV normal saline (provides Cl-), potassium replacement, and addressing the ongoing gastric losses.

stem: How do you systematically interpret an ABG using the 5-step approach?
options: 1) Assess pH (acidemia vs alkalemia), 2) Evaluate PaCO2 (respiratory component), 3) Evaluate HCO3 (metabolic component), 4) Determine primary disorder and compensation, 5) Calculate anion gap if metabolic acidosis
1) Check PaO2 first, 2) Look at SpO2, 3) Assess FiO2, 4) Check pH, 5) Evaluate PaCO2
1) Calculate A-a gradient, 2) Check base excess, 3) Evaluate lactate, 4) Assess pH, 5) Check electrolytes
1) Evaluate PaCO2, 2) Assess pH, 3) Calculate anion gap, 4) Check PaO2, 5) Evaluate HCO3
rationale: The systematic 5-step ABG interpretation: Step 1: Assess pH — <7.35 acidemia, >7.45 alkalemia. Step 2: Evaluate PaCO2 — does it explain the pH change? (respiratory component). Step 3: Evaluate HCO3 — does it explain the pH change? (metabolic component). Step 4: Determine if there is compensation and whether it is appropriate (use Winter's formula for metabolic acidosis, expected compensation formulas for others). Step 5: If metabolic acidosis exists, calculate the anion gap and delta-delta ratio to identify mixed disorders.

stem: A patient with sepsis has an ABG showing pH 7.33, PaCO2 28 mmHg, HCO3 14 mEq/L, lactate 6.8 mmol/L, Na+ 138, Cl- 102. What is the complete acid-base analysis?
options: High anion gap metabolic acidosis (AG = 22, lactic acidosis from sepsis) with appropriate respiratory compensation — Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 of 27–31, actual is 28
Normal anion gap metabolic acidosis
Pure respiratory alkalosis causing the low PaCO2
Compensated respiratory acidosis
rationale: Step-by-step: 1) pH 7.33 = acidemia. 2) PaCO2 28 = low (hyperventilation). 3) HCO3 14 = low (metabolic acidosis). 4) Primary disorder: metabolic acidosis (HCO3 is moving in direction to explain pH). PaCO2 is compensatory. Winter's formula: (1.5 × 14) + 8 ± 2 = 27–31. Actual PaCO2 28 is within range = appropriate compensation. 5) AG = 138 − (102 + 14) = 22 = elevated (HAGMA). The lactate of 6.8 mmol/L (>2 is abnormal) confirms lactic acidosis from tissue hypoperfusion in sepsis.

stem: A patient with chronic renal failure has ABG: pH 7.30, PaCO2 30 mmHg, HCO3 15 mEq/L, Na+ 140, Cl- 115. What type of metabolic acidosis is present?
options: Normal anion gap (hyperchloremic) metabolic acidosis — AG = 10, with elevated chloride compensating for lost bicarbonate
High anion gap metabolic acidosis
Respiratory acidosis
Mixed metabolic and respiratory acidosis
rationale: AG = Na+ − (Cl- + HCO3-) = 140 − (115 + 15) = 10 mEq/L, which is normal (8–12). This is a non-anion gap (hyperchloremic) metabolic acidosis. The chloride is elevated (115, normal 96–106) to maintain electroneutrality as HCO3 is lost. Causes of NAGMA include: renal tubular acidosis (common in early CKD), diarrhea (GI HCO3 loss), carbonic anhydrase inhibitors, ureteral diversions, and saline administration (dilutional acidosis). Early CKD often presents with NAGMA before progressing to HAGMA (uremic acidosis) in advanced disease.

stem: What is the P/F ratio and how is it interpreted?
options: PaO2/FiO2 ratio — values <300 indicate ALI, <200 indicate ARDS, and <100 indicate severe ARDS
PaCO2/FiO2 ratio used to assess ventilation adequacy
Peak pressure/Flow ratio to measure airway resistance
Plateau pressure/FiO2 used to calculate compliance
rationale: The P/F ratio (PaO2/FiO2) is a standardized index of oxygenation. Normal P/F ratio on room air: 400–500 (PaO2 80–100 / FiO2 0.21). Berlin criteria for ARDS classification: Mild ARDS: P/F 200–300 (with PEEP ≥5 cmH2O), Moderate ARDS: P/F 100–200, Severe ARDS: P/F <100. Example: PaO2 80 on FiO2 0.40 = P/F of 200 (moderate ARDS). The ratio allows comparison of oxygenation across different FiO2 levels, though it has limitations at very high FiO2 due to absorption atelectasis.

stem: A patient with salicylate (aspirin) overdose has ABG: pH 7.45, PaCO2 20 mmHg, HCO3 14 mEq/L, Na+ 140, Cl- 102. What acid-base disturbance is present?
options: Mixed respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis — characteristic of salicylate toxicity
Pure respiratory alkalosis
Pure metabolic acidosis with compensation
Normal acid-base balance
rationale: AG = 140 − (102 + 14) = 24 (elevated). Despite a normal-appearing pH (7.45), two opposing processes are occurring: 1) Respiratory alkalosis (PaCO2 20) — salicylates directly stimulate the medullary respiratory center, causing hyperventilation. This occurs early in overdose. 2) HAGMA (AG 24, HCO3 14) — salicylates uncouple oxidative phosphorylation causing lactic acidosis, and salicylic acid itself contributes to the AG. The near-normal pH results from these opposing effects canceling each other. This mixed pattern is classic for salicylate toxicity. Delta-delta: ΔAG = 12, ΔHCO3 = 10, ratio 1.2 = pure HAGMA.

stem: What is the primary function of the lungs?
options: Gas exchange — delivering oxygen to the blood and removing carbon dioxide from the body
Filtering bacteria from the bloodstream
Producing red blood cells
Regulating body temperature
rationale: The primary function of the lungs is gas exchange: oxygen (O2) moves from the alveoli into pulmonary capillary blood, while carbon dioxide (CO2) moves from the blood into the alveoli to be exhaled. This occurs across the alveolar-capillary membrane by simple diffusion, driven by partial pressure gradients. The lungs also have secondary functions including acid-base regulation (CO2 elimination), filtration of small blood clots, immune defense, and metabolism of certain substances (e.g., converting angiotensin I to angiotensin II).

stem: How many lobes does each lung have?
options: Right lung has 3 lobes (upper, middle, lower); left lung has 2 lobes (upper, lower)
Both lungs have 3 lobes each
Right lung has 2 lobes; left lung has 3 lobes
Both lungs have 2 lobes each
rationale: The right lung has 3 lobes (superior/upper, middle, and inferior/lower), separated by the horizontal and oblique fissures. The left lung has 2 lobes (superior/upper and inferior/lower), separated by the oblique fissure. The left lung is smaller to accommodate the heart (cardiac notch). The right lung has 10 bronchopulmonary segments, while the left has 8–10 depending on the classification system.

stem: What structure prevents food from entering the trachea during swallowing?
options: The epiglottis — a leaf-shaped cartilaginous flap that covers the laryngeal inlet during swallowing
The vocal cords
The uvula
The cricoid cartilage
rationale: The epiglottis is an elastic cartilage covered by mucosa that sits posterior to the tongue base. During swallowing, the larynx elevates and the epiglottis folds backward to cover the glottic opening (laryngeal inlet), directing food and liquid into the esophagus rather than the trachea. This is a critical protective reflex. Epiglottic dysfunction (e.g., from neurological disease, sedation, or structural abnormalities) increases aspiration risk.

stem: What is the carina, and at what vertebral level is it typically located?
options: The carina is the ridge where the trachea bifurcates into the right and left main bronchi, located at approximately the T4-T5 level (sternal angle of Louis)
The carina is the first cartilage ring of the trachea at C6
The carina is the opening of the larynx at C3-C4
The carina is the junction between the pharynx and esophagus at T1
rationale: The carina is the cartilaginous ridge at the bifurcation of the trachea into the right and left mainstem bronchi. It is located at approximately the T4-T5 vertebral level, corresponding to the angle of Louis (sternal angle) anteriorly. The carina is an important anatomical landmark for endotracheal tube placement — the ETT tip should be 3–5 cm above the carina (approximately T2-T4 level on chest X-ray). The carina is highly sensitive to touch and stimulating it causes vigorous coughing.

stem: Why is the right mainstem bronchus more susceptible to aspiration than the left?
options: The right mainstem bronchus is wider, shorter, and more vertically oriented than the left, allowing aspirated material to enter more easily
The right bronchus has fewer cilia for mucociliary clearance
The right lung has more blood flow than the left
The left bronchus has a valve that prevents aspiration
rationale: The right mainstem bronchus branches from the trachea at a 20–30° angle (more vertical), while the left branches at approximately 40–60° (more horizontal). The right bronchus is also wider (about 2.5 cm diameter vs 2 cm for the left) and shorter (~2.5 cm vs ~5 cm for the left). These anatomical differences mean aspirated material, foreign bodies, and misplaced endotracheal tubes preferentially enter the right bronchus. The right lower lobe is the most common site for aspiration pneumonia in upright patients, while the right upper lobe (posterior segment) is most common in supine patients.

stem: What is the function of type II alveolar cells (pneumocytes)?
options: Type II cells produce and secrete pulmonary surfactant, which reduces surface tension and prevents alveolar collapse
Type II cells are responsible for gas exchange across the alveolar membrane
Type II cells produce mucus for the airways
Type II cells are immune cells that engulf bacteria
rationale: Type II alveolar cells (also called type II pneumocytes or great alveolar cells) make up about 5% of the alveolar surface area but about 60% of alveolar cells by number. Their primary function is synthesizing, storing, and secreting pulmonary surfactant — a phospholipid-protein complex (mainly dipalmitoylphosphatidylcholine, DPPC) that reduces alveolar surface tension. Surfactant prevents atelectasis, reduces the work of breathing, and maintains alveolar stability (LaPlace's law). Type II cells also serve as progenitor cells that can differentiate into Type I cells to repair the alveolar epithelium after injury.

stem: What is tidal volume (VT) and what is the normal value for a healthy adult?
options: The volume of air inhaled or exhaled during a normal breath — approximately 500 mL (6–8 mL/kg ideal body weight)
The maximum amount of air that can be inhaled — approximately 3000 mL
The volume of air remaining in the lungs after maximal exhalation — approximately 1200 mL
The total lung capacity — approximately 6000 mL
rationale: Tidal volume (VT) is the volume of air moved in and out of the lungs during normal quiet breathing. Normal VT is approximately 500 mL or 6–8 mL/kg of ideal body weight (IBW). At a normal respiratory rate of 12–20 breaths/min, this produces a minute ventilation (VE) of approximately 6–10 L/min. In mechanical ventilation, lung-protective strategies target VT of 6–8 mL/kg IBW (or 4–6 mL/kg in ARDS) to prevent ventilator-induced lung injury.

stem: What is functional residual capacity (FRC) and why is it clinically important?
options: FRC is the volume of air remaining in the lungs after a normal exhalation (~2400 mL) — it maintains alveolar patency and serves as an oxygen reserve between breaths
FRC is the maximum volume of air that can be exhaled after a normal breath
FRC is the total volume of air the lungs can hold
FRC is the volume of air that cannot be exhaled even with maximal effort
rationale: FRC = ERV (Expiratory Reserve Volume) + RV (Residual Volume), approximately 2400 mL in a healthy adult. FRC is clinically important because: 1) It maintains alveolar inflation between breaths, preventing cyclic atelectasis. 2) It serves as an oxygen reserve, allowing continuous gas exchange between breaths. 3) FRC is reduced in: obesity, pregnancy, supine position, anesthesia, restrictive lung disease, and ARDS. 4) FRC is increased in: COPD/emphysema (air trapping). 5) PEEP is used in mechanical ventilation to increase FRC and improve oxygenation.

stem: According to the Bohr effect, how does pH affect hemoglobin's oxygen-binding affinity?
options: Decreased pH (acidosis) reduces hemoglobin's oxygen affinity, facilitating oxygen release to tissues; increased pH (alkalosis) increases affinity
pH has no effect on hemoglobin's oxygen binding
Decreased pH increases oxygen affinity
Only PaCO2 affects hemoglobin's oxygen binding, not pH
rationale: The Bohr effect describes how H+ ions and CO2 affect hemoglobin-oxygen binding. In metabolically active tissues: pH drops (more H+) and CO2 rises → hemoglobin's oxygen affinity decreases → O2 is released to tissues (right shift of oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve). In the lungs: pH is higher and CO2 is lower → hemoglobin's oxygen affinity increases → O2 is loaded onto hemoglobin. This elegant mechanism ensures efficient O2 delivery where it's needed most. The molecular basis involves protonation of histidine residues in hemoglobin, which stabilizes the deoxy (T-state) conformation.

stem: What is the Haldane effect and how does it relate to CO2 transport?
options: Deoxygenated hemoglobin carries more CO2 than oxygenated hemoglobin — in the tissues, O2 release facilitates CO2 loading; in the lungs, O2 loading facilitates CO2 release
CO2 is only transported dissolved in plasma
Oxygenated hemoglobin carries more CO2 than deoxygenated hemoglobin
The Haldane effect describes how CO2 affects oxygen binding
rationale: The Haldane effect describes the relationship between hemoglobin oxygenation and CO2 carrying capacity. Deoxygenated hemoglobin (in tissues) binds CO2 more readily (as carbaminohemoglobin) and buffers H+ more effectively than oxygenated hemoglobin. In the lungs, as O2 binds to hemoglobin, CO2 is released. CO2 transport in blood: 70% as bicarbonate (HCO3-) via carbonic anhydrase in RBCs, 23% as carbaminohemoglobin (bound to hemoglobin), and 7% dissolved in plasma. The Haldane effect accounts for approximately 50% of total CO2 exchange in the lungs.

stem: What is dead space ventilation and what are the two types?
options: Dead space is ventilated lung volume that does not participate in gas exchange — anatomic dead space (conducting airways ~150 mL) and alveolar dead space (ventilated but not perfused alveoli)
Dead space refers to the air remaining in the lungs after maximal exhalation
Dead space is the volume of air that bypasses the lungs entirely
Dead space only occurs in patients on mechanical ventilation
rationale: Dead space ventilation is wasted ventilation where air does not participate in gas exchange. Two types: 1) Anatomic dead space: the conducting airways (nose/mouth to terminal bronchioles) — approximately 150 mL or 1 mL/lb IBW. These airways conduct air but have no alveoli for gas exchange. 2) Alveolar dead space: alveoli that are ventilated but not perfused (or poorly perfused). Normally minimal in healthy lungs but increases with pulmonary embolism, low cardiac output, or overdistension. Physiologic dead space = anatomic + alveolar dead space. Measured using the Bohr equation: VD/VT = (PaCO2 − PeCO2)/PaCO2. Normal VD/VT is 0.20–0.40.

stem: What is the chloride shift (Hamburger phenomenon)?
options: The movement of chloride ions into red blood cells in exchange for bicarbonate ions moving out, which occurs during CO2 transport in the tissues
The renal reabsorption of chloride in the proximal tubule
The movement of chloride across the alveolar membrane during gas exchange
The exchange of sodium and chloride in sweat glands
rationale: The chloride shift occurs in the systemic capillaries: CO2 enters RBCs → carbonic anhydrase converts CO2 + H2O → H2CO3 → H+ + HCO3-. The HCO3- is transported out of the RBC into plasma via the band 3 protein (anion exchanger), and Cl- moves into the RBC to maintain electrical neutrality. In the lungs, the reverse occurs: Cl- exits the RBC, HCO3- enters, and is converted back to CO2 for exhalation. This process is essential for efficient CO2 transport and helps explain why venous blood has slightly higher Cl- content in RBCs and lower plasma Cl- compared to arterial blood.

stem: What is pulmonary compliance, and how is static compliance calculated?
options: Compliance is the change in volume per change in pressure (ΔV/ΔP) — static compliance = VT / (Plateau pressure − PEEP), normal is 60–100 mL/cmH2O
Compliance is the resistance to airflow in the airways
Compliance = Peak pressure × Tidal volume
Compliance is measured only during spontaneous breathing
rationale: Pulmonary compliance measures the distensibility (stretchability) of the lungs and chest wall. Static compliance (Cst) = VT / (Pplat − PEEP), measured during an inspiratory hold when there is no airflow (eliminating airway resistance). Normal: 60–100 mL/cmH2O. Decreased compliance (stiff lungs): ARDS, pulmonary fibrosis, pulmonary edema, pneumothorax, atelectasis, pneumonia, chest wall abnormalities. Increased compliance: emphysema (loss of elastic recoil). Dynamic compliance = VT / (PIP − PEEP) and includes both compliance and resistance components.

stem: What is the law of LaPlace and how does it apply to alveolar physiology?
options: P = 2T/r — smaller alveoli generate higher collapsing pressure than larger ones; surfactant equalizes surface tension to prevent small alveoli from collapsing into large ones
Gas volume is inversely proportional to pressure at constant temperature
The rate of diffusion is proportional to the surface area and pressure gradient
Gas solubility increases with increasing pressure
rationale: LaPlace's law for a sphere: P = 2T/r, where P = collapsing pressure, T = surface tension, r = radius. Without surfactant, smaller alveoli would have higher collapsing pressures and empty into larger alveoli, causing widespread atelectasis. Surfactant reduces surface tension proportionally more in smaller alveoli (surfactant molecules are more concentrated on a smaller surface area), equalizing pressures and maintaining alveolar stability. This is why premature infants lacking surfactant (born before 34–36 weeks) develop infant respiratory distress syndrome (IRDS).

stem: What are the muscles of inspiration and expiration during normal quiet breathing versus forced breathing?
options: Normal inspiration: diaphragm and external intercostals (active); normal expiration: passive elastic recoil. Forced: accessory muscles (SCM, scalenes) for inspiration; abdominals and internal intercostals for expiration
Both inspiration and expiration are always active processes using the same muscles
The diaphragm is the only muscle involved in both inspiration and expiration
Expiration is always an active process requiring muscular effort
rationale: Normal quiet breathing: Inspiration is active — the diaphragm contracts (descends, accounting for 75% of tidal volume) and external intercostals contract (elevate ribs). Expiration is passive — elastic recoil of the lungs and chest wall returns the system to FRC. Forced/labored breathing: Accessory inspiratory muscles include sternocleidomastoid, scalenes, pectoralis major/minor, and trapezius. Forced expiration uses internal intercostals (depress ribs) and abdominal muscles (rectus abdominis, obliques, transversus abdominis) to actively push the diaphragm upward. Use of accessory muscles is a sign of respiratory distress.

stem: Explain the ventilation-perfusion (V/Q) ratio concept. What are the normal V/Q ratios at the apex versus the base of the lung in an upright person?
options: Normal overall V/Q is ~0.8; the apex has higher V/Q (~3.3, more ventilation relative to perfusion) and the base has lower V/Q (~0.6, more perfusion relative to ventilation) due to gravity
V/Q is uniform throughout the lung at 1.0
The apex has lower V/Q and the base has higher V/Q
V/Q ratio only applies during mechanical ventilation
rationale: The V/Q ratio describes the matching of ventilation (V, ~4 L/min total) to perfusion (Q, ~5 L/min total), giving a normal overall ratio of ~0.8. Due to gravity in an upright person: Apex — less perfusion (low hydrostatic pressure) but relatively maintained ventilation → high V/Q (~3.3) → relative dead space effect. Base — more perfusion (gravity-dependent blood flow) with relatively less ventilation → low V/Q (~0.6) → relative shunt effect. V/Q mismatch is the most common cause of hypoxemia. Extreme V/Q states: V/Q = 0 (shunt: perfused but not ventilated) and V/Q = infinity (dead space: ventilated but not perfused).

stem: What is hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction (HPV) and what is its physiological purpose?
options: HPV is the constriction of pulmonary arterioles in response to local alveolar hypoxia, redirecting blood flow from poorly ventilated to better ventilated lung regions to optimize V/Q matching
HPV is the dilation of pulmonary vessels in response to hypoxia
HPV is the constriction of systemic arteries during hypoxia
HPV only occurs in patients with pulmonary hypertension
rationale: Hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction (HPV) is a unique response of the pulmonary vasculature (opposite to systemic vessels, which dilate in response to hypoxia). When alveolar PO2 drops below ~70 mmHg, local pulmonary arterioles constrict, diverting blood flow away from poorly ventilated areas to better-ventilated regions. This optimizes V/Q matching and improves oxygenation. HPV is inhibited by: vasodilators, calcium channel blockers, inhaled anesthetics, nitric oxide, and severe acidosis. In generalized hypoxia (e.g., high altitude), global HPV causes pulmonary hypertension, right heart strain, and potentially high-altitude pulmonary edema (HAPE).

stem: What is Fick's law of diffusion and how does it apply to gas exchange in the lungs?
options: The rate of gas diffusion is proportional to the surface area and pressure gradient, and inversely proportional to membrane thickness — Vgas = (A × D × ΔP) / T
Gas diffusion rate depends only on the concentration gradient
Diffusion is limited by blood flow rate only
