Demographics: most common in young adults (20–40 years), African Americans (3× more common than Caucasians), and women. Extrapulmonary involvement: skin (erythema nodosum, lupus pernio), eyes (uveitis), liver, spleen, heart (arrhythmias, cardiomyopathy), CNS, joints. Diagnosis: biopsy showing non-caseating granulomas + compatible clinical picture + exclusion of other causes (TB, fungal infection, lymphoma). Labs: elevated ACE level, hypercalcemia. Treatment: observation (many resolve spontaneously), corticosteroids for symptomatic/progressive disease.

stem: What is the difference between emphysema and chronic bronchitis, the two main phenotypes of COPD?
options: Emphysema: permanent enlargement and destruction of alveoli distal to terminal bronchioles (loss of elastic recoil, air trapping). Chronic bronchitis: chronic productive cough for ≥3 months/year for ≥2 consecutive years due to mucus gland hypertrophy and airway inflammation
They are the same disease with the same pathology
Emphysema affects only the trachea; chronic bronchitis affects only the alveoli
Chronic bronchitis is defined by imaging findings, not clinical criteria
rationale: Emphysema (pathological diagnosis): Permanent destruction of alveolar walls distal to terminal bronchioles. Types: Centrilobular/centriacinar (smoking-related, upper lobes), Panacinar/panlobular (alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, lower lobes), Paraseptal (subpleural, can cause spontaneous pneumothorax). Results in: loss of elastic recoil, air trapping, hyperinflation, increased compliance, impaired gas exchange. Classic presentation: thin, barrel-chested ("pink puffer"), pursed-lip breathing, dyspnea predominant. Chronic bronchitis (clinical diagnosis): Chronic productive cough ≥3 months/year for ≥2 consecutive years (after excluding other causes). Pathology: mucus gland hypertrophy (Reid index >0.50), goblet cell metaplasia, airway inflammation, excess mucus production. Classic presentation: overweight, cyanotic ("blue bloater"), productive cough, cor pulmonale. Most COPD patients have features of both.

stem: What is the appropriate oxygen saturation target for a patient with an acute COPD exacerbation?
options: SpO2 88–92% — controlled oxygen therapy to avoid suppressing the hypoxic ventilatory drive in chronic CO2 retainers
rationale: In acute COPD exacerbations, the target SpO2 is 88–92%. This is based on the BTS and GOLD guidelines, supported by the Austin et al. (2010) study showing that high-flow oxygen (targeting SpO2 >96%) increased mortality compared to titrated oxygen (targeting 88–92%) in COPD patients. Rationale: chronic CO2 retainers may rely on hypoxic drive for ventilation (peripheral chemoreceptors). Excessive oxygen → reduced hypoxic drive → hypoventilation → worsening hypercapnia → CO2 narcosis → respiratory failure. Additionally, the Haldane effect contributes: increased PaO2 causes O2-loaded hemoglobin to release CO2, further raising PaCO2. Use Venturi masks for precise FiO2 delivery. Start with 24–28% FiO2 and titrate. Monitor ABGs 30–60 minutes after any oxygen adjustment.

stem: What is a massive hemoptysis, and what is the initial management?
options: Expectoration of >100–600 mL of blood in 24 hours (or >100 mL/hour) — initial management includes positioning the bleeding side down (if known), securing the airway (large ETT, selective intubation of non-bleeding lung), IV access, blood products, and urgent bronchoscopy or interventional radiology
Any amount of blood in the sputum requires emergency surgery
Hemoptysis of 5 mL is considered massive
Massive hemoptysis is managed with cough suppressants only
rationale: Massive hemoptysis (definition varies: >100–600 mL/24 hours depending on the source; life-threatening hemoptysis is generally >100 mL/hour or any amount causing hemodynamic instability or airway compromise). The cause of death is usually asphyxiation (drowning in blood), not exsanguination. Most common sources: bronchial arteries (90%). Management: 1) Position bleeding side down (lateral decubitus) to protect the non-bleeding lung. 2) Secure airway: large ETT (≥8.0) for suctioning; selective mainstem intubation of the non-bleeding lung if needed. 3) Resuscitation: IV access, crossmatch, blood products. 4) Bronchoscopy: localize bleeding, attempt local therapy (cold saline, epinephrine, balloon tamponade). 5) Bronchial artery embolization (interventional radiology) — definitive therapy in most cases (>90% success). 6) Surgery if embolization fails. Common causes: bronchiectasis, TB, lung cancer, aspergilloma.

stem: What is the purpose of a cuff leak test before extubation?
options: To assess for upper airway edema and predict post-extubation stridor — the ETT cuff is deflated and the difference between inspired and expired tidal volumes is measured; a cuff leak volume <110 mL or <12% of VT suggests higher risk of post-extubation stridor
To check if the endotracheal tube is properly sized
To measure tidal volume accuracy
To assess readiness for spontaneous breathing
rationale: The cuff leak test assesses air leak around the deflated ETT cuff, indirectly evaluating the caliber of the subglottic airway. Procedure: On volume-controlled ventilation, deflate the cuff and measure the difference between inspiratory and expiratory VT over 6 breaths (average the lowest 3 values). Cuff leak = Inspiratory VT − Expiratory VT. Interpretation: Leak >110 mL or >12% of VT → low risk of stridor. Leak <110 mL or <12% → higher risk of post-extubation stridor (not a contraindication to extubation, but should prompt preparation). If failed: consider systemic steroids (methylprednisolone) 12–24 hours before extubation. Have equipment ready: racemic epinephrine, heliox, reintubation equipment, and consider direct laryngoscopy to assess edema. Most useful in patients at higher risk: prolonged intubation, traumatic intubation, female, large ETT relative to airway size.

stem: What is the effect of prone positioning on oxygenation in ARDS, and what does the PROSEVA trial show?
options: Prone positioning improves oxygenation by improving V/Q matching, recruiting dependent lung regions, and reducing compression — the PROSEVA trial showed significant mortality reduction (16% vs 32.8%) with ≥16 hours/day of prone positioning in severe ARDS
Prone positioning only improves comfort with no effect on oxygenation
Prone positioning increases mortality in ARDS
Prone positioning is only used for patients with mild ARDS
rationale: Physiological benefits of prone positioning: 1) Redistribution of ventilation: gravitational change improves ventilation of previously dependent (dorsal) lung regions. 2) More homogeneous ventilation: reduces the ventral-dorsal transpulmonary pressure gradient. 3) Improved V/Q matching: better matching of ventilation and perfusion. 4) Reduced lung compression: the heart no longer compresses the left lower lobe. 5) Improved secretion drainage. PROSEVA trial (Guérin, 2013): Early prone positioning (≥16 hours/day) in severe ARDS (P/F <150, FiO2 ≥0.60, PEEP ≥5) showed 28-day mortality of 16% (prone) vs. 32.8% (supine), p<0.001 — NNT of 6. Contraindications: spinal instability, open abdomen, facial trauma, elevated ICP, unstable fractures. Complications: pressure injuries, ETT displacement, IV/line complications.

stem: What is pressure support ventilation (PSV) and when is it used?
options: PSV is a spontaneous breathing mode where the ventilator augments each patient-triggered breath with a set inspiratory pressure — used for weaning, reducing work of breathing, and as a standalone mode for patients with adequate respiratory drive
PSV delivers mandatory breaths regardless of patient effort
PSV is the same as assist-control ventilation
PSV can only be used with an artificial airway
rationale: Pressure support ventilation (PSV): Patient-triggered, pressure-limited, flow-cycled mode. The patient initiates every breath (no mandatory/backup rate). The ventilator delivers a preset inspiratory pressure (above PEEP) to augment the breath. Inspiration terminates when inspiratory flow drops to a percentage of peak flow (typically 25%). Tidal volume depends on: set PS level, patient effort, lung compliance, and airway resistance. Uses: 1) Overcome ETT resistance (5–8 cmH2O). 2) Weaning mode — gradually reduce PS level. 3) Combined with SIMV for spontaneous breaths. 4) Non-invasive ventilation (IPAP on BiPAP = PEEP + PS). Advantages: patient controls rate, flow, and inspiratory time → improved comfort and synchrony. Risks: no guaranteed volume — if patient effort decreases, VT drops (must set appropriate apnea alarms). Cannot be used in patients with inadequate respiratory drive without a backup rate.

stem: What is the appropriate initial ventilator settings for a patient being intubated for acute respiratory failure?
options: Mode: AC (volume or pressure control), VT: 6-8 mL/kg IBW, RR: 14-18 breaths/min, PEEP: 5 cmH2O initially, FiO2: 1.0 initially then titrate down to SpO2 ≥92%, I:E ratio: 1:2 or greater
VT 15 mL/kg, RR 8, PEEP 0, FiO2 0.21
No standard initial settings exist
Always start with SIMV mode at maximum settings
rationale: Initial ventilator settings: 1) Mode: AC (volume control or pressure control) — ensures minimum minute ventilation while allowing patient triggering. 2) Tidal volume: 6–8 mL/kg IBW (4–6 if ARDS). Calculate IBW first: Males = 50 + 2.3(height inches − 60), Females = 45.5 + 2.3(height inches − 60). 3) Respiratory rate: 14–18/min initially, adjust based on ABG/minute ventilation needs. 4) FiO2: Start at 1.0, then wean rapidly to lowest FiO2 maintaining SpO2 ≥92% (88–92% for COPD). Prolonged FiO2 >0.60 risks oxygen toxicity. 5) PEEP: 5 cmH2O initially, increase as needed for oxygenation (ARDS: use PEEP/FiO2 tables). 6) I:E ratio: 1:2 or greater (longer expiration to prevent air trapping). 7) Flow rate: 40–60 L/min (adjust for patient comfort). 8) Trigger sensitivity: flow trigger 2 L/min or pressure trigger −1 to −2 cmH2O. Obtain ABG 30 minutes after initiation and adjust.

stem: What is airway pressure release ventilation (APRV) and how does it work?
options: APRV is a mode that maintains a high continuous pressure (P-high) for a prolonged time (T-high, ~4-6 seconds) for oxygenation, then briefly releases to a low pressure (P-low, usually 0) for a short time (T-low, ~0.5-0.8 seconds) for CO2 removal, while allowing spontaneous breathing throughout
APRV is a conventional volume-controlled mode
APRV does not allow spontaneous breathing
APRV uses only low airway pressures
rationale: APRV (Airway Pressure Release Ventilation) is essentially CPAP with intermittent brief releases. Settings: P-high (high pressure level, similar to mean airway pressure goal — typically 20–30 cmH2O), T-high (time at high pressure — 4–6 seconds, the bulk of the respiratory cycle), P-low (release pressure — typically 0 cmH2O), T-low (release time — typically 0.5–0.8 seconds, set to terminate at 75% of peak expiratory flow to maintain recruitment). Mechanism: The long T-high at P-high provides sustained alveolar recruitment and oxygenation (similar to high PEEP). The brief release (T-low) allows some exhaled volume for CO2 removal. Patients can breathe spontaneously at any point. Benefits: high mean airway pressure without high peak pressures, promotes spontaneous breathing (may reduce sedation needs), improved hemodynamics compared to equivalent conventional PEEP. Used as a rescue strategy in ARDS, though evidence for mortality benefit is limited.

stem: What are the chest X-ray findings in a patient with a right-sided tension pneumothorax?
options: Absent lung markings on the right, visible visceral pleural line, mediastinal shift to the left, flattened right hemidiaphragm, widened right intercostal spaces, and possible subcutaneous emphysema
Bilateral infiltrates
Left lung consolidation
Normal chest X-ray
rationale: CXR findings in right-sided tension pneumothorax: 1) Absent lung markings on the right (no vascular markings beyond the pleural line). 2) Visible visceral pleural line — sharp white line separating aerated lung from pleural air. 3) Mediastinal shift to the LEFT (heart, trachea, and mediastinal structures pushed away from the tension). 4) Flattened or inverted right hemidiaphragm (pushed down by positive pressure). 5) Widened intercostal spaces on the right. 6) Possible subcutaneous emphysema (air tracking into soft tissues). 7) Deep sulcus sign on supine films (radiolucent costophrenic angle). IMPORTANT: Tension pneumothorax is a clinical diagnosis — do NOT delay treatment to obtain a CXR if clinical signs are present (absent breath sounds, tracheal deviation, hypotension, JVD). Treat first with needle decompression, then chest tube.

stem: What is the significance of the angle of Louis (sternal angle) in respiratory assessment?
options: The angle of Louis is the junction between the manubrium and body of the sternum at the T4-T5 level — it marks the level of the carina, the second intercostal space, and the upper border of the heart
The angle of Louis is where the diaphragm attaches
It has no significance in respiratory assessment
The angle of Louis is located at the xiphoid process
rationale: The angle of Louis (sternal angle) is the palpable ridge at the junction of the manubrium and sternal body. Anatomical significance: 1) Corresponds to the T4-T5 vertebral level. 2) Marks the level of the tracheal bifurcation (carina). 3) The 2nd costal cartilage articulates here — allowing identification of the 2nd intercostal space (important for needle decompression of tension pneumothorax, central line placement, and auscultation landmarks). 4) Marks the upper border of the heart and the aortic arch. 5) The azygos vein drains into the SVC at this level. 6) Used as a reference point for CVP measurement (5 cm below the sternal angle approximates the right atrium). For respiratory therapists, this landmark helps confirm ETT positioning (tip should be 3–5 cm above the carina).

stem: An intubated patient suddenly develops high peak pressures, decreased breath sounds on the left, tracheal deviation to the right, and hypotension. What is the most likely cause and immediate action?
options: Left tension pneumothorax — immediately perform needle decompression at the 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line on the left, followed by chest tube placement
Right mainstem intubation — withdraw the ETT 2 cm
Mucus plug in the left bronchus — suction the airway
Equipment malfunction — check the ventilator circuit
rationale: The clinical triad strongly suggests left tension pneumothorax: 1) High peak pressures (reduced compliance from compressed lung). 2) Decreased/absent breath sounds on the LEFT. 3) Tracheal deviation to the RIGHT (mediastinum pushed away). 4) Hypotension (decreased venous return from increased intrathoracic pressure). This is more common in mechanically ventilated patients because positive pressure ventilation can cause or worsen pneumothorax. Immediate action: Needle decompression with a 14-gauge angiocatheter at the 2nd ICS, MCL on the LEFT side (or 4th-5th ICS, anterior axillary line). A rush of air confirms the diagnosis. Follow with chest tube (tube thoracostomy) placement at the 4th-5th ICS, anterior axillary line. Document with CXR after tube placement. While the mnemonic DOPE (Displacement, Obstruction, Pneumothorax, Equipment) is used for acute ventilator deterioration, the specific signs here point to pneumothorax.

stem: What is the DOPE mnemonic used for in mechanically ventilated patients?
options: DOPE is used to troubleshoot acute deterioration: Displacement (ETT), Obstruction (secretions, kinking), Pneumothorax, Equipment failure — systematically evaluate these when a ventilated patient suddenly worsens
DOPE stands for Dosage, Oxygen, Pressure, Expiration settings
DOPE is a sedation assessment scale
DOPE is a weaning protocol
rationale: DOPE is a systematic approach to acute deterioration in mechanically ventilated patients: D — Displacement: ETT dislodged, migrated to right mainstem, or esophageal. Check ETT depth at teeth (usually 21–23 cm at lip for adults), auscultate, check ETCO2, visualize with laryngoscope if needed. O — Obstruction: Mucus plug, blood clot, patient biting tube, kinked ETT. Pass suction catheter, use bite block. P — Pneumothorax: Absent breath sounds unilaterally, tracheal deviation, subcutaneous emphysema, elevated PIP. Needle decompression if tension. E — Equipment failure: Ventilator malfunction, circuit leak, disconnection, empty O2 source. Disconnect from ventilator and bag-mask ventilate while troubleshooting. If in doubt, always disconnect and bag — this simultaneously tests the airway and eliminates equipment as the cause.

stem: What is the difference between flow triggering and pressure triggering on a mechanical ventilator?
options: Flow triggering: the ventilator detects a decrease in bias flow caused by patient inspiration (typically set at 1-2 L/min below bias flow). Pressure triggering: the ventilator detects a negative pressure change in the circuit (typically set at -1 to -2 cmH2O). Flow triggering requires less patient effort and improves synchrony
They are identical mechanisms with different names
Pressure triggering is always more sensitive than flow triggering
Flow triggering is only available on ICU ventilators
rationale: Trigger mechanisms determine how the ventilator detects patient inspiratory effort: Pressure triggering: A continuous baseline pressure (PEEP) is maintained. When the patient inhales, circuit pressure drops. When it drops below the set trigger sensitivity (e.g., −1 to −2 cmH2O below PEEP), the ventilator delivers a breath. Disadvantage: requires the patient to generate enough negative pressure to trigger — increases work of breathing. Flow triggering: A continuous flow (bias flow, typically 5–20 L/min) passes through the circuit. When the patient inhales, some flow is diverted, and the ventilator detects the difference between delivered and returned flow (e.g., 2 L/min decrease). Advantage: requires less patient effort (lower trigger work), improved patient-ventilator synchrony, reduced auto-triggering. Most modern ICU ventilators use flow triggering as default. Trigger sensitivity that is too sensitive → auto-triggering; too insensitive → increased work of breathing and missed triggers.

stem: What is the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve's clinical significance when PaO2 drops below 60 mmHg?
options: Below PaO2 60 mmHg (SpO2 ~90%), the curve enters the steep portion where small decreases in PaO2 cause large drops in SpO2 — this is why 60 mmHg is the critical threshold for supplemental oxygen
The curve is flat below 60 mmHg so changes are minimal
PaO2 below 60 mmHg has no effect on SpO2
The curve only applies above PaO2 80 mmHg
rationale: The oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve has a sigmoid (S-shaped) curve. The flat upper portion (PaO2 60–100 mmHg, SpO2 90–100%) means large PaO2 changes cause minimal SpO2 changes — this provides a safety buffer. The steep middle portion (PaO2 20–60 mmHg, SpO2 35–90%) means small PaO2 changes cause dramatic SpO2 drops. At PaO2 60 mmHg (SpO2 ~90%), the curve transitions from flat to steep. This is why PaO2 60 mmHg / SpO2 90% is the critical threshold: below this, oxygen delivery to tissues becomes increasingly compromised with any further PaO2 decrease. Clinical implication: supplemental O2 should maintain PaO2 ≥60 mmHg (SpO2 ≥90%). Above PaO2 100, further increases provide minimal benefit (fully saturated) and risk oxygen toxicity.

stem: What is the innervation of the diaphragm, and what is its clinical significance?
options: The phrenic nerve (C3, C4, C5) innervates the diaphragm — 'C3, 4, 5 keeps the diaphragm alive.' Cervical spinal cord injuries above C3 result in diaphragmatic paralysis requiring mechanical ventilation
The vagus nerve innervates the diaphragm
The diaphragm is innervated by T1-T6 intercostal nerves
The diaphragm contracts without neural input
rationale: The phrenic nerve arises from cervical nerve roots C3, C4, and C5 (primarily C4). It provides both motor and sensory innervation to the diaphragm. Clinical significance: 1) Spinal cord injury at or above C3 → bilateral diaphragmatic paralysis → patient cannot breathe spontaneously → lifelong ventilator dependence (or diaphragmatic pacing). 2) C3-C5 injury: variable diaphragmatic function depending on completeness. 3) C5 and below: diaphragm function preserved, patient can breathe spontaneously (though accessory muscles may be affected). 4) Unilateral phrenic nerve injury (cardiac surgery, thoracic tumor, birth trauma): elevated hemidiaphragm on CXR, usually tolerated. 5) The phrenic nerve runs along the anterior scalene muscle — can be damaged during central line placement or neck surgery.

stem: What is the Graham's law of diffusion and which respiratory gas diffuses faster?
options: Graham's law states that the rate of diffusion of a gas is inversely proportional to the square root of its molecular weight — however, in the lungs, CO2 diffuses ~20 times faster than O2 because of its much greater solubility, which overrides the molecular weight effect
O2 diffuses 20 times faster than CO2
Both gases diffuse at exactly the same rate
Nitrogen diffuses faster than both O2 and CO2
rationale: Graham's law: Rate of diffusion ∝ 1/√(MW). O2 (MW 32) vs CO2 (MW 44): by MW alone, O2 would diffuse ~1.17× faster. However, Fick's law of diffusion incorporates the diffusion coefficient D = solubility/√MW. CO2 is approximately 24× more soluble in plasma than O2. Net effect: CO2 diffuses ~20× faster across the alveolar-capillary membrane than O2. Clinical significance: diffusion impairment (thickened membrane or reduced surface area) primarily affects O2 transfer. PaCO2 remains normal until very late in diffusion-limited diseases. Patients with pulmonary fibrosis may have hypoxemia with normal PaCO2 at rest, but develop hypoxemia more quickly with exercise (shortened capillary transit time).

stem: An ABG from a patient with severe methanol ingestion shows pH 7.05, PaCO2 12 mmHg, HCO3 3 mEq/L, Na+ 140, Cl- 100. Calculate the anion gap and delta-delta ratio. Is there a concurrent metabolic disorder?
options: AG = 37, ΔAG = 25, ΔHCO3 = 21, delta-delta ratio = 1.19 — consistent with a pure high anion gap metabolic acidosis from methanol. No significant concurrent non-AG acidosis or metabolic alkalosis
AG = 12, normal — no metabolic disorder present
Delta-delta ratio of 0.5 suggests concurrent non-AG metabolic acidosis
Cannot calculate without knowing potassium level
rationale: AG = 140 − (100 + 3) = 37 (severely elevated). ΔAG = 37 − 12 = 25. ΔHCO3 = 24 − 3 = 21. Delta-delta ratio = ΔAG/ΔHCO3 = 25/21 = 1.19. Interpretation: ratio 1–2 = pure HAGMA (consistent with methanol toxicity — formic acid accumulation). If ratio <1: concurrent non-AG metabolic acidosis. If ratio >2: concurrent metabolic alkalosis. Winter's formula: expected PaCO2 = (1.5 × 3) + 8 ± 2 = 10.5–14.5. Actual PaCO2 of 12 falls within the expected range, confirming appropriate respiratory compensation without a concurrent respiratory disorder. This severe HAGMA with osmolar gap (methanol is osmotically active) requires urgent treatment: fomepizole (alcohol dehydrogenase inhibitor), sodium bicarbonate for pH <7.15, and hemodialysis.

stem: What is the clinical significance of capnography (end-tidal CO2 monitoring) in mechanically ventilated patients?
options: ETCO2 confirms endotracheal tube placement, monitors ventilation adequacy, detects changes in CO2 production and cardiac output, and can identify circuit disconnections — normal ETCO2 is 35–45 mmHg with a PaCO2-ETCO2 gradient of 2–5 mmHg
ETCO2 only measures oxygen levels
ETCO2 is the same as PaCO2 in all patients
Capnography is only used during anesthesia
rationale: Capnography (continuous ETCO2 monitoring) provides real-time waveform and numerical CO2 data. Clinical uses: 1) ETT placement confirmation: persistent ETCO2 waveform confirms tracheal (not esophageal) intubation — considered the gold standard. 2) Ventilation monitoring: ETCO2 trends correlate with PaCO2 (normally ETCO2 is 2–5 mmHg lower than PaCO2 due to dead space). 3) Cardiac output: sudden drop in ETCO2 → decreased pulmonary blood flow (cardiac arrest, PE, decreased cardiac output). 4) CPR quality: ETCO2 >10 mmHg during CPR indicates effective chest compressions. ETCO2 >20 mmHg may predict ROSC. 5) Waveform analysis: shark fin pattern → bronchospasm; gradually rising baseline → rebreathing; absent waveform → disconnection/extubation. 6) PaCO2-ETCO2 gradient: widens with increased dead space (PE, emphysema, low cardiac output).

stem: What are the complications of prolonged mechanical ventilation?
options: Ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP), ventilator-induced lung injury (VILI), oxygen toxicity, tracheal stenosis, diaphragmatic atrophy/weakness, hemodynamic compromise, GI stress ulcers, and ICU-acquired weakness
Mechanical ventilation has no complications if settings are correct
The only complication is pneumothorax
Complications only occur with ventilation lasting more than 30 days
rationale: Complications of prolonged mechanical ventilation: 1) VAP: incidence 9–27%, develops >48 hours after intubation. Prevention: HOB elevation 30–45°, oral care with chlorhexidine, subglottic secretion drainage, daily sedation vacation and SBT, peptic ulcer and DVT prophylaxis. 2) VILI: barotrauma, volutrauma, atelectrauma, biotrauma. Prevention: lung-protective ventilation. 3) Oxygen toxicity: prolonged FiO2 >0.60 causes free radical damage (absorption atelectasis, diffuse alveolar damage). 4) Tracheal injury: stenosis, tracheomalacia, tracheoesophageal fistula from cuff pressure >25 cmH2O. 5) Diaphragmatic atrophy: ventilator-induced diaphragmatic dysfunction (VIDD) — disuse atrophy from controlled ventilation. 6) Hemodynamic: positive pressure reduces venous return → decreased cardiac output. 7) GI: stress ulcers, decreased motility. 8) ICU-acquired weakness: polyneuropathy/myopathy. 9) Psychological: anxiety, PTSD, delirium.

stem: What is the difference between a Venturi mask and a non-rebreather mask?
options: Venturi mask is a high-flow device delivering precise FiO2 (24–50%) via air entrainment. Non-rebreather mask is a reservoir device delivering high FiO2 (~60–80% in practice, theoretically up to 100%) at 10–15 L/min via a reservoir bag and one-way valves
Both devices deliver the same FiO2
Non-rebreather masks deliver precise FiO2
Venturi masks deliver higher FiO2 than non-rebreather masks
rationale: Venturi mask (air-entrainment mask): Uses the Venturi principle to entrain specific amounts of room air. High-flow system (total flow exceeds patient demand). Delivers precise, fixed FiO2: 24%, 28%, 31%, 35%, 40%, 50%. Ideal for COPD patients needing controlled O2. Interchangeable color-coded adapters. Non-rebreather mask (NRB): A reservoir bag filled with 100% O2 plus one-way valves (flaps) that prevent room air entrainment and exhaled air from entering the reservoir. Flow: 10–15 L/min (bag should remain >2/3 full). Theoretically delivers >90% FiO2, but in practice delivers 60–80% due to mask leak and imperfect seal. Low-flow, variable-performance device. Used for acute hypoxemia requiring high FiO2 (trauma, acute MI, CO poisoning). Not appropriate for precise FiO2 delivery.

stem: What are the four phases of a normal capnography waveform?
options: Phase I: inspiratory baseline (zero CO2), Phase II: expiratory upstroke (mixing of dead space and alveolar gas), Phase III: alveolar plateau (relatively flat, represents alveolar CO2), Phase IV: inspiratory downstroke (fresh gas inspiration, CO2 drops to baseline). ETCO2 is measured at the end of Phase III
There are only two phases: inspiration and expiration
All four phases show the same CO2 level
Phase I is the highest CO2 reading
rationale: Normal capnogram waveform: Phase I (inspiratory baseline/early expiration): CO2 is near zero — represents dead space gas (no CO2). Phase II (expiratory upstroke): Rapid rise in CO2 as alveolar gas mixes with dead space gas. The angle (alpha angle) indicates V/Q matching — a slurred upstroke (shark fin) suggests bronchospasm. Phase III (alveolar plateau): Relatively flat portion representing pure alveolar gas. ETCO2 is measured at the end of this phase (highest CO2 point). A steep slope suggests V/Q mismatch. Phase IV (inspiratory downstroke): Rapid drop to baseline as fresh gas (no CO2) is inspired. Should return sharply to zero — if baseline is elevated, rebreathing is occurring (exhausted CO2 absorber, inadequate fresh gas flow, faulty expiratory valve).

stem: What is the significance of the PaCO2-ETCO2 gradient, and what conditions cause it to widen?
options: The normal gradient is 2–5 mmHg (PaCO2 > ETCO2); it widens with increased dead space (PE, low cardiac output, emphysema, high PEEP overdistension) because more ventilated-but-not-perfused alveoli dilute the exhaled CO2
The gradient is always zero
ETCO2 is always higher than PaCO2
The gradient only changes with airway obstruction
rationale: ETCO2 normally underestimates PaCO2 by 2–5 mmHg because dead space gas (no CO2) dilutes the exhaled sample. An increased PaCO2-ETCO2 gradient (>5 mmHg) indicates increased physiologic dead space: 1) Pulmonary embolism: blocked pulmonary vessels → ventilated but not perfused alveoli → sudden drop in ETCO2 with widened gradient. 2) Low cardiac output: decreased pulmonary perfusion. 3) Emphysema: destroyed alveolar-capillary interface. 4) Excessive PEEP: overdistension creating Zone 1 (west) conditions. 5) Hypovolemia: reduced pulmonary blood flow. A narrowed or reversed gradient (ETCO2 > PaCO2) can occur in: pregnancy, pediatric patients, and low tidal volume ventilation. Trending the gradient is more useful than single measurements.

stem: What is the role of the intercostal muscles in breathing?
options: External intercostals elevate the ribs during inspiration (bucket-handle and pump-handle motion), increasing thoracic volume. Internal intercostals (interosseous portion) depress the ribs during forced expiration. The parasternal internal intercostals assist with inspiration
All intercostal muscles function only during expiration
Intercostal muscles play no role in breathing
Only the internal intercostals are active during quiet breathing
rationale: Intercostal muscles are arranged in 11 pairs between adjacent ribs: External intercostals: Fibers run anteroinferiorly (hands-in-pockets direction). Contraction elevates the ribs → increases AP and lateral thoracic dimensions (pump-handle and bucket-handle motion). Active during inspiration along with the diaphragm. More prominent role during increased ventilatory demand. Internal intercostals (interosseous portion): Fibers run posteroinferiorly (perpendicular to externals). Contraction depresses the ribs → decreases thoracic volume. Active during forced expiration. Parasternal internal intercostals (intercartilaginous portion): Despite being internal intercostals, they assist INSPIRATION by elevating the ribs (due to their position near the sternum, their mechanical advantage changes). Innervation: segmental intercostal nerves (T1-T11). Paralysis of intercostal muscles (high thoracic spinal cord injury) reduces vital capacity but breathing continues if the diaphragm is intact.

stem: What is tuberculosis (TB) and what is the classic chest X-ray presentation?
options: TB is a chronic bacterial infection caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis — primary TB shows Ghon complex (calcified focus + hilar lymphadenopathy); reactivation TB shows upper lobe cavitary lesions, often with fibrosis and volume loss
TB always presents with bilateral lower lobe infiltrates
TB is a viral infection with normal chest X-ray
TB only affects the pleura, not the lung parenchyma
rationale: Tuberculosis (TB) is caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis, an acid-fast bacillus (AFB) spread by airborne droplet nuclei. Primary TB (initial infection): Often asymptomatic or mild. CXR: Ghon focus (calcified lower/middle lobe lesion), Ghon complex (Ghon focus + calcified hilar lymph nodes), Ranke complex (healed Ghon complex). Latent TB: Positive TST/IGRA, no symptoms, non-infectious. Reactivation TB (active disease): Classic CXR findings: upper lobe cavitary lesions (apical/posterior segments), fibronodular infiltrates, air-fluid levels in cavities. Symptoms: chronic cough, hemoptysis, night sweats, weight loss, fever. Miliary TB: hematogenous spread → diffuse miliary (millet seed-sized) nodules throughout both lungs. Diagnosis: AFB smear and culture, NAAT (GeneXpert). Treatment: RIPE therapy (Rifampin, Isoniazid, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol) for 6–9 months.

stem: What is the difference between an arterial blood gas (ABG) and a capillary blood gas (CBG)?
options: ABG is obtained from an artery (typically radial) and is the gold standard for acid-base and oxygenation assessment. CBG is obtained from a warmed capillary bed (typically heel in neonates) and correlates well with pH and PaCO2 but PaO2 is unreliable
CBG and ABG are identical in all values
CBG is more accurate than ABG for oxygenation
ABG can only be obtained from the femoral artery
rationale: ABG: Obtained from a peripheral artery (radial most common — perform Allen test first; also brachial or femoral). Gold standard for: pH, PaCO2, PaO2, HCO3, base excess, SaO2. Complications: hematoma, arterial spasm, infection, pseudoaneurysm. CBG (capillary blood gas): Obtained from arterialized capillary blood (warmed heel/finger to increase arterial flow). Used primarily in neonates (heel stick) and pediatrics. Correlation with ABG: pH and PCO2 correlate well (within 0.01 pH units and 3–5 mmHg for PCO2). PO2 does NOT correlate well — capillary PO2 is significantly lower and unreliable for assessing oxygenation. When PaO2 assessment is critical, an ABG must be obtained. CBG is acceptable for monitoring trends in acid-base status and ventilation when arterial access is difficult.

stem: What is the Allen test and why is it performed before arterial blood gas sampling?
options: The Allen test assesses collateral blood flow through the ulnar artery before radial artery puncture — the patient clenches the fist, both arteries are occluded, the fist is opened, and ulnar pressure is released; normal color return within 5-10 seconds confirms adequate collateral flow
The Allen test measures blood pressure in both arms
The Allen test is used to check for DVT
The Allen test is performed after the blood draw, not before
rationale: The modified Allen test evaluates ulnar collateral circulation before radial artery cannulation or puncture. Procedure: 1) Have the patient make a tight fist (or squeeze the palm in unconscious patients) to empty blood from the hand. 2) Occlude both radial and ulnar arteries at the wrist. 3) Have the patient open the hand — the palm should be pale/blanched. 4) Release ULNAR artery pressure while maintaining radial occlusion. 5) Observe color return to the palm: Positive (normal) test: color returns within 5–10 seconds → adequate ulnar collateral flow → safe to use radial artery. Negative (abnormal) test: color does not return within 10–15 seconds → inadequate ulnar collateral → do NOT use that radial artery (risk of hand ischemia). Use the other wrist or an alternative site. The test is not 100% reliable but is a standard-of-care assessment.

stem: What is the effect of temperature on ABG results? How are results adjusted for hypothermia or hyperthermia?
options: ABG analyzers warm blood to 37°C for measurement. In hypothermic patients, actual (in vivo) PaO2 and PaCO2 are lower and pH is higher than the reported 37°C values; in hyperthermic patients, the opposite is true. Temperature-corrected values can be calculated but most clinicians manage using uncorrected (37°C) values (alpha-stat strategy)
Temperature has no effect on ABG results
ABG values are automatically corrected for patient temperature
Hypothermia increases PaCO2 on the ABG report
rationale: ABG analyzers heat the blood sample to 37°C (standard conditions) regardless of patient temperature. Gas solubility is affected by temperature (Henry's law): Cold blood: gases are more soluble → less comes out of solution → actual (in vivo) PaO2 and PaCO2 are lower than reported at 37°C; actual pH is higher. Warm blood: gases are less soluble → more comes out → actual PaO2 and PaCO2 are higher; actual pH is lower. Two management strategies: Alpha-stat: Use uncorrected (37°C) values. Most commonly used. Maintains intracellular pH homeostasis. Preferred for most clinical scenarios. pH-stat: Use temperature-corrected values. CO2 is added to maintain corrected pH at 7.40. Used in some cardiac surgery protocols (deep hypothermic circulatory arrest). Most clinicians use alpha-stat and manage based on uncorrected values.

stem: What is a pulmonary function test (PFT) flow-volume loop, and how does it differ in obstructive versus restrictive disease?
options: A flow-volume loop plots airflow (y-axis) against volume (x-axis) during forced inspiration and expiration. Obstructive disease shows a scooped/concave expiratory limb with reduced peak flow. Restrictive disease shows a narrower but normally shaped loop with reduced volumes
Flow-volume loops are identical in all lung diseases
Flow-volume loops only measure inspiratory flow
Restrictive disease shows a scooped expiratory limb
rationale: The flow-volume loop is a graphical representation of forced inspiration and expiration: Normal: The expiratory limb rises sharply to peak expiratory flow (PEF) then descends linearly. The inspiratory limb forms a symmetric semicircle. Obstructive disease (COPD, asthma): Scooped/concave expiratory limb (airflow limitation, especially at lower lung volumes). Reduced PEF. Normal or increased TLC (air trapping). The more severe the obstruction, the more pronounced the concavity. Restrictive disease (fibrosis, neuromuscular): The loop shape is preserved (normal morphology) but narrower — reduced volumes (smaller loop). PEF may be reduced proportionally. FVC is reduced. Fixed upper airway obstruction: Flattened both inspiratory and expiratory limbs (truncated box shape). Variable extrathoracic obstruction: Flattened inspiratory limb. Variable intrathoracic obstruction: Flattened expiratory limb.

stem: What is the role of inhaled corticosteroids (ICS) in asthma management?
options: ICS are the cornerstone of long-term asthma control — they reduce airway inflammation, bronchial hyperresponsiveness, mucus production, and exacerbation frequency. Not used for acute relief, but for maintenance therapy starting at Step 2 of the stepwise approach
ICS are the first-line rescue medication for acute bronchospasm
ICS have no role in asthma management
ICS are only used for severe asthma requiring ICU admission
rationale: Inhaled corticosteroids (ICS) are the most effective anti-inflammatory medications for persistent asthma. Mechanism: suppress inflammatory gene transcription, reduce eosinophilic inflammation, decrease mucus secretion, reduce bronchial hyperresponsiveness, and restore beta-receptor sensitivity. GINA stepwise approach: Step 1: As-needed low-dose ICS-formoterol (preferred) or SABA. Step 2: Daily low-dose ICS (e.g., budesonide 200–400 μg/day, fluticasone 100–250 μg/day). Step 3: Low-dose ICS/LABA combination (e.g., fluticasone/salmeterol, budesonide/formoterol). Step 4: Medium-dose ICS/LABA. Step 5: High-dose ICS/LABA + add-on therapy (tiotropium, biologics). Common ICS: beclomethasone, budesonide, fluticasone, ciclesonide, mometasone. Side effects: oral candidiasis (thrush), dysphonia — minimized by using spacer and mouth rinsing after use.

stem: What is Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS) and why is respiratory monitoring critical?
options: GBS is an acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy causing ascending paralysis — respiratory monitoring is critical because diaphragmatic weakness can cause rapid respiratory failure; serial FVC and NIF measurements guide the decision to intubate (FVC <20 mL/kg or NIF weaker than -30 cmH2O)
GBS only affects the legs and never causes respiratory failure
GBS is a chronic condition that develops over years
Respiratory failure in GBS is caused by pneumonia, not muscle weakness
rationale: GBS is an autoimmune disorder typically triggered by preceding infection (Campylobacter jejuni most common). It causes acute ascending weakness/paralysis that can progress to respiratory failure in 20–30% of cases. The 20/30/40 rule for intubation: FVC <20 mL/kg (normal >60), NIF weaker than −30 cmH2O (normal more negative than −60), and maximum expiratory pressure (MEP) <40 cmH2O. Serial measurements (every 2–4 hours) are crucial because deterioration can be rapid. ABG changes are late findings — by the time PaCO2 rises, the patient may be in imminent danger. Additional indicators for intubation: rapid decline in FVC (>30% in 24 hours), inability to cough/clear secretions, bulbar weakness (swallowing dysfunction, aspiration risk). Treatment: IVIG or plasmapheresis, ICU monitoring, DVT prophylaxis, pain management.

stem: What is the significance of the I:E ratio in mechanical ventilation?
options: The I:E ratio is the ratio of inspiratory time to expiratory time — normal is 1:2 to 1:3; adequate expiratory time prevents air trapping, while inverse ratio (I:E >1:1) can improve oxygenation in severe ARDS but requires deep sedation
I:E ratio has no clinical significance
I:E ratio should always be 1:1
Inverse ratio ventilation is the standard for all patients
rationale: I:E ratio = inspiratory time : expiratory time. Normal spontaneous: ~1:2. Mechanical ventilation: typically 1:2 to 1:3. Calculated from RR and inspiratory time: with RR 15 (4-second cycle) and Ti 1.5 sec, Te 2.5 sec → I:E = 1:1.67. Importance of adequate expiratory time: Prevents air trapping (auto-PEEP), especially critical in obstructive disease (COPD, asthma) where long time constants require prolonged expiration. Strategies: reduce RR, reduce VT, increase inspiratory flow rate. Inverse ratio ventilation (IRV): I:E >1:1 (e.g., 2:1, 3:1). Used rarely in severe ARDS to increase mean airway pressure and improve oxygenation. Mechanism: prolonged inspiration recruits alveoli; shortened expiration creates intrinsic PEEP. Requires deep sedation and often paralysis (very uncomfortable). Evidence for improved outcomes is limited.

stem: What is the difference between oxygen toxicity and absorption atelectasis, and at what FiO2 levels do they become concerns?
options: Oxygen toxicity (FiO2 >0.60 for >24–48 hours) causes free radical damage to the alveolar-capillary membrane (tracheobronchitis, then diffuse alveolar damage resembling ARDS). Absorption atelectasis occurs at high FiO2 when nitrogen is washed out and oxygen is rapidly absorbed, causing alveolar collapse
Oxygen is always safe at any concentration for any duration
Oxygen toxicity only occurs at FiO2 1.0
Absorption atelectasis occurs at FiO2 0.21
rationale: Oxygen toxicity: Prolonged exposure to FiO2 >0.60 generates reactive oxygen species (superoxide, hydroxyl radicals, hydrogen peroxide) that overwhelm antioxidant defenses. Progression: tracheobronchitis (first 12–24 hours) → decreased mucociliary clearance → surfactant dysfunction → diffuse alveolar damage (resembles ARDS). Minimize by weaning FiO2 as quickly as tolerable. No exact safe threshold, but generally try to reduce to FiO2 ≤0.60 as soon as possible. Absorption atelectasis: At high FiO2, nitrogen (the primary 'splint' gas maintaining alveolar patency) is washed out. In areas of low V/Q, oxygen is rapidly absorbed into the blood, faster than it is replaced, causing alveolar collapse. Can occur at FiO2 as low as 0.50 in susceptible areas. Contributes to worsening shunt fraction. Both phenomena argue for using the lowest effective FiO2.

stem: What is the primary structural difference between the trachea and the esophagus that is relevant to intubation?
options: The trachea has C-shaped cartilaginous rings anteriorly and laterally that keep it open, with a posterior membranous wall shared with the esophagus — the esophagus has no cartilage and collapses when empty
The trachea is posterior to the esophagus
Both structures are identical in wall composition
The esophagus has cartilage rings while the trachea does not
rationale: The trachea is located anterior to the esophagus in the midline of the neck and upper mediastinum. Tracheal anatomy: 16–20 C-shaped (horseshoe-shaped) hyaline cartilage rings provide structural support, keeping the airway patent. The posterior wall (shared with the esophagus) is membranous (trachealis muscle — smooth muscle) to allow esophageal expansion during swallowing. Length: ~10–12 cm (adults). Diameter: ~2.0–2.5 cm. Relevance to intubation: The rigid anterior/lateral cartilage rings are palpable landmarks. The posterior membranous wall is vulnerable to perforation during intubation if the stylet protrudes past the ETT tip. Esophageal intubation can be detected by: absence of ETCO2, no chest rise, gastric distension, and no condensation in the ETT. Confirmation: ETCO2 is the gold standard for verifying tracheal placement.

stem: What ABG changes would you expect to see after a patient hyperventilates for 30 minutes?
options: Increased pH (alkalemia), decreased PaCO2 (blown off by hyperventilation), and slightly decreased HCO3 (acute compensation — HCO3 drops ~2 mEq/L per 10 mmHg decrease in PaCO2)
Decreased pH and increased PaCO2
No changes would occur in 30 minutes
Increased PaCO2 and decreased pH
rationale: Hyperventilation increases alveolar ventilation → more CO2 is exhaled → PaCO2 decreases → respiratory alkalosis. After 30 minutes: pH rises above 7.45 (acute respiratory alkalosis). PaCO2 drops below 35 mmHg. HCO3 decreases slightly by acute buffering mechanism: HCO3 drops ~2 mEq/L per 10 mmHg decrease in PaCO2. Example: if PaCO2 drops from 40 to 20 mmHg, expected HCO3 = 24 − (2 × 2) = 20 mEq/L. Full renal compensation takes 2–3 days (chronic response: HCO3 drops 4–5 mEq/L per 10 mmHg decrease in PaCO2). PaO2 increases slightly (alveolar gas equation: lowering PaCO2 increases PAO2). Clinical effects of acute respiratory alkalosis: lightheadedness, paresthesias (perioral, fingertips), carpopedal spasm (Trousseau sign from decreased ionized calcium), chest tightness.

stem: What is the function of the larynx in the respiratory system?
options: The larynx serves as an airway between the pharynx and trachea, protects the lower airway during swallowing (via epiglottic closure and vocal cord adduction), enables phonation, and participates in the cough reflex and Valsalva maneuver
The larynx only produces sound and has no protective function
The larynx is part of the digestive system only
The larynx has no role in the cough reflex
rationale: The larynx is located at C3-C6 vertebral level. Components: thyroid cartilage (Adam's apple), cricoid cartilage (only complete ring), arytenoid cartilages, epiglottis, and the vocal cords (true and false). Functions: 1) Airway: provides passage for air between pharynx and trachea. 2) Protection: epiglottis closes during swallowing; true vocal cords (glottis) adduct as a secondary protective mechanism; cough reflex clears aspirated material. 3) Phonation: vocal cord vibration produces sound; pitch controlled by cord tension (cricothyroid muscle). 4) Valsalva maneuver: glottis closes to increase intrathoracic/intra-abdominal pressure (coughing, bearing down, lifting). Innervation: superior laryngeal nerve (external branch: cricothyroid muscle; internal branch: sensory above cords) and recurrent laryngeal nerve (all other intrinsic muscles, sensory below cords). Recurrent laryngeal nerve injury → vocal cord paralysis (hoarseness).

stem: What is the significance of the cricoid cartilage in respiratory care?
options: The cricoid is the only complete cartilaginous ring in the airway — it is the landmark for cricothyrotomy (emergency surgical airway), serves as the narrowest point of the pediatric airway, and is used for Sellick's maneuver (cricoid pressure) during rapid sequence intubation
The cricoid cartilage has no clinical significance
The cricoid is the largest cartilage in the larynx
The cricoid is only relevant in neonatal patients
rationale: The cricoid cartilage is a signet ring-shaped cartilage located at the C6 vertebral level, just below the thyroid cartilage. It is the only complete (360°) cartilage ring in the airway. Clinical significance: 1) Emergency surgical airway (cricothyrotomy): performed through the cricothyroid membrane (between thyroid and cricoid cartilages) when the airway cannot be secured by intubation. 2) Pediatric airway: the cricoid ring is the narrowest point of the airway in children <8 years (in adults, the narrowest point is the glottis). This is why uncuffed ETTs were traditionally used in small children — sized by the cricoid ring. 3) Sellick's maneuver: posterior pressure on the cricoid compresses the esophagus against the vertebral body to prevent passive regurgitation during RSI (controversial — may worsen laryngoscopic view). 4) Landmark for palpation: identifying the CTM, palpating tracheal position.

stem: What is the relationship between minute ventilation, tidal volume, and respiratory rate?
options: Minute ventilation (VE) = tidal volume (VT) × respiratory rate (f) — normal VE is approximately 6–10 L/min. Alveolar ventilation (VA) = (VT − VD) × f, which is the effective ventilation participating in gas exchange
Minute ventilation = tidal volume / respiratory rate
Minute ventilation is independent of respiratory rate
Minute ventilation only measures inspiratory volume
rationale: Minute ventilation (VE) = VT × f. Example: VT 500 mL × RR 14 = VE 7000 mL/min (7 L/min). However, not all of VE participates in gas exchange. Alveolar ventilation (VA) = (VT − VD) × f, where VD = dead space volume (~150 mL). Example: (500 − 150) × 14 = 4900 mL/min alveolar ventilation. Clinical importance: Two breathing patterns can produce the same VE but very different VA: Pattern 1: VT 500 × RR 20 = VE 10,000 → VA = (500−150) × 20 = 7000 mL/min. Pattern 2: VT 250 × RR 40 = VE 10,000 → VA = (250−150) × 40 = 4000 mL/min. Pattern 2 (rapid shallow breathing) is far less efficient because a greater proportion of each breath is wasted on dead space ventilation, explaining why rapid shallow breathing leads to CO2 retention.

stem: What is the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation and how does it relate to ABG interpretation?
options: pH = 6.1 + log([HCO3−] / (0.03 × PaCO2)) — it mathematically describes the relationship between pH, HCO3, and PaCO2, showing that pH depends on the ratio of metabolic (HCO3) to respiratory (PaCO2) components
pH = HCO3 + PaCO2
pH = PaCO2 / HCO3
The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation does not apply to blood
rationale: The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation: pH = pKa + log([HCO3−] / [H2CO3]). Since H2CO3 = 0.03 × PaCO2: pH = 6.1 + log([HCO3−] / (0.03 × PaCO2)). Key insight: pH depends on the RATIO of HCO3 to PaCO2, not their absolute values. Normal: pH = 6.1 + log(24 / (0.03 × 40)) = 6.1 + log(24/1.2) = 6.1 + log(20) = 6.1 + 1.3 = 7.40. The equation shows: 1) If HCO3 decreases (metabolic acidosis) or PaCO2 increases (respiratory acidosis), pH falls. 2) If HCO3 increases (metabolic alkalosis) or PaCO2 decreases (respiratory alkalosis), pH rises. 3) Compensation maintains the ratio close to 20:1 to keep pH near 7.40. 4) The body cannot fully compensate to exactly 7.40 through compensation alone — the pH will remain slightly on the side of the primary disorder.

stem: What is the proper technique for obtaining an arterial blood gas sample?
options: Perform modified Allen test, prepare a heparinized syringe, palpate the radial artery, insert the needle at 45° angle bevel up, allow arterial pressure to fill the syringe, remove air bubbles, cap immediately, place on ice, and transport to lab within 15–30 minutes
Use a venipuncture technique with a large-bore needle at 90°
No special preparation is needed — any syringe can be used
ABGs can only be drawn by physicians
rationale: ABG sampling technique: 1) Perform modified Allen test on the selected wrist. 2) Position: extend wrist over a towel roll (dorsiflexion). 3) Use pre-heparinized ABG syringe (or heparinize a 1–3 mL syringe with sodium heparin). 4) Clean the site with alcohol. 5) Palpate the radial artery with 2–3 fingers. 6) Insert needle (22–25G) at 45° angle, bevel up, proximal to the palpation point. 7) Advance slowly until arterial blood pulses into the syringe (should fill spontaneously under arterial pressure). 8) Obtain 1–2 mL of blood. 9) Remove needle and apply firm pressure for ≥5 minutes (longer if on anticoagulants). 10) Expel any air bubbles immediately (air contamination increases PaO2 and decreases PaCO2). 11) Cap the syringe. 12) Roll between palms to mix with heparin. 13) Place on ice (slows cellular metabolism). 14) Transport and analyze within 15–30 minutes. Document: patient temperature, FiO2, ventilator settings, and clinical status.

stem: What is idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF) and what are its key clinical features?
options: IPF is a chronic, progressive fibrotic lung disease of unknown cause characterized by usual interstitial pneumonia (UIP) pattern — presenting with progressive dyspnea, dry cough, bibasilar velcro-like crackles, clubbing, and honeycombing on HRCT
IPF is an infectious disease treated with antibiotics
IPF causes obstructive lung disease with wheezing
IPF is reversible with corticosteroids
rationale: IPF is the most common idiopathic interstitial pneumonia. Epidemiology: typically males >50 years, smokers or ex-smokers. Pathology: usual interstitial pneumonia (UIP) pattern — patchy fibrosis with honeycombing, temporal heterogeneity (areas of old and new fibrosis), fibroblast foci. Clinical features: insidious progressive exertional dyspnea, nonproductive cough, bibasilar inspiratory velcro-like crackles (fine crackles), digital clubbing (25–50%), and eventually cor pulmonale. HRCT: bibasilar, subpleural reticular opacities, traction bronchiectasis, honeycombing. PFTs: restrictive pattern with markedly reduced DLCO. Prognosis: median survival 3–5 years from diagnosis. Treatment: antifibrotic agents (pirfenidone, nintedanib) slow progression. Corticosteroids and immunosuppressants are NOT effective and may be harmful. Lung transplant for eligible patients.

stem: What is the proper endotracheal tube (ETT) size for adult males and females, and how is proper placement confirmed?
options: Males: 7.5–8.5 mm ID; Females: 7.0–8.0 mm ID. Proper placement confirmed by bilateral breath sounds, ETCO2 waveform (gold standard), chest X-ray showing tip 3–5 cm above the carina (approximately T2-T4 level), and depth at teeth (21–23 cm for males, 19–21 cm for females)
All adults use the same ETT size: 6.0 mm
ETT placement is confirmed only by auscultation
The ETT tip should be at the carina
rationale: ETT sizing: Adult males: typically 8.0 mm ID (range 7.5–8.5). Adult females: typically 7.5 mm ID (range 7.0–8.0). Larger tubes: easier suctioning, lower resistance, better bronchoscopy access. Smaller tubes: may be needed for difficult airways. Confirmation of placement: 1) ETCO2: persistent waveform for ≥6 breaths = tracheal placement (gold standard). 2) Auscultation: bilateral equal breath sounds, no epigastric sounds. 3) Direct visualization: see the tube pass through the vocal cords. 4) CXR: ETT tip should be 3–5 cm above the carina (at T2-T4 level with head in neutral position). 5) Depth at teeth: 21–23 cm for males, 19–21 cm for females (rule of thumb: 3 × ETT ID). 6) FOB: direct visualization of carina through ETT. Cuff pressure: maintain 20–25 cmH2O (check q8-12h) to prevent tracheal ischemia (<30 cmH2O) while maintaining seal.

stem: What is the expected compensation for acute versus chronic respiratory acidosis?
options: Acute: HCO3 rises 1 mEq/L per 10 mmHg rise in PaCO2 (buffering). Chronic: HCO3 rises 3.5 mEq/L per 10 mmHg rise in PaCO2 (renal compensation over 3–5 days). The degree of HCO3 elevation helps distinguish acute from chronic
There is no difference in compensation between acute and chronic
HCO3 decreases in both acute and chronic respiratory acidosis
Acute compensation is greater than chronic compensation
rationale: Compensation rules for respiratory acidosis: Acute (minutes to hours — cellular buffering): HCO3 increases ~1 mEq/L per 10 mmHg increase in PaCO2. pH drops significantly. Example: PaCO2 60 → expected HCO3 = 24 + 1 × (60−40)/10 = 26. Chronic (3–5 days — renal compensation): HCO3 increases ~3.5 mEq/L per 10 mmHg increase in PaCO2. pH is near-normal. Example: PaCO2 60 → expected HCO3 = 24 + 3.5 × (60−40)/10 = 31. If measured HCO3 is between the acute and chronic expected values, the condition may be partially compensated (subacute) or acute-on-chronic. If HCO3 is higher than chronic expected, consider concurrent metabolic alkalosis. If lower than acute expected, consider concurrent metabolic acidosis. These compensation formulas are essential for identifying mixed acid-base disorders.

stem: What is the expected respiratory compensation for metabolic alkalosis?
options: PaCO2 increases approximately 0.7 mmHg for each 1 mEq/L increase in HCO3 — respiratory compensation for metabolic alkalosis is limited because hypoventilation causes hypoxemia, which stimulates breathing
PaCO2 decreases in metabolic alkalosis
There is no respiratory compensation for metabolic alkalosis
PaCO2 increases 3 mmHg for each 1 mEq/L increase in HCO3
rationale: In metabolic alkalosis, the respiratory compensation is hypoventilation (CO2 retention to lower pH). Expected PaCO2 = 40 + 0.7 × (HCO3 − 24). Alternatively: expected PaCO2 ≈ 0.7 × HCO3 + 21 (± 2). Example: HCO3 36 → expected PaCO2 = 40 + 0.7 × 12 = 48.4 mmHg. Important limitation: respiratory compensation for metabolic alkalosis is limited because: 1) Hypoventilation → CO2 retention → hypoxemia, which triggers peripheral chemoreceptors to increase ventilation. 2) Maximum compensatory PaCO2 rarely exceeds 55–60 mmHg. If PaCO2 is higher than predicted, suspect concurrent respiratory acidosis. If lower, suspect concurrent respiratory alkalosis. This is in contrast to metabolic acidosis, where hyperventilation can drive PaCO2 very low (Kussmaul breathing) without being limited by hypoxemia.

stem: What is the work of breathing (WOB) and what factors increase it?
options: WOB is the energy required to move air in and out of the lungs — increased by: decreased compliance (stiff lungs), increased airway resistance, increased minute ventilation demands, auto-PEEP, and artificial airway resistance
WOB is constant regardless of lung disease
WOB only refers to the diaphragmatic effort
WOB decreases with increasing airway resistance
rationale: Work of breathing (WOB) = Pressure × Volume (area under the pressure-volume curve). Normal WOB: 0.3–0.6 J/L. Components: Elastic work (65%): overcoming lung and chest wall recoil. Resistive work (35%): overcoming airway and tissue resistance. Factors increasing WOB: 1) Decreased compliance: ARDS, fibrosis, pulmonary edema, obesity → more pressure needed per unit volume. 2) Increased resistance: bronchospasm, secretions, small airways, narrow ETT → more pressure needed to generate flow. 3) Auto-PEEP: patient must overcome intrinsic PEEP before generating inspiratory flow. 4) Increased ventilatory demand: sepsis, fever, acidosis → increased minute ventilation required. 5) Artificial airway: ETT imposes additional resistance (Poiseuille's law: resistance ∝ 1/r⁴). Clinical signs of increased WOB: tachypnea, accessory muscle use, paradoxical breathing, diaphoresis. Excessive WOB → respiratory muscle fatigue → respiratory failure.

stem: What is the normal pH of arterial blood, and how much can it deviate before becoming life-threatening?
options: Normal pH is 7.35–7.45; pH <6.80 or >7.70 is generally considered incompatible with life — even smaller deviations (pH <7.20 or >7.60) can cause significant organ dysfunction
pH can safely range from 6.0 to 8.0
Normal pH is 7.0 and any deviation is immediately fatal
pH does not affect organ function
rationale: Normal arterial pH: 7.35–7.45 (hydrogen ion concentration of 35–45 nmol/L). Life-threatening ranges: pH <6.80 and >7.70 are generally incompatible with life. Clinical effects of severe acidemia (pH <7.20): decreased myocardial contractility, vasodilation, arrhythmias (ventricular fibrillation), decreased response to catecholamines, hyperkalemia, decreased consciousness. Clinical effects of severe alkalemia (pH >7.60): decreased cerebral blood flow (vasoconstriction), arrhythmias, seizures, hypokalemia (H+/K+ exchange), decreased ionized calcium (tetany, Trousseau/Chvostek signs), leftward shift of oxyhemoglobin curve (impaired O2 delivery). The body has three lines of defense: 1) Chemical buffers (immediate — seconds), 2) Respiratory compensation (minutes to hours), 3) Renal compensation (hours to days).

stem: What is the Poiseuille's law and how does it apply to airway management?
options: Poiseuille's law states that resistance to flow is inversely proportional to the fourth power of the radius (R ∝ 1/r⁴) — a 50% reduction in airway radius increases resistance 16-fold, explaining why even small amounts of edema or secretions significantly increase airway resistance
Resistance is directly proportional to the radius
Resistance does not change with airway radius
Poiseuille's law only applies to liquids, not gases
rationale: Poiseuille's law: R = 8ηL/πr⁴, where R = resistance, η = gas viscosity, L = tube length, r = radius. The critical relationship is R ∝ 1/r⁴: halving the radius increases resistance 16-fold. Clinical applications: 1) ETT size: a 6.0 mm ETT has ~2.5× the resistance of an 8.0 mm ETT (6/8)⁴ = 0.316 → resistance ratio ~3.2. This is why larger ETTs are preferred when possible. 2) Pediatric airways: a small child's airway is narrow; 1 mm of edema has dramatic effects. In a 4 mm infant airway, 1 mm edema reduces radius by 50% → 16× resistance increase. In a 10 mm adult airway, 1 mm edema reduces radius by 20% → only ~2.4× increase. 3) Bronchospasm/secretions: even modest airway narrowing dramatically increases resistance and WOB. 4) Heliox: helium is less dense than nitrogen → reduces turbulent flow resistance (though Poiseuille's law strictly applies to laminar flow; Reynold's number determines flow type).

stem: What is meconium aspiration syndrome (MAS) and how is it managed?
options: MAS occurs when a newborn aspirates meconium-stained amniotic fluid, causing airway obstruction, chemical pneumonitis, surfactant inactivation, and pulmonary hypertension — management includes gentle suctioning, supplemental oxygen, mechanical ventilation if needed, surfactant therapy, and potentially inhaled nitric oxide or ECMO for refractory cases
MAS is always benign and requires no treatment
MAS only occurs in premature infants
The primary treatment for MAS is antibiotics alone
rationale: Meconium aspiration syndrome (MAS) affects 2–5% of infants born through meconium-stained amniotic fluid (MSAF). More common in post-term infants. Pathophysiology: 1) Mechanical obstruction: meconium plugs airways → ball-valve effect → air trapping, uneven ventilation. 2) Chemical pneumonitis: bile acids and enzymes in meconium cause inflammation. 3) Surfactant inactivation: meconium components inhibit surfactant function. 4) Persistent pulmonary hypertension (PPHN): hypoxia and acidosis cause pulmonary vasoconstriction. CXR: patchy bilateral infiltrates, hyperinflation, possible pneumothorax (air trapping). Management: 1) Delivery room: if vigorous (good tone, crying, RR >100) — routine care; if depressed — intubate and suction. Current NRP guidelines no longer recommend routine tracheal suctioning for all depressed infants born through MSAF. 2) Supportive: supplemental O2, gentle ventilation (avoid high pressures → risk of air leak). 3) Surfactant replacement therapy. 4) iNO for PPHN. 5) ECMO for refractory hypoxemia. 6) Antibiotics (infection cannot be excluded). Mortality: 5–10%.

stem: What are the key ventilator alarm causes and troubleshooting for high-pressure alarms?
options: High-pressure alarms indicate increased PIP — causes include bronchospasm, secretions/mucus plugging, patient biting the ETT, coughing/fighting the ventilator, kinking of ETT or circuit, pneumothorax, decreased compliance (ARDS worsening), and right mainstem migration. Troubleshoot: suction, check ETT position, assess breath sounds, bronchodilators, sedate if asynchronous
High-pressure alarms always indicate ventilator malfunction
Ignore high-pressure alarms as they are never clinically significant
High-pressure alarms only occur in volume-controlled ventilation
rationale: High-pressure alarm (PIP exceeds set limit) — systematic approach: 1) Assess the patient immediately (not just the ventilator). 2) Disconnect and bag-ventilate if acute distress. 3) Check ETT: obstruction (pass suction catheter), kinking, position (right mainstem migration → unilateral breath sounds). 4) Check circuit: water accumulation, kinking. 5) Patient causes: bronchospasm (wheezing → bronchodilators), secretions (rhonchi → suction), coughing/biting (bite block, suctioning), patient-ventilator asynchrony (sedation, adjust settings), pneumothorax (absent breath sounds, tracheal deviation → needle decompression). 6) Decreased compliance: worsening ARDS, pulmonary edema, abdominal distension, pleural effusion, atelectasis. 7) Check PIP vs Pplat: elevated PIP with normal Pplat → resistance issue. Both elevated → compliance issue. High-pressure alarms in VCV prevent further volume delivery (safety feature). In PCV, pressure is limited so tidal volume drops instead (monitor for low VT alarm).

stem: What is tracheostomy and what are the indications for performing one in mechanically ventilated patients?
options: Tracheostomy is a surgical opening in the anterior trachea — indications include prolonged mechanical ventilation (typically >10–14 days expected), upper airway obstruction, facilitation of airway suctioning and pulmonary toilet, and to promote ventilator weaning. Benefits include improved patient comfort, reduced sedation, and easier oral care
Tracheostomy is never performed in ICU patients
Tracheostomy is only for permanent ventilator-dependent patients
Tracheostomy increases the risk of ventilator-associated pneumonia
rationale: Tracheostomy indications: 1) Prolonged mechanical ventilation: generally considered when >10–14 days of ventilation is anticipated (timing varies by institution/patient). 2) Upper airway obstruction: tumors, bilateral vocal cord paralysis, severe angioedema. 3) Airway protection: severe traumatic brain injury, stroke with poor neurologic prognosis, bulbar dysfunction. 4) Facilitation of weaning: reduced dead space (~50% less than oral ETT), improved secretion management, reduced work of breathing. 5) Patient comfort: allows oral feeding, speaking (with speaking valve), improved mobility, reduced sedation. Techniques: Surgical (open) tracheostomy or Percutaneous dilatational tracheostomy (PDT, bedside procedure). Timing controversy: early (<7 days) vs. late (>10 days) — evidence is mixed, but early tracheostomy may reduce sedation duration and ICU length of stay. TracMan and SETPOINT trials did not show mortality benefit with early tracheostomy.

stem: What is the concept of oxygen delivery (DO2) and how is it calculated?
options: DO2 = cardiac output (CO) × arterial oxygen content (CaO2) × 10 — normal DO2 is approximately 1000 mL O2/min. It represents the total amount of oxygen delivered to tissues per minute and depends on both cardiac function and oxygen carrying capacity
DO2 = PaO2 × respiratory rate
DO2 only depends on FiO2
DO2 is the same as oxygen consumption (VO2)
rationale: Oxygen delivery (DO2) = CO × CaO2 × 10 (the ×10 converts dL to L). CaO2 = (1.34 × Hb × SaO2) + (0.003 × PaO2). Normal DO2: 5 L/min × 20 mL O2/dL × 10 = 1000 mL O2/min. Normal VO2 (oxygen consumption): ~250 mL O2/min. O2 extraction ratio: VO2/DO2 = 250/1000 = 25% (normal). The three determinants of DO2: 1) Cardiac output (heart rate × stroke volume): affected by preload, contractility, afterload. 2) Hemoglobin: anemia reduces O2 carrying capacity. 3) SaO2/PaO2: affected by pulmonary gas exchange. Optimizing DO2 requires addressing all three components. Critical DO2: the point below which VO2 becomes supply-dependent → anaerobic metabolism → lactic acidosis. In sepsis, extraction is impaired, so critical DO2 occurs at higher levels. Mixed venous O2 saturation (SvO2, normal 65–75%) reflects the balance between DO2 and VO2.

stem: What is the difference between pressure-regulated volume control (PRVC) and standard volume control ventilation?
options: PRVC is a dual-control mode that targets a set tidal volume but uses a decelerating flow pattern and automatically adjusts inspiratory pressure breath-by-breath to deliver the desired volume at the lowest possible pressure — combining the volume guarantee of VCV with the flow pattern benefits of PCV
PRVC and VCV are identical modes
PRVC does not guarantee any tidal volume
PRVC uses a constant (square) flow pattern
rationale: Pressure-Regulated Volume Control (PRVC) is an adaptive dual-control mode. The ventilator delivers a test breath, measures compliance, then adjusts inspiratory pressure for subsequent breaths to achieve the target VT. Key features: 1) Volume-targeted: guarantees VT delivery (like VCV). 2) Pressure-limited: uses lowest pressure needed (auto-adjusting). 3) Decelerating flow pattern (like PCV): may improve gas distribution. 4) Breath-by-breath adaptation: if compliance improves, pressure decreases; if compliance worsens, pressure increases (up to a maximum pressure limit). Advantages: volume guaranteed, lower peak pressures than VCV, adapts to changing lung mechanics. Disadvantages: if the patient has variable effort, the ventilator may inappropriately decrease support (patient works harder → compliance appears better → ventilator reduces pressure). May not work well in patients with highly variable respiratory efforts. Brand names: PRVC (Maquet), AutoFlow (Dräger), Volume Control+ (Medtronic).

stem: What is the concept of permissive hypercapnia in ARDS management?
options: Permissive hypercapnia is the deliberate acceptance of elevated PaCO2 (and associated respiratory acidosis) to allow the use of lower tidal volumes and airway pressures in lung-protective ventilation — generally tolerated if pH remains above 7.20 and there are no contraindications
PaCO2 must always be maintained at exactly 40 mmHg
Permissive hypercapnia means allowing PaCO2 above 100 mmHg
Permissive hypercapnia is used in all ventilated patients
rationale: Permissive hypercapnia is a key component of lung-protective ventilation in ARDS. Rationale: Limiting VT to 6 mL/kg IBW and Pplat ≤30 cmH2O may result in inadequate CO2 elimination. Rather than increasing VT/pressures (risking VILI), elevated PaCO2 is accepted. Target: maintain pH >7.20 (some centers accept 7.15). If pH drops below threshold, consider: increasing RR (up to 35/min, ensuring adequate expiratory time), sodium bicarbonate infusion, or THAM (tris-hydroxymethyl aminomethane). Beneficial effects of hypercapnia: rightward shift of oxyhemoglobin curve (enhanced O2 delivery), possibly anti-inflammatory. Contraindications to permissive hypercapnia: elevated intracranial pressure (CO2 is a cerebral vasodilator), severe pulmonary hypertension, significant cardiac arrhythmias, and severe metabolic acidosis. CO2 rises approximately 6–10 mmHg in the first few minutes of hypoventilation, then 3–6 mmHg per hour. In practice, PaCO2 of 60–80 mmHg is commonly tolerated in ARDS.

stem: What is the role of neuromuscular blocking agents (NMBAs) in ARDS management?
options: NMBAs (e.g., cisatracurium) may be used in early severe ARDS to improve patient-ventilator synchrony, reduce oxygen consumption, improve chest wall compliance, and facilitate lung-protective ventilation — the ACURASYS trial showed 90-day mortality benefit with 48 hours of cisatracurium in severe ARDS
NMBAs are never used in ARDS
NMBAs improve spontaneous breathing ability
NMBAs are only used for intubation, never for maintenance
rationale: Neuromuscular blocking agents in ARDS: The ACURASYS trial (2010) showed that early cisatracurium (48-hour infusion) in severe ARDS (P/F <150) reduced 90-day mortality (31.6% vs 40.7%, adjusted HR 0.68). However, the ROSE trial (2019) did not confirm this benefit with a different protocol. Mechanisms of potential benefit: 1) Eliminates patient-ventilator asynchrony (prevents dyssynchronous breathing worsening VILI). 2) Reduces oxygen consumption (paralyzed muscles consume less O2). 3) Improves chest wall compliance (relaxed muscles). 4) Facilitates strict low VT ventilation. 5) May have anti-inflammatory properties (cisatracurium specifically). Risks: ICU-acquired weakness/myopathy (especially with concurrent corticosteroids), inadequate sedation/awareness (always ensure adequate sedation before paralysis — BIS monitoring recommended), prolonged paralysis, pressure injuries. Current practice: consider cisatracurium in early severe ARDS with severe dyssynchrony despite deep sedation, for 24–48 hours maximum.

stem: What is venovenous extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (VV-ECMO) and when is it indicated?
options: VV-ECMO is a form of extracorporeal life support that drains venous blood, oxygenates it through an artificial membrane, removes CO2, and returns it to the venous system — indicated for severe reversible respiratory failure (ARDS) refractory to conventional therapy including lung-protective ventilation, prone positioning, and NMBAs
VV-ECMO replaces the heart function
VV-ECMO is the first-line therapy for mild ARDS
VV-ECMO can only be used for cardiac failure
rationale: VV-ECMO circuit: Drainage cannula (usually femoral or internal jugular vein) → centrifugal pump → membrane oxygenator (gas exchange) → return cannula (usually internal jugular vein). Blood is oxygenated and CO2 is removed extracorporeally, allowing "lung rest" (ultra-protective ventilation: low VT, low pressures, low FiO2). Indications (ELSO guidelines): 1) Severe ARDS with P/F <80 on FiO2 >0.80 despite optimal ventilatory management. 2) Refractory hypercapnia with pH <7.20 despite maximal ventilatory settings. 3) Potentially reversible cause. Contraindications: irreversible lung disease (unless bridge to transplant), severe neurological injury, advanced age (relative), prolonged mechanical ventilation >7–10 days (relative). EOLIA trial (2018): suggested mortality benefit with VV-ECMO in severe ARDS (35% vs 46%, p=0.09 — not statistically significant but Bayesian analysis supports benefit). Complications: bleeding (anticoagulation required), hemolysis, infection, circuit thrombosis, recirculation.

stem: What are the types of oxygen delivery systems classified as low-flow versus high-flow devices?
options: Low-flow (variable FiO2): nasal cannula, simple face mask, partial rebreather, non-rebreather — do not meet total inspiratory demand. High-flow (fixed/precise FiO2): Venturi mask, large-volume nebulizer, high-flow nasal cannula — meet or exceed total inspiratory demand
All oxygen devices are low-flow
Low-flow devices deliver higher FiO2 than high-flow devices
The classification is based solely on liter flow
rationale: Low-flow (variable performance) devices: Total gas flow is less than the patient's inspiratory demand → room air is entrained → actual FiO2 varies with breathing pattern. Nasal cannula (1–6 L/min, ~24–44% FiO2). Simple face mask (5–10 L/min, ~35–55%). Partial rebreather mask (6–10 L/min, ~35–60%). Non-rebreather mask (10–15 L/min, ~60–80%). High-flow (fixed performance) devices: Total gas flow meets or exceeds the patient's peak inspiratory flow rate (~30–60 L/min) → no room air entrainment → FiO2 is predictable/fixed. Air-entrainment (Venturi) mask (24–50%, total flow 30–100+ L/min). Large-volume nebulizer (28–100%). High-flow nasal cannula (21–100%, flow 20–60 L/min). The key distinction is not the liter flow rate but whether the system meets total inspiratory demand — HFNC at 60 L/min is high-flow, while a nasal cannula at 6 L/min is low-flow.

stem: What is the importance of ideal body weight (IBW) in setting mechanical ventilator tidal volumes?
options: Tidal volume is based on IBW (not actual body weight) because lung size correlates with height, not weight — obese patients have the same lung volume as someone of the same height but normal weight, so using actual weight in obese patients would cause overdistension and VILI
Actual body weight should always be used for VT calculations
IBW and actual body weight are the same
VT should be set based on patient age, not weight
rationale: IBW calculation: Males: 50 + 2.3 × (height in inches − 60) kg. Females: 45.5 + 2.3 × (height in inches − 60) kg. Why IBW: Lung size (and specifically the number and size of alveoli) is determined by height and sex, not by body mass. An obese patient (e.g., 150 kg, 5'8") has the same lung volume as a normal-weight person of the same height (~70 kg IBW). Setting VT at 6–8 mL/kg actual weight for this patient would deliver 900–1200 mL — massively overdistending the lungs and causing VILI. Using IBW: VT = 6–8 mL/kg × 70 kg = 420–560 mL. This principle applies to all mechanically ventilated patients, but is especially critical in obesity where the discrepancy between actual and ideal weight is large. Always calculate IBW from height before initiating mechanical ventilation.

stem: What are the signs and symptoms of respiratory distress versus respiratory failure?
options: Respiratory distress: tachypnea, dyspnea, accessory muscle use, nasal flaring, retractions, diaphoresis — compensatory mechanisms are still functional. Respiratory failure: inability to maintain adequate gas exchange (PaO2 <60 or PaCO2 >50 with acidosis) — compensatory mechanisms have failed
Respiratory distress and failure are identical
Respiratory failure always occurs without any preceding distress
Respiratory distress is more dangerous than respiratory failure
rationale: Respiratory distress (the body is compensating): Signs: tachypnea (increased RR), dyspnea (subjective breathlessness), accessory muscle use (SCM, scalenes, intercostal retractions), nasal flaring, paradoxical breathing, diaphoresis, tachycardia, anxiety/agitation, tripod positioning, pursed-lip breathing. ABG may still be adequate or show compensatory changes (hyperventilation with respiratory alkalosis). Respiratory failure (compensation has failed): Defined by ABG criteria: Type I: PaO2 <60 mmHg. Type II: PaCO2 >50 mmHg with acidosis (pH <7.35). Late signs: bradycardia, bradypnea/apnea, altered consciousness, cyanosis, lethargy, cardiac arrest. Important: respiratory distress precedes failure. Intervene during distress to prevent failure. A fatiguing patient may appear calmer (decreased respiratory effort) — this is a dangerous sign of impending respiratory arrest, not improvement.

stem: What is the ventilator bundle for preventing ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP)?
options: Head of bed elevation 30-45°, daily sedation interruption and assessment for extubation readiness, peptic ulcer prophylaxis, DVT prophylaxis, oral care with chlorhexidine, and subglottic secretion drainage
Prophylactic antibiotics for all ventilated patients
No interventions can prevent VAP
Only hand hygiene is needed to prevent VAP
